subtitle: 浅草・渋谷・水道橋・新宿 · ベジ・グルテンフリー
note: Restaurant guide for four 1-mile circles — Asakusa (Sensōji), Shibuya, Suidōbashi/Jimbocho and Shinjuku — with gluten-free/vegan/mom-&-pop/ramen finds, plus the city-wide konbini (🏪), survival guides, label reader, 100 Dishes and Learn.

places.name: Bistro Beppinsha (カリー＆ワイン ビストロべっぴん舎 お茶の水本店)
places.gf_detail: Beppinsha's signature medicinal (薬膳) spice curries are made without wheat flour by design — the shop states plainly 「小麦粉を使わないグルテンフリーカリー」 ("The spice curry without wheat flour") and describes 「小麦粉を使わないさらさらの薬膳カレー」, a thin, roux-free broth built from 20+ spices rather than a flour thickener. This makes the core curries structurally wheat-free, but it is a shared bistro kitchen (14 seats, curry + wine) with no celiac allergy protocol, so cross-contact and wheat-containing seasonings (soy sauce, Worcestershire-type sauces) in individual components are possible. Confirm no wheat soy/roux is used and that no bread or croutons are plated; treat spice level and toppings case by case.
places.vegan_detail: The core curries contain animal protein (chicken drumstick, beef tendon, shrimp) and the lunch set includes potato salad (typically mayonnaise-based). There is no advertised vegan curry; a mostly-vegetable version might be negotiable but is not documented. Vegan diners should ask directly.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun & holidays 11:00–15:30 (lunch only at present); closed Mon & Wed. (Formerly also dinner 18:00–20:00.)
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神田小川町 (Kanda-Ogawamachi / Ochanomizu–Jimbocho), Tokyo
places.cuisine: Medicinal (薬膳) Japanese-style spice curry & wine bistro
places.notes: [GF] Beppinsha actively markets its spice curries as wheat-free (グルテンフリーカリー) and the thin, roux-free 薬膳 broth is thickened by spices, not flour — a genuinely low-risk base. But it is a shared curry-and-wine bistro with no formal celiac handling, so ask the staff to confirm no wheat-based soy/Worcestershire sauce touches your plate, order the curry over plain rice, and skip any bread. Cash only; lunch-only at present with irregular closing days (Mon & Wed).

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan
chef_bio.background: Beppinsha opened in 2016 in the Kanda-Jimbocho/Ochanomizu district, originally conceived as a curry-and-wine bistro serving curry only at lunch. The owner-manager brought experience from a long-established curry shop and a wine bistro; the curry lineage traces back through Kashiwa's 「ボンベイ」 (Bombay) to Ueno's 「デリー」 (Delhi), often cited as Japan's first authentic Indian restaurant. After winning the 神田カレーグランプリ (Kanda Curry Grand Prix) two years running, the shop pivoted to focus on curry. It was selected as a Michelin Guide Tokyo 2021 Bib Gourmand and repeatedly listed in Tabelog's Curry TOKYO 100 (2019, 2020, 2023, 2024, 2026).
chef_bio.philosophy: Curry as wellness — an oil-light, wheat-flour-free 薬膳 (medicinal) broth built from 20+ spices that the manager calls 「元気になれるカレー」 ("curry that restores vitality"), easy on the stomach with a clean, refreshing finish.
chef_bio.specialty: 赤のべっぴん薬膳カリー — the "red" medicinal curry with eight wine-simmered vegetables and 20+ medicinal spices in a thin, roux-free broth.

anecdotes.text: The shop pivoted from a wine bistro to a curry specialist after winning the Kanda Curry Grand Prix two consecutive years.
Curry lineage traces to Kashiwa 'Bombay', itself descended from Ueno 'Delhi', regarded as Japan's first authentic Indian cuisine.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: べっぴん舎公式サイトは主力の薬膳カリーを「小麦粉を使わないグルテンフリーカリー」と明記。るるぶ&more.は2016年神田開業、当初はワインビストロで昼のみカレー提供、神田カレーグランプリ受賞後にカレー専門へ転換、店長は「元気になれるカレー」と説明と記載。食べ歩きブログはミシュラン2021ビブグルマン、カレーの系譜が柏『ボンベイ』→上野『デリー』に遡ると紹介。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shared 14-seat curry-and-wine bistro kitchen with no advertised celiac/allergy protocol; wheat items (wine-bistro sides, bread) may be handled nearby.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Not documented whether any component uses wheat-based soy or Worcestershire sauce; the curry broth itself is stated to be flour-free but seasoning details are unverified — confirm with staff.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Curries contain meat/seafood and the set includes mayonnaise-based potato salad; no vegan preparation documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No celiac allergy-handling procedure is published; GF is a product characteristic (wheat-free curry) rather than a managed protocol.

positives.text: Shop explicitly states 「小麦粉を使わないグルテンフリーカリー」 — the signature curries are made without wheat flour and use spices, not roux, to thicken.
「小麦粉を使わないさらさらの薬膳カレー」 — a thin, roux-free medicinal broth, reducing the classic wheat-flour risk of Japanese curry.

places.name: BIOSSA (ビオッサ)
places.gf_detail: BIOSSA is a 100% rice-flour, completely wheat-free dedicated bakery — the gold standard for a celiac. Their own site states 「『完全』グルテンフリーのベーカリー…グルテンを含む原材料を生産現場に持ち込まない」 (a 'completely' gluten-free bakery that does NOT bring gluten-containing ingredients into the production site) and 「小麦を持ち込まない完全グルテンフリー製造工場」 (a completely gluten-free manufacturing facility with no wheat brought in), explicitly naming 小麦アレルギー・セリアック病 (wheat allergy / celiac disease) as who it is safe for. No wheat is handled on premises, so cross-contact risk is essentially eliminated at source. Only caveat: it is ~1.2km NW of Suidobashi, just outside the strict 1km circle (flagged for Greg).
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan bakery, but many items are plant-based: they build on 国産米粉100% with 米油 (rice oil) and てんさい糖 (beet sugar) rather than butter. Baguettes, bean/red-bean breads and the soy (SOI) bread are typically dairy- and egg-free, while items like the 塩バターロール (salt butter roll), 豆乳クリームパン (soy-milk cream bun) and some sweet breads may contain butter, egg or dairy. Confirm the specific loaf's ingredients in-store, as vegan status varies by product.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 daily; closed Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: 文京区春日 (Kasuga, Bunkyo-ku) — ~1.2km NW of Suidobashi, ~9 min walk from Myogadani Station
places.notes: [GF-dedicated] A completely wheat-free, 100% rice-flour bakery — the strongest celiac option in the wider Suidobashi/Bunkyo area and safe for a 13-year-old celiac to eat freely, since no wheat is handled on-site. It is a takeout bakery (no seating/reservations), so grab loaves, rolls and buns to go. Note it sits ~1.2km NW of Suidobashi, just OUTSIDE the strict 1km circle — flagged for Greg. Closed Tuesdays; cards and QR payment (PayPay, d払い, Rakuten Pay, au PAY) accepted.

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan
chef_bio.background: 宮城伸夫 (Miyagi Nobuo) founded 株式会社biossa in August 2019 and opened the Kasuga bakery on 18 December 2019 in Bunkyo-ku. He himself has a wheat allergy (代表の宮城氏自身も小麦アレルギーを持つ), which became the personal driving force behind the business. The company built a completely gluten-free manufacturing facility that brings no wheat in — 「小麦を持ち込まない完全グルテンフリー製造工場」 — using reliable technique and quality ingredients to make health-conscious, original breads. In 2023 they added a new factory in Kawaguchi, Saitama (operations from December 2023, full production from January 2024) to mass-produce five staple lines: BIO食パン, BIOバゲット, BIOプチブール and BIOバンズ among them.
chef_bio.philosophy: 「パンとしておいしくなくては、食べ続けることはできません。開発にあたっては、味や食感にこだわった製品を目指しました」 — bread has to taste good or you can't keep eating it. Rather than the typical dense 'mochi-mochi' rice-bread texture, BIOSSA deliberately engineers a light, fluffy crumb like wheat bread by finely tuning the amylose content of the rice flour and the ratio of thickener to water.
chef_bio.specialty: Completely gluten-free 100% 国産米粉 (domestic rice flour) breads made with rice oil and beet sugar, including a signature rice-flour bread incorporating tiger nuts (a superfood).

anecdotes.text: Miyagi's own wheat allergy is cited as the reason the company exists — 代表の宮城氏の個人的な経験がビジネスの原動力となっています.
He aims to send Japanese rice to the world in the form of bread: 「日本の米をパンという形で世界へ発信すれば、多くの人に受け入れてもらえると期待しています」.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Company site (biossajapan.com) and the komeko-koubo project profile confirm a founder (宮城伸夫) with his own wheat allergy, a 2019 founding, and an explicitly wheat-free dedicated production facility ('小麦を持ち込まない完全グルテンフリー製造工場'). The PRTimes press release confirms the August 2019 incorporation and December 2019 Kasuga opening; komeko-koubo adds the 2023 Kawaguchi factory expansion and his philosophy quotes on taste, texture and global ambition.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Completely gluten-free bakery that does not bring gluten-containing ingredients into the production site: 「『完全』グルテンフリーのベーカリー…グルテンを含む原材料を生産現場に持ち込まない」. No wheat handled on premises, so cross-contact is eliminated at source.
「小麦を持ち込まない完全グルテンフリー製造工場」 — a completely gluten-free manufacturing facility with no wheat brought in.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bakery (breads only) — no soy-sauce-based dishes; standard products use 米粉 (rice flour), 米油 (rice oil) and てんさい糖 (beet sugar).

vegan_cross_contact.text: Butter, egg and dairy are used in some items (e.g. 塩バターロール, 豆乳クリームパン, sweet breads), so vegan customers should confirm the specific product; the facility is not vegan-only.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Founder Miyagi Nobuo has a wheat allergy himself and built the business around allergen safety; site names 小麦アレルギー・セリアック病・グルテン不耐性 as who the bakery is safe for.

positives.text: Explicitly states products are safe for people with wheat allergy, celiac disease and gluten intolerance: 「小麦アレルギー、セリアック病やグルテン不耐性の方でも安心してお召し上がりいただけます」.
100% domestic rice flour, zero wheat flour, in a dedicated wheat-free facility — the gold-standard setup for a celiac.

places.name: Cafe de Forte (カフェ・ド・フォルテ)
places.gf_detail: A rice-flour (米粉) focused home-style cafe that markets itself as celiac-aware — its own materials say it caters to 「グルテン不耐症やセリアック病を含む様々なお客様」(guests with gluten intolerance and celiac disease). BUT the venue explicitly disclaims full safety: its own site states 「全てのメニューがグルテンフリーではありません」(not all menu items are gluten-free) and 「アレルギー完全対応ではありません」(we are not fully allergy-compliant), asking allergy guests to notify staff in advance. The JP celiac guide glutenfree.empacede.co.jp corroborates that wheat is present and warns 「メニューに小麦成分が含まれているかどうかは、お店で確認してください」(check with the shop whether a menu item contains wheat). Wheat dishes (hamburg with breadcrumbs, hayashi/beef-stew roux, 醤油麹 soy-koji bowls) are served on-site in a shared kitchen, so despite the 米粉 core this is downgraded from 'high' to options: order only the clearly rice-flour items and confirm each one.
places.vegan_detail: The menu leans on dairy and meat — Iwate Iwaizumi yogurt on the acai bowl, butter and vanilla ice cream on the pancakes, plus roast beef, hamburg, beef stew and chicken dishes. A vegetable salad and the rice-flour breads/noodles may be adaptable, but no explicitly vegan dishes were confirmed, so vegans should ask and expect few options.
places.hours_raw: Tue/Wed/Fri 9:30–15:00; Sat (biweekly) ~12:00–17:00; closed Sun/Mon/Thu. Hours are irregular — seasonal pancakes are reservation-only.
places.neighborhood: 文京区春日 (Kasuga, Bunkyo-ku) — 小石川Jフラッツ 1F, ~0.9km WNW of Suidobashi; ~11 min walk from Myogadani / ~13 min from Korakuen
places.cuisine: Rice-flour (米粉) cafe — Japanese home-style light meals, pancakes & sweets
places.notes: [GF] — A 米粉 (rice-flour) cafe run by an Iwate-born owner and her daughter; it caters to celiac/gluten-intolerant guests but openly states 「全てのメニューがグルテンフリーではありません」and 「アレルギー完全対応ではありません」, so wheat is present in a shared kitchen. For a celiac 13-year-old: stick to the clearly rice-flour items (米粉パン, 米粉パンケーキ/ワッフル, 盛岡米粉冷めん, acai bowl with GF granola), avoid the hamburg/loco-moco/hayashi/beef-stew and 醤油麹 soy-koji dishes, and confirm soy sauce is tamari on any savory order. Tell staff in advance (they ask allergy guests to notify them). Seasonal pancakes are reservation-only.

chef_bio.origin: Iwate Prefecture, Japan
chef_bio.background: Cafe de Forte is a small home-style cafe run by an Iwate-born (岩手県出身) owner together with her daughter, which opened in Kasuga, Bunkyo-ku in spring 2024 (around March; early blog posts date to April–May 2024). The owner describes the shop's food as growing out of dishes she used to make for her own children, centered on domestic rice flour (国産米粉) and body-friendly, low-sugar, additive-free cooking. She leans on ingredients from her home region of Iwate — Iwaizumi yogurt (岩泉ヨーグルト), a Morioka-style rice-flour cold noodle (盛岡米粉冷めん), and additive-free (無添加) seasonings. The cafe explicitly welcomes guests with gluten intolerance and celiac disease, while stating it is not a fully allergy-compliant kitchen. It is a hidden-gem (隠れ家) style cafe emphasizing a warm, 'friend's house' atmosphere.
chef_bio.philosophy: 体に優しい — health-first, low-sugar, additive-free home cooking built around domestic rice flour, welcoming gluten-intolerant and celiac guests while being honest that not every dish is gluten-free.
chef_bio.specialty: 米粉 (rice-flour) pancakes and rice-flour bread; seasonal reservation-only pancake specials

anecdotes.text: Run by the owner and her daughter as an at-home cafe; some menu items are dishes the owner originally made for her own children.
The owner is from Iwate and features Iwate-region products such as Iwaizumi yogurt and a Morioka rice-flour cold noodle.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 店の公式サイト・コラム（cafe-de-f.jp）とグルテンフリーガイド（empacede）によると、岩手県出身のオーナーと娘さんが2024年春に文京区春日で開業。国産米粉・岩手県産品（岩泉ヨーグルト、盛岡米粉冷めん）・無添加・糖質オフにこだわる家庭的なカフェで、グルテン不耐症やセリアック病の客にも対応するが「全てのメニューがグルテンフリーではありません」「アレルギー完全対応ではありません」と明記。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Venue's own site: 「全てのメニューがグルテンフリーではありません」(not all menu items are gluten-free) — wheat dishes share the kitchen, so cross-contact is a real risk for celiacs.
JP celiac guide: 「メニューに小麦成分が含まれているかどうかは、お店で確認してください」(check with the shop whether an item contains wheat).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Savory items such as the 醤油麹 (soy-sauce koji) yakitori bowl use soy sauce, which contains wheat unless tamari; confirm before ordering.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen uses dairy (yogurt, butter, ice cream), eggs and meat throughout; no dedicated vegan prep noted.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The cafe caters to gluten-intolerant/celiac guests and asks allergy guests to notify staff in advance 「アレルギーをお持ちの方は事前にスタッフにお知らせください」, but states 「アレルギー完全対応ではありません」(not fully allergy-compliant).

positives.text: Core menu is built on domestic rice flour (国産米粉100%) — GF pancakes, waffles, bread, roll cake and Morioka rice-flour cold noodle.
Acai bowl uses additive-free, gluten-free rice-flour granola (米粉の無添加・グルテンフリーのグラノーラ).
Owner-cook explicitly welcomes celiac and gluten-intolerant guests and cooks health-first, additive-free food.

places.name: Be green by KIELO COFFEE (ビー グリーン バイ キエロ コーヒー)
places.gf_detail: Every sweet and baked good on the menu is gluten-free and made without wheat: the shop describes its offering as 「ヴィーガン＆グルテンフリーの焼き菓子」 (vegan & gluten-free baked goods, ~6 seasonal varieties) and reviewers report 「ドリンクやデザートはすべて植物性の材料で作られており ヴィーガンかつグルテンフリー」 (all drinks and desserts are plant-based, vegan and gluten-free). Cakes such as the signature carrot cake are 米粉 (rice-flour) based with cashew cream. No wheat bread or wheat pastry was found in any of 8+ Japanese sources, so cross-contact risk within the shop is low; however there is no published celiac/allergen protocol and it is a small shared café/roastery counter, so confirm on the day and this stays "high" rather than certified-dedicated.
places.vegan_detail: A fully plant-based café: all milk is oat, almond, soy or pistachio (no dairy upcharge) and every dessert and baked good is vegan. Vegewel lists it as vegan-certified. Concept is 「All for green すべては自然のために」 (everything for nature).
places.hours_raw: 9:00-18:00, irregular closing days (roasting on Tuesdays); one listing shows 11:00-18:00 closed Mon — confirm on the day
places.neighborhood: 文京区本郷 (Hongo, Bunkyo-ku) — ~3 min from Hongo-sanchome Stn, ~0.9km NE of Suidobashi
places.cuisine: Vegan & gluten-free café, specialty coffee roastery and gluten-free bakery
places.notes: [GF] — The entire sweets/bakery menu is gluten-free and vegan (rice-flour cakes, cashew cream), and no wheat products were found on the menu, so this is a rare fully-GF stop for a celiac teen. Coffee is naturally GF; all milks are plant-based. There is no formal celiac cross-contamination protocol published, so it is worth a quick spoken check (「小麦は使っていますか？」) but the risk profile is much better than a mixed bakery. Small counter (~5 seats + 2 outside); takeout available.

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan
chef_bio.background: KIELO COFFEE was founded by roaster 吉池浩紀 (Hiroki Yoshiike), who became interested in coffee through a student part-time job and fell for Nordic-style light-roast coffee on a trip to Northern Europe (「kielo」 references a Finnish/Nordic sensibility). The original KIELO COFFEE stands near Akihabara; 「Be green by KIELO COFFEE」 in Hongo is the brand's second location and roastery, opened as a sustainability-focused café. The Hongo shop is run day-to-day by a store manager (reported as Shimizu / 清水) who emphasises face-to-face conversation with customers over one-way messaging. Roasting is done on-site (notably Tuesdays) using specialty beans said to be in the top ~5% of the market, prepared as light roasts to highlight origin character.
chef_bio.philosophy: 「All for green すべては自然のために」 — minimise environmental load and body burden: plant-based only, gluten-free baking, traceable specialty coffee, renewable-energy extraction, and food-waste reduction (the carrot cake reuses pulp from a neighbouring juice/smoothie shop).
chef_bio.specialty: Light-roast single-origin specialty coffee plus vegan & gluten-free rice-flour baked goods (signature carrot cake with cashew cream and cinnamon).

anecdotes.text: The signature carrot cake is made with rice flour and a cashew-based butter-like cream with hints of cinnamon, and reuses spent pulp from a nearby juice shop as part of a food-waste reduction collaboration.
Coffee is extracted using renewable (solar/wind) energy and beans are traceable, part of the shop's aim for a sustainable coffee chain where everyone involved benefits.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 日本語の情報源（hachidory、vegewel、るるぶ&more.、note、japanese-heart、CafeSnap）は一致して、当店のドリンク・スイーツはすべて植物性でヴィーガンかつグルテンフリーであり、キャロットケーキは米粉ベースであると伝えている。焙煎は北欧志向のライトローストで、オーナー焙煎士は吉池浩紀。小麦パンを焼いているという記述はどの情報源にもなかった。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The full sweets/bakery menu is gluten-free with no wheat baked goods found on the menu, so there is no wheat flour handled at the pastry counter; still a small shared café/roastery with no published allergen protocol, so cross-contact cannot be certified.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce / savoury cooking involved — menu is coffee, plant-milk drinks and gluten-free sweets only, so wheat-in-soy-sauce is not a concern here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fully plant-based operation: all milks are oat/almond/soy/pistachio and all desserts are vegan, so no dairy or egg is used on premises.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Store philosophy emphasises direct conversation with customers; no formal written allergy protocol was published, so verify wheat/cross-contact verbally on arrival.

positives.text: 「ドリンクやデザートはすべて植物性の材料で作られており ヴィーガンかつグルテンフリー」 — all drinks and desserts are plant-based, vegan and gluten-free.
「スイーツメニューもすべてヴィーガンかつグルテンフリー」 — the entire sweets menu is vegan and gluten-free; signature carrot cake is rice-flour (米粉) based.
Vegewel lists the shop as vegan-certified with a gluten-free menu.

places.name: Sumatra Curry Kyoeido (スマトラカレー 共栄堂)
places.gf_detail: The signature dark Sumatra curry sauce is genuinely wheat-free by design — the shop states 「濃褐色のソースは小麦粉を一切使わず、20数種類の香辛料を1時間かけて炒めて作ります」 (the dark-brown sauce uses no wheat flour at all; 20-odd spices are toasted for an hour), which removes the roux/flour that thickens ordinary Japanese curry. That makes the curry-over-rice naturally low-gluten, BUT this is a 1924 old-school single-item curry counter with NO allergy protocol and a shared kitchen, so it caps at 'options,' not 'high.' Skip the free potage soup (may be flour-thickened) and the fukujinzuke pickle topping (usually wheat-soy-based), and there is no dedicated-fryer or cross-contact control — appropriate only for a family comfortable managing residual-trace risk.
places.vegan_detail: Every curry is built on separately simmered meat, seafood or beef tongue (pork, chicken, beef, shrimp, tongue) plus a meat-and-vegetable base; there is no vegetable-only curry on the menu. Not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–20:00 (L.O. 19:45); closed Sundays and some public holidays (irregular)
places.neighborhood: 神田神保町 (Jimbocho), Chiyoda — ~0.8 km SW of Suidobashi, 2 min from Jimbocho Stn A5 exit
places.notes: [GF] A rare Japanese-curry option for celiacs because the shop deliberately builds its Sumatra sauce 「小麦粉を一切使わず」 (with no wheat flour), unlike standard flour-roux curry. Order any curry over plain Koshihikari rice, but tell them no fukujinzuke pickle and skip the complimentary potage soup, and understand there is no allergen handling in this century-old counter kitchen. Cash only, no reservations, one curry per person required.

chef_bio.origin: Jimbocho, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Kyoeido opened in Taishō 13 (1924) in Kanda-Jimbocho and has served the same Sumatra-style curry for a full century. The recipe traces to Itō Yūjirō, a friend of the founder who traveled widely through Southeast Asia in the late Meiji/Taishō era and taught the shop how to cook a Sumatra-island curry, which was then adapted to the Japanese palate. The shop is now run by later generations of the family and remains a single-purpose curry counter in the basement of the Jimbocho Sun Building. It is a fixture of the Kanda curry scene and a recurring Michelin Bib/guide mention.
chef_bio.philosophy: Depth through spice and time rather than flour: roast 20-odd spices for an hour, simmer meat and vegetables until the vegetables dissolve completely, and let the curry stay 'sarari' (light-bodied) yet concentrated — no thickening roux.
chef_bio.specialty: Jet-black, bitter-edged Sumatra spice curry, each protein simmered in its own pot so pork, chicken, beef, shrimp and tongue each taste distinct.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen simmers each protein separately so every curry variant has its own flavor, and finishes vegetables until they are formless in the sauce.
A winter-only baked apple (焼りんご) is made in tiny daily quantities from roughly October to early May.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site and Kanda Curry Grand Prix page both state the 1924 (大正13年) founding, the Itō Yūjirō / Sumatra origin, and the flour-free sauce claim 「小麦粉を一切使わず、20数種類の香辛料を1時間かけて炒めて」. Tabelog and gurunavi confirm current operation, hours, cash-only, and the one-plate-per-person rule.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Century-old single-item curry counter with no stated allergen protocol; sauce is flour-free by recipe but the kitchen is shared and residual cross-contact is not controlled.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The fukujinzuke pickle served with curry is typically seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce; request the curry without it. The spice sauce itself is described as flour-free but the shop makes no tamari claim, so confirm no wheat soy in the masala if pressed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not applicable — all curries are meat/seafood based; no vegan menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling or gluten-free program is advertised; this is a traditional counter shop, so a celiac must rely on the naturally flour-free recipe plus their own topping/soup avoidance.

positives.text: Shop explicitly cooks its dark curry sauce with no wheat flour — 「小麦粉を一切使わず、20数種類の香辛料を1時間かけて炒めて作ります」 — the key reason a flour-roux dish like Japanese curry becomes possible for a celiac here.
Curry is served over plain premium Koshihikari rice, which is naturally gluten-free.

places.name: LA VORO Iidabashi (LA VORO 飯田橋店 / ラ ボーロ)
places.gf_detail: Rice-flour baumkuchen specialist whose entire product line is 100% 生米粉 (raw rice flour from their own 常陸小田米) with no wheat used at all. The shop states 「自家製の生米粉を使用したこだわりの品はグルテンフリーのため安心してお召し上がりいただけます」 and press describes it as 「米粉100％のグルテンフリーバウムクーヘン」. Because there is no verified explicit dedicated/no-wheat-facility statement (some sources phrase only the products, not the workshop, as gluten-free), rated high rather than dedicated; for a celiac, the all-rice-flour lineup is reassuring but confirm the workshop is wheat-free before eating.
places.vegan_detail: Every product carries the allergen listing 卵・米粉・乳 (egg, rice flour, milk) — the baumkuchen is built on eggs, cream and butter (モンコーネ also uses 発酵バター and アーモンドパウダー). Nothing on the menu is vegan.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 10:00-19:00; closed Sat & Sun
places.neighborhood: 千代田区飯田橋 (Iidabashi, Chiyoda-ku) — ~0.4km W of Suidobashi Stn / 5 min from JR Iidabashi
places.cuisine: Gluten-free rice-flour baumkuchen & yogashi (Western confectionery, takeout)
places.notes: [GF] Takeout-only baumkuchen/confectionery shop — not a sit-down meal, but a genuinely safe sweet stop for a celiac 13-year-old. The whole line is 100% rice flour (no wheat products made here), and the signature エテルノ and モンコーネ are marketed as gluten-free 米粉100%. All items contain egg and dairy. Closed weekends, so plan a weekday visit; cut-baum singles (~¥540) are in-store only.

chef_bio.origin: Tsukuba, Ibaraki, Japan
chef_bio.background: LA VORO is run by 小久保凌, who also operates 筑波農場 (Tsukuba Farm) in Tsukuba City, Ibaraki, growing and selling rice under the proprietary brand 常陸小田米 (Hitachi Oda-mai). His company is オーパス (Opus), Tsukuba. He founded LA VORO to achieve 「生産から加工、販売まで一貫して取り組む『6次産業化』」 — value-added, farm-to-shop processing of his own rice. The farm mills its own fresh 生米粉 (raw rice flour) from unprocessed grain, a moisture-rich flour said to be possible only for a working farm. The Iidabashi shop opened 3 April 2024 as the second location after the original Tsukuba store, and its first in Tokyo.
chef_bio.philosophy: Six-industry (6次産業化) farm-to-confectionery integration — turning his own freshly milled rice into gluten-free baumkuchen so people with wheat allergy can eat with peace of mind; his site calls the pieces 『作品』 (works of art) made with こだわり.
chef_bio.specialty: 100% 生米粉 (raw rice flour) gluten-free baumkuchen using own-brand 常陸小田米

anecdotes.text: Uses freshly milled RAW rice flour (生米粉) rather than dried commercial rice flour, which retains more moisture and gives a distinctively moist baumkuchen — a method he says is only possible on a working farm.
Okubo says he wanted to realize full '6th-industrialization' — handling production, processing and sales in one integrated chain using his own rice brand.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 市ケ谷経済新聞 (ichigaya.keizai.biz) profiles owner 小久保凌 / company オーパス / 筑波農場 and the 常陸小田米 6次産業化 story; the shop's own BASE site (lavoro.base.shop/about) confirms the self-milled 生米粉 and the gluten-free 「安心して」 claim; entertainment/TV recap blogs confirm the April 2024 opening and product line.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Entire product line is 100% rice flour with no wheat products made at the shop, greatly reducing cross-contact risk; however a fully dedicated wheat-free-facility statement was not verified, so confirm the workshop uses no wheat.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Not applicable — sweets-only shop; no soy sauce or savory items.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All items contain egg and milk (allergen listing 卵・米粉・乳); no vegan production.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Shop markets to wheat-allergic customers: 「小麦アレルギーの方でも安心して召し上がれます」; each product's allergen (卵・米粉・乳, モンコーネ also アーモンド) is listed on the online store.

positives.text: 100% 米粉 gluten-free baumkuchen specialist using own-farm raw rice flour; venue states 「グルテンフリーのため安心してお召し上がりいただけます」.
Press confirms 「米粉100％のグルテンフリーバウムクーヘン」 with per-item allergen labeling.

places.name: PAMOJA (コーヒーとおにぎり PAMOJA 神保町本店)
places.gf_detail: PAMOJA is a rice-based onigiri & coffee café, so the plain rice base is naturally gluten-free — but the shop makes NO gluten-free claim of its own. The only GF signal is an aggregator tag on Vegewel (「グルテンフリーメニューあり」), which cannot establish celiac safety. Many fillings are wheat risks: soy-sauce-simmered tsukudani/nimono (生姜昆布, まぐろ昆布, アサリの佃煮, いわしの生姜煮), teriyaki tofu, miso-based fillings (barley), and okaka (soy sauce). There is no allergen protocol and the kitchen shares soy sauce; a celiac must confirm each filling is salt-only or tamari-based (not wheat shoyu). Best treated as ask-and-choose-carefully, sticking to plain-salt fish or simple rice fillings.
places.vegan_detail: Despite the seed describing it as all-vegan, PAMOJA is a MIXED shop — its concept (PAMOJA = Swahili for "together") is vegans and non-vegans eating at the same table, so the menu includes fish and meat onigiri (塩サバ, 旨味サケ, 生たらこ, いわしの生姜煮, 肉味噌) alongside a strong vegan lineup. Clearly vegan items include 豆＆TOFUチーズ and 黒ゴマきなこ onigiri, the 野菜DELI/ヴィーガンプレート, 厚揚げのTERIYAKI, and vegan sweets (ヴィーガンスノーボール). Organic ingredients are used throughout; the Balance Plate lets you pick a vegan main. Vegans have plenty of choice but must select deliberately.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Thu 11:00–16:00; Fri 11:00–16:00 & 18:30–21:30; Sat 11:00–16:00; Sun 11:00–17:00; Closed Mon. Hours irregular — morning/evening service announced on Instagram.
places.neighborhood: 神田猿楽町 (Kanda-Sarugakucho), Jimbocho, Chiyoda — ~4 min from Jimbocho Stn, ~7 min from Ochanomizu
places.cuisine: Onigiri (rice balls), vegetable deli plates, specialty coffee & sweets
places.notes: [GF] — Naturally rice-based but NOT a gluten-free shop: no GF protocol, shared soy sauce, and many fillings are soy-simmered or miso-based (wheat risk). A celiac should ask filling-by-filling and stick to plain-salt fish (塩サバ, 旨味サケ) or simple items, confirming tamari vs wheat shoyu. Vegans have good choice but this is a mixed (meat/fish/vegan) shop — do not assume any item is vegan without asking.

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan
chef_bio.background: PAMOJA was founded by Toshiyuki Etoh (江藤智幸), representative of 株式会社PAMOJA, which was established in April 2023. The Jimbocho flagship (本店) sits in Kanda-Sarugakucho, a quiet block just off the bookshop district, and centers on freshly hand-pressed onigiri (握りたて) made with organic ingredients. The shop name PAMOJA is Swahili for "together": Etoh's stated concept is a table where vegans and non-vegans can enjoy food side by side, which is why the lineup deliberately spans vegan, fish, and meat fillings. The café pairs its rice with specialty coffee and rotating seasonal sweets, and runs irregular morning/evening pop-up service announced via Instagram.
chef_bio.philosophy: 「みんなで食を楽しむ」 — everyone enjoying food together, regardless of diet; organic, seasonal ingredients and an inclusive table where vegan and non-vegan guests share the same menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly pressed organic onigiri (10+ seasonal fillings, both vegan and non-vegan) with specialty coffee.

anecdotes.text: The name PAMOJA is Swahili for "together," chosen so that vegans and non-vegans can share the same table.
The 厚揚げのTERIYAKI (teriyaki thick-fried tofu) vegan onigiri is offered only at the Jimbocho location.
The vegan snowball sweets (ヴィーガンスノーボール) frequently sell out.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: PR Times press release from 株式会社PAMOJA describes founder 江藤智幸, an April 2023 founding, the Swahili 'together' concept, and the vegan lineup; the official foodre listing and Vegewel confirm the address, organic sourcing, mixed vegan/fish/meat onigiri, and plate pricing; Tabelog and Yahoo Map corroborate location, phone, and hours.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free protocol; the only GF mention is an aggregator tag on Vegewel (「グルテンフリーメニューあり」), not a venue claim. Rice base is naturally GF but fillings and shared soy sauce are the risk.
Multiple fillings are soy-sauce-simmered tsukudani/nimono (生姜昆布, まぐろ昆布, アサリの佃煮, いわしの生姜煮) — wheat risk unless tamari is used.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy sauce (wheat) appears across simmered fillings, okaka, and teriyaki tofu; no statement that tamari is used. Must confirm per item.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Mixed kitchen handling fish and meat onigiri (塩サバ, 旨味サケ, 生たらこ, 肉味噌) alongside vegan items — shared rice-pressing station is a cross-contact consideration for strict vegans.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergy-handling procedure; hours and service are irregular and directed to Instagram. Treat as ask-and-verify in person.

positives.text: Rice-based menu with organic ingredients and a documented vegan lineup, giving a naturally low-gluten baseline if fillings are chosen carefully.
Plain-salt fish onigiri (塩サバ, 旨味サケ) are the most likely wheat-free choices for a celiac to confirm.

places.name: Sagatani Jūwari Soba, Jimbocho (十割蕎麦 嵯峨谷 神保町店)
places.gf_detail: The noodle itself is a genuine plus: this is a 100% buckwheat (十割) shop that stone-mills and makes its own noodles in-house — the operator states 「石臼での店内製粉」 and 「挽きたて・打ちたて・茹でたて・揚げたて」, and because the whole shop is jūwari soba, the boiling water is not shared with wheat udon. HOWEVER the standard tsuyu (soup/dipping broth, described 「甘辛濃いめ」) is made with ordinary wheat-containing soy sauce, and tempura/kakiage is deep-fried on-site — so shared frying oil and airborne wheat flour are present. This is a fast, cheap standing-soba shop (立ち食い) with no allergy protocol; a celiac can only eat here by negotiating no-tsuyu plain noodles and bringing their own tamari, and accepting airborne cross-contact risk. Rated ASK, not high.
places.vegan_detail: The buckwheat noodles themselves are plant-based (soba flour, water), but the standard tsuyu is a katsuo (bonito) dashi broth, so ordered as served the soba is not vegan. No dedicated vegan option is advertised; a plain no-tsuyu bowl would be needed and there is no allergy/vegan handling.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 10:30–21:40; Sat/Sun/Holidays 11:00–19:40. Irregular closures during Golden Week & Obon; closed Dec 31–Jan 3.
places.neighborhood: 神田神保町 (Kanda-Jimbocho), Chiyoda, Tokyo
places.cuisine: Standing jūwari (100% buckwheat) soba — low-price rō-men shop
places.notes: [GF] Genuine 100% buckwheat noodle, stone-milled and made in-house, in an all-soba shop (no shared wheat-udon water) — but the tsuyu uses wheat soy sauce and tempura is fried on the premises. For a celiac this is negotiate-only: ask for plain noodles with no tsuyu (bring tamari), and understand airborne flour + shared fryer are unavoidable in a fast standing shop with no allergy training. A cautious lunch, not a guaranteed-safe meal.

roles: Chain / operator: 株式会社フォーユー (For You Co., Ltd.)
chef_bio.origin: Tokyo (chain)
chef_bio.background: 嵯峨谷 (Sagatani) is a low-price standing-soba (立ち食いそば) chain operated by 株式会社フォーユー (For You Co., Ltd.), which grew into central Tokyo — Shibuya, Shinjuku and the Jimbocho branch here — and is frequently cited (e.g. Nikkei xtrend) as part of a 'new force' shaking up the standing-soba world. Its distinguishing move is doing on-site stone-milling and noodle-making in a cheap standing shop, which is unusual for the format. The Jimbocho branch sits in the 三光堂ビル 1F at 神田神保町1-7, about a 2-minute walk from Jimbocho Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: 「毎日食べても飽きがこない体にも財布にも優しい食事」— food you can eat every day that is gentle on both body and wallet: cheap prices with real 100% buckwheat quality, achieved by 「挽きたて・打ちたて・茹でたて・揚げたて」 (freshly ground, rolled, boiled, fried).
chef_bio.specialty: 100% buckwheat (十割) mori/kake soba from a few hundred yen; signature 'aji-gohan' (あじ御飯) rice side and cheap don sets.

anecdotes.text: The shop grinds whole buckwheat groats (玄そば) on an in-house electric stone mill and aims to use each day's flour the same day; water ratio is adjusted daily for season, weather and flour condition.
Cold soba is recommended as 太麺 (thick) and hot soba as 細麺 (thin); the shop offers a monthly ~¥200 subscription giving two free large portions a day.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Operator page (foryou-group.co.jp) and Japanese food blogs confirm 石臼での店内製粉 (in-house stone milling), 十割そば (100% buckwheat), daily-adjusted water ratio, standing-shop format, hours and address. No source claims any gluten-free or allergy program; tsuyu and on-site tempura frying are described plainly.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura and kakiage are deep-fried on-site ('揚げたて'), so a shared fryer and airborne wheat flour (batter/dredge) are present in this small standing shop.
Mitigating factor: the entire shop is 100% buckwheat soba, so noodle boiling water is not shared with wheat udon.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsuyu/broth is a standard sweet-savory soy-based soba broth ('甘辛濃いめ'), i.e. ordinary wheat-containing soy sauce, not tamari — the noodle is wheat-free but the sauce as served is not.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard tsuyu is bonito (katsuo) dashi, so soba as served is not vegan even though the noodles are plant-based.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Fast, cheap standing-soba shop with ticket-machine ordering; no allergy protocol, allergen menu, or gluten-free claim appears in any source.

positives.text: Genuine 十割 (100% buckwheat) noodles, stone-milled and made in-house daily — the noodle itself contains no wheat.
All-soba shop means the boiling water is not shared with wheat udon, unlike typical mixed soba/udon stands.

places.name: 浅草むぎとろ 本店 (Asakusa Mugitoro (Honten))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated fryer was found in any available source. This is a traditional Japanese restaurant relying on conventional soy sauce and dashi seasonings and offering tempura-style dishes on some menus, so wheat exposure and cross-contamination should be assumed likely. Diners with celiac disease or severe wheat allergy should not treat this as a safe option without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant's signature seasoning is a secret dashi (broth) based on bonito flakes (katsuobushi) and kelp, used since the 1929 founding, so the core mugitoro dish and much of the kaiseki menu are not vegan. No vegan-specific menu, substitutions, or plant-based dashi option were found in available sources.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Kaminarimon, by the Sumida River, near Sensō-ji)
places.notes: Founded on June 10, 1929 (Shōwa 4) beside the Sumida River near the Kaminarimon/Komagata-bashi area of Asakusa. According to the company's own history, founder Tazō Nakajima had worked for a produce (vegetable/fruit) wholesaler and wanted to start a food business of his own; he i

chef_bio.background: Founded on June 10, 1929 (Shōwa 4) beside the Sumida River near the Kaminarimon/Komagata-bashi area of Asakusa. According to the company's own history, founder Tazō Nakajima had worked for a produce (vegetable/fruit) wholesaler and wanted to start a food business of his own; he is said to have traveled the country searching for the best mountain yam before settling on a strain and opening a shop built entirely around tororo (grated yam). The restaurant has been run by the same family and stayed on essentially the same stretch of riverbank for nearly a century, expanding over the decades into a multi-floor kaiseki restaurant while keeping the original tororo recipe at its core.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's founding wish was simply 'we want everyone to be able to eat one bowl of mugitoro' — a focus on making a single humble, health-associated dish available to ordinary diners, which still shapes the menu today.
chef_bio.specialty: Mugitoro (麦とろ) — barley-mixed rice topped with tororo (grated Japanese mountain yam, made from a strong, low-astringency yamato-imo), seasoned with a secret bonito-and-kelp dashi recipe passed down since the 1929 founding. The restaurant also serves broader tororo-kaiseki courses and a 'guro-don' (fatty tuna) rice bowl.

anecdotes.text: Founder Tazō Nakajima worked at a produce company and, wanting to go into a food business of his own, is said to have traveled the country before finding what he considered the finest mountain yam — the discovery that became the basis for the restaurant.
The shop hung its noren for the first time on June 10, 1929, at the foot of the Sumida River, with the founding wish that 'everyone could eat a single bowl of mugitoro.'

cultural_comfort.note: A long-running, high-traffic tourist destination steps from Sensō-ji with an English-language site/menu and English-language press coverage, so English-speaking visitors should find it easy to navigate even without Japanese.

places.name: 浅草 更科天狐（さらしな てんこ） (Asakusa Sarashina Tenko)
places.gf_detail: All three house soba noodles (juwari/100% buckwheat, sarashina, and kurobiki) are made with 100% soba flour and no wheat binder, per the restaurant's own account and independent food-media coverage. The restaurant also states it reformulated its dipping sauce into a proprietary 'gluten-free Edo tsuyu,' specifically to remove the wheat-derived gluten that ordinary soba tsuyu picks up from soy sauce. That said, no source confirms a dedicated gluten-free kitchen/fryer, cross-contact protocols for any fried (tempura-style) items, or celiac-level handling procedures — those claims could not be verified, so treat as strong ingredient-level gluten-free intent rather than a certified celiac-safe kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: No source confirms a vegan/plant-based menu or a kombu/vegetable-only dashi. The restaurant's showcased 'Edo tsuyu' is described as a rich, traditionally aged-style dashi-based sauce, which in classic Edo soba preparation is typically built on katsuobushi (bonito) dashi; none of the available sources state the dashi is fish-free, so it should not be assumed vegan without asking staff directly.
places.neighborhood: 台東区浅草（花屋敷通り、浅草寺南側、浅草駅より徒歩3分）
places.notes: Opened January 22, 2025 on Hanayashiki-dori in Asakusa, just south of Sensoji, a short walk from Asakusa Station. Billed as a 'shin-soba-ya' (next-generation soba restaurant) built around the idea that a meal can help the body 'tonou' (naturally come into balance) — combining tra

roles: Soba craftsmanship supervised by soba shokunin Hiroyasu Nagayama (永山寛康)
Concept produced by restaurant producer Mori (森) and molecular-nutrition counselor Ayako Iwamoto (岩本亜矢子/綾子)
chef_bio.background: Opened January 22, 2025 on Hanayashiki-dori in Asakusa, just south of Sensoji, a short walk from Asakusa Station. Billed as a 'shin-soba-ya' (next-generation soba restaurant) built around the idea that a meal can help the body 'tonou' (naturally come into balance) — combining traditional Edo-style soba craft with a modern, gluten-free, low-glycemic approach devised with a molecular-nutrition counselor.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated aim is food that tastes indulgent — sashimi, grilled dishes, wagashi, and soba — while being gluten-free and low in sugar, so guests can eat without the bloating or heaviness they associate with a typical soba-and-tsuyu meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Three house-made 100% buckwheat soba noodles — juwari, sarashina, and kurobiki — made entirely without wheat binder, using flour from long-established Nerima miller Miyamoto Seifun (the same supplier used by the famed Kanda Yabu Soba), served with a proprietary gluten-free 'Edo tsuyu' dipping sauce.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's own account explains that a classic soba tsuyu normally carries hidden wheat gluten from the soy sauce used to make it, so the kitchen spent time developing a reformulated 'Edo tsuyu' recipe that keeps the same rich, aged Edo-style flavor while removing that wheat gluten — applied across all three of their soba varieties.

The concept was framed publicly as a 'shin-soba-ya' (next-gen soba shop) pairing a veteran soba craftsman's noodle-making with a molecular-nutrition counselor's menu design, aiming at food that's 'hard to gain weight from' and 'keeps you looking good.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source found addresses shared-kitchen or cross-contamination handling for celiac-level safety; the restaurant's claims are about ingredient composition (no wheat binder, reformulated tsuyu) rather than facility-level gluten controls.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant states it developed a proprietary 'Edo tsuyu' specifically to eliminate the wheat-derived gluten that a normal soba dipping sauce picks up from its soy sauce, applying this across all three noodle varieties.

positives.text: All three house soba noodles (juwari, sarashina, kurobiki) are 100% buckwheat with no wheat binder added, using flour from Miyamoto Seifun, the long-established Nerima mill that also supplies the noted Kanda Yabu Soba.
The dipping sauce (tsuyu) itself was reformulated by the kitchen to remove wheat gluten, rather than just serving gluten-free noodles alongside a conventional sauce.

cultural_comfort.note: A newly opened, design-forward concept restaurant in the heart of tourist Asakusa with online table-reservation availability, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found in available sources, so first-time foreign visitors should expect a mostly-Japanese experience.

places.name: 鶏よし (Tori Yoshi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation process was found. Yakitori is typically finished with a soy-based tare (standard Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat) or grilled shio-style (salt only); diners would need to specifically request shio skewers to avoid tare, and even then share a grill also used for tare-brushed skewers. The oyako-don's sauce and the restaurant's soups/broths are soy- and dashi-based, which commonly carries wheat. The menu also includes fried items (e.g., cartilage karaage), and there is no indication of a dedicated fryer or any allergy protocol. Treat this as a high-risk venue for celiac diners; do not rely on an established gluten-free process.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around chicken — yakitori, oyako-don (chicken and egg), and chicken mizutaki hot pot — with no vegetarian or vegan dishes identified in any menu listing found. Broths are chicken-bone based and soy sauce/tare is used throughout, so this is not a viable option for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Fri: lunch 11:30-14:30 (LO 14:00), dinner 17:00-21:00 (LO 20:30). Sat/Sun/holidays: lunch 11:30-15:00 (LO 14:00-14:30), dinner 17:00-21:00 (LO 20:30). Closed Mondays, plus one Tuesday per month.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa / Tawaramachi, Taito-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Toriyoshi traces its roots to the postwar years, when the shop began as a traditional Japanese kappo (割烹) restaurant. About 19 years ago it was reworked into its current form focused on charcoal-grilled chicken (yakitori) and mizutaki hot pot — giving the shop roughly 70 years of

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Taito-ku, Tokyo — a few minutes' walk from Sensō-ji, near Tawaramachi Station and the Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station
chef_bio.background: Toriyoshi traces its roots to the postwar years, when the shop began as a traditional Japanese kappo (割烹) restaurant. About 19 years ago it was reworked into its current form focused on charcoal-grilled chicken (yakitori) and mizutaki hot pot — giving the shop roughly 70 years of history in the neighborhood when counted back to its kappo-restaurant origins.
chef_bio.specialty: Toroeru oyako-don — a 'melting' chicken-and-egg rice bowl made with Date jidori chicken from Fukushima, its skin charcoal-grilled first for fragrance, topped with rich, thick egg and plenty of scallion, served with the house's clear chicken soup at lunch.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name combines 鶏 (tori, chicken) — a nod to its use of jidori chicken — with 'Yoshie,' the name of a previous proprietress, from whom the second half of the name was taken.
What most yakitori shops call tsukune, Toriyoshi calls 'dango' instead, because the mixture is kneaded from a whole chicken without any binder.
The house mizutaki broth is made by simmering local chicken bones with aromatic vegetables for around 12 hours, crushing the bones to draw out collagen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen fries items such as cartilage karaage and cooks all yakitori on a shared charcoal grill; no separate gluten-free preparation area, fryer, or grill section is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu's core dishes (oyako-don sauce, yakitori tare, clear soup/broths) are soy- and dashi-based; standard Japanese soy sauce and tare contain wheat, and no wheat-free soy sauce or tare alternative is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is chicken-based (yakitori, oyako-don, mizutaki hot pot); shared grills, fryers, and chicken-bone broths are used throughout with no vegetarian or vegan prep line identified.

cultural_comfort.note: A long-running, very local neighborhood chicken restaurant with tatami and counter seating; expect a Japanese-language menu and traditional service rather than English-forward, tourist-oriented hospitality.

places.name: 喜久屋 (Kikuya)
places.gf_detail: No shop-specific gluten-free statement was found. The mame daifuku (mochi + azuki + sugar) uses ingredients that are naturally free of wheat, but the mitarashi dango's base is made from jōshinko (rice flour, gluten-free grain) while the mitarashi sauce itself is typically soy-sauce-based — and soy sauce (shoyu) sold in Japan is usually brewed with wheat — so the finished dango is not confirmed gluten-free. Ask staff about the sauce's ingredients and about shared prep surfaces before ordering if you have celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: No shop-specific vegan statement was found. Based on the ingredients described for the two named specialty items — mame daifuku (mochi rice, azuki beans, sugar) and mitarashi dango (jōshinko rice-flour dango with a soy-sauce/sugar/starch glaze) — neither item appears to rely on dashi, bonito, egg, or dairy, but this is inferred from general recipe conventions and Kikuya's own ingredient descriptions, not a confirmed vegan claim from the shop. Broader menu items (other dango/daifuku flavors) were not individually checked.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-17:30（売り切れ次第閉店） 定休日：月曜日（祝日の場合は翌日）
places.neighborhood: Asakusa / Nakamise-dori (near Kaminarimon)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kikuya is a long-running sweets stall on Asakusa's Nakamise-dori, the covered shopping street that runs from Kaminarimon Gate to Sensoji Temple. It sits about nine shops in from Kaminarimon, on the right-hand side as you walk toward the temple, and specializes in grilled dango an

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Kikuya is a long-running sweets stall on Asakusa's Nakamise-dori, the covered shopping street that runs from Kaminarimon Gate to Sensoji Temple. It sits about nine shops in from Kaminarimon, on the right-hand side as you walk toward the temple, and specializes in grilled dango and daifuku made in-house.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes sourcing carefully selected domestic ingredients — Tokachi red beans, Shiga-grown mochi rice — and avoiding additives so the natural flavor of the rice and beans comes through.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly grilled mitarashi dango — skewered dango made from jōshinko (non-glutinous rice flour), grilled to order and finished with warm mitarashi sauce for a just-made, chewy texture — and mame daifuku, mochi (made with Shiga-grown rice) wrapped around whole Tokachi azuki beans for a lightly salted, textured tsubu-an filling.

anecdotes.text: A Tokyo visitor blog singles out Kikuya as a favorite Nakamise stop for grabbing a 120-yen skewer of hot grilled dango to eat while strolling toward Sensoji, noting it's especially busy around New Year's hatsumode crowds.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Mitarashi-style dango sauce is conventionally made with soy sauce (shoyu), sugar, and starch; soy sauce produced in Japan is usually wheat-brewed, so mitarashi dango is generally not considered gluten-free unless a shop confirms otherwise — this is a general caution about the dish style, not a statement specific to Kikuya.

positives.text: The shop's two named specialty items are built from plant-based staples — daifuku is mochi rice and Tokachi azuki beans with sugar, and the dango base is jōshinko (rice flour) — with the official site emphasizing no additives and domestic ingredient sourcing, though this describes ingredient philosophy rather than a formal allergen or vegan certification.

cultural_comfort.note: Located on the extremely tourist-heavy Nakamise-dori right by Kaminarimon, and the shop maintains an English-language section of its site, but no source confirms English-speaking staff or an allergen menu, so expect basic gesture/simple-phrase level interaction.

places.name: 吾妻橋やぶそば (Azumabashi Yabusoba)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Edo-mae "Yabu" style soba shop, which by convention uses nihachi (2:8) noodles — roughly 20% wheat flour blended with buckwheat — not 100%-buckwheat (juwari) noodles, so the noodles themselves are not gluten-free. The Kanda-Yabusoba-lineage dipping sauce (tsuyu) is a soy-sauce-based warishita, and tempura dishes on the menu use wheat batter. No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation was found in any available source. Not recommended for diners with celiac disease or a wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-forward, traditional Edo-mae soba-ya — duck (kamo) broth and duck-nanban dishes are the signature, best-known items, alongside a soy/dashi-based dipping sauce typical of the style (traditionally bonito-based in this genre, though not explicitly confirmed for this specific shop). No vegan menu, substitution, or dashi disclosure was found in any available source. A plain mori-soba might be closest to workable but the sauce itself is the open question — ask staff directly before ordering, and do not assume it is vegan.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–15:00, closing early once the day's soba sells out; closed Mondays and Tuesdays. (One secondary source listed a later 16:00 close; treated here as the outer bound of an inherently variable sell-out schedule.)
places.neighborhood: 浅草・隅田川沿い（駒形橋たもと、墨田区吾妻橋）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Despite the name 'Azumabashi' (Azuma Bridge), the shop today actually stands at the Sumida-ku foot of Komagata-bashi, one bridge south of Azuma-bashi itself, along the Sumida River a few minutes from Asakusa Station. It's a small, old-school Edo-mae soba-ya that keeps a lunch-onl

chef_bio.origin: Founded in 1984 (Showa 59) by a founding chef-owner who spent 20 years training at the famed Kanda Yabusoba before striking out on his own — a direct lineage from one of Tokyo's most storied soba houses.
chef_bio.background: Despite the name 'Azumabashi' (Azuma Bridge), the shop today actually stands at the Sumida-ku foot of Komagata-bashi, one bridge south of Azuma-bashi itself, along the Sumida River a few minutes from Asakusa Station. It's a small, old-school Edo-mae soba-ya that keeps a lunch-only rhythm true to the genre — the room fills at midday with regulars sharing a quiet 'hiru-zake' (daytime drinking) ritual of a small carafe of sake alongside their soba before the kitchen closes for the day.
chef_bio.philosophy: Thin, fragrant, firm-textured Edo-mae buckwheat noodles paired with a rich, salty dipping sauce in the classic Yabu-soba style handed down from Kanda Yabusoba.
chef_bio.specialty: Duck (kamo) soba dishes — cold kamo-jiru zaru, hot kamo nanban — and a sesame-broth (goma-jiru) soba are the standout, most-loved items alongside the classic mori-soba.

anecdotes.text: The shop's founder trained for 20 years at Kanda Yabusoba before opening independently in 1984.
Known locally as a lunchtime 'sacred site' for hiru-zake — enjoying a discreet drink and soba-zensai alongside the noodles before the early close.
Though named for Azuma-bashi, the restaurant is actually located at the foot of Komagata-bashi bridge on the Sumida-ku side of the river.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's dipping sauce follows classic Edo-mae style — a rich, aromatic tsuyu built on a concentrated soy-sauce base, characteristic of the Kanda Yabusoba lineage.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built around duck (kamo) — cold kamo-jiru soba and hot kamo nanban are among the most recommended dishes — alongside chicken nanban and sesame-broth soba.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, cash-only, old-school Tokyo soba shop with no confirmed English menu — comfortable for Japanese speakers or visitors ready to point-and-order, less so for those wanting full English support.

places.name: どぜう飯田屋 (Dozeu Iidaya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation was found. The signature warishita sauce and the various soup bases (dojō-jiru, tofu-jiru, etc.) are built on standard Japanese soy sauce and miso, both of which normally contain wheat, and there is a fried dish (dojō karaage) on the menu with no information on a dedicated fryer. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners without direct, detailed confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around dojō (loach), unagi (eel), and seasonal namazu (catfish); even the soups and side dishes (e.g., tofu-jiru) come out of the same kitchen and most likely share fish-based dashi. No plant-based main dish or confirmed vegan option was found. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–21:30, Last Order 21:00, daily except Wednesdays (holiday transfer applies)
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Asakusa (West Asakusa), near Kappabashi, Asakusa
places.notes: Idaya has stayed in the same Nishi-Asakusa spot for over a century, passing the kitchen and the sauce recipe down through five generations of the Iida family. The current fourth-generation owner is training his son, Tadayuki, to take over as the fifth.

chef_bio.origin: Founded in 1902 (Meiji 35) as a dedicated dojō (loach) specialty shop in Nishi-Asakusa, with the family's food-service roots in the same neighborhood reaching back to the Keiō era (1865-68).
chef_bio.background: Idaya has stayed in the same Nishi-Asakusa spot for over a century, passing the kitchen and the sauce recipe down through five generations of the Iida family. The current fourth-generation owner is training his son, Tadayuki, to take over as the fifth.
chef_bio.philosophy: The recipe for the sweet-savory warishita sauce is a closely guarded family secret handed to only one successor at a time, and the kitchen still follows the same cooking method used since the shop's founding. The house precept — 'Father's job is to make the sauce and mind the shoe check' — is taken as a reminder that hospitality should extend all the way down to a guest's feet.
chef_bio.specialty: Dojō nabe — live loach hot pot, cooked to order in a shallow iron pot with the house's own soy-based warishita sauce and a heavy mound of chopped scallion; the bones are gently pre-softened so a little pleasant resistance remains in the bite. The menu also runs to hone-nuki nabe (boneless loach pot), yanagawa nabe (loach and egg), unagi (eel) grilled dishes, and, in winter, namazu (catfish) nabe.

anecdotes.text: The founding family's precept has always been 'Father's job is to make the sauce and mind the shoe check' — a reminder that hospitality extends all the way down to a guest's feet.

Marking its 113th year in business, the restaurant was in the process of passing from its fourth-generation owner to his son, Iida Tadayuki, the presumptive fifth generation, who grew up watching his father work.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dojō are cooked in sake and soy sauce and then simmered in a shallow iron pot with the house's soy-sauce-based warishita sauce, a recipe unchanged and passed down since the restaurant's founding.

cultural_comfort.note: A century-old, largely Japanese-language establishment popular with domestic regulars and Asakusa tourists alike; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found, though it can be booked through multilingual third-party reservation apps.

places.name: 悦納 (injoy) (Etsunō (Injoy))
places.gf_detail: No source found describes a dedicated gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free menu labeling. The menu centers on shōjin dishes that include wheat-based noodles (tantan noodles, ramen-style dishes, udon-style broth dishes), so the kitchen is not gluten-free by default. Soy sauce/tamari wheat content and cross-contamination handling were not documented in any source reviewed — celiac or strict-GF diners should call ahead and confirm directly.
places.vegan_detail: Consistently described across multiple independent sources as a fully vegan shōjin-ryori kitchen: no meat, fish, dairy, or eggs, and no "five pungent" alliums (onion, garlic, chives, leek, scallion) per Buddhist dietary tradition. Broths appear to be vegetable-based rather than fish/bonito-based, per reviewer commentary on the noodle dishes. No katsuo/dashi exceptions were found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-20:30 (L.O. 19:30), Monday and Wednesday-Sunday; closed Tuesdays. Lunch is walk-in; dinner is reservation-only, requested by 3:00pm the previous day.
places.neighborhood: Taito-ku, Asakusa (Hanakawado), directly by Asakusa Station
places.notes: No source found describes a dedicated gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free menu labeling. The menu centers on shōjin dishes that include wheat-based noodles (tantan noodles, ramen-style dishes, udon-style broth dishes), so the kitchen is not gluten-free by defaul

chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen frames shōjin ryori as drawing out the umami, nutritional value and beauty already present in each plant-based ingredient, with a stated ethos that nothing should be wasted — down to a single grain of rice or drop of juice.
chef_bio.specialty: Oriental vegan shōjin ryori — recurring signature dishes mentioned across reviews include dandan (tantan) noodles, a vegetable-broth noodle soup, ramen, bibimbap, and a lion's mane mushroom dish

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has hosted seasonal, hands-on events such as an Oriental-vegan chimaki (rice dumpling) making experience, and prepared a fully vegan osechi (Japanese New Year) box for 2023.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is described as excluding all animal-derived ingredients as well as the Buddhist "five pungent roots" (onion, garlic, chives, leek, scallion), consistent with strict shōjin-style plant-based cooking.

positives.text: Menu and site descriptions consistently state the food uses no animal products at all (fully vegan shōjin ryori), not just a vegetarian subset of dishes.
A reviewer noted the noodle-dish broth was vegetable-only yet tasted deeply umami, suggesting deliberate care in building flavor without fish-based dashi.

cultural_comfort.note: Reservations (required for dinner) are made by phone, and no confirmed English menu or English-speaking-staff claim was found, though the restaurant is covered favorably by several English-language vegan/travel sites.

places.name: 浅草もんじゃ かのや (Asakusa Monja Kanoya)
places.gf_detail: Monjayaki batter is wheat flour mixed with dashi; the shop's sauces and seasonings use standard (wheat-containing) Japanese soy sauce. All items are cooked together on shared communal teppan hotplates, so cross-contact is essentially unavoidable. No gluten-free batter, flour substitution, or dedicated cooking surface was mentioned in any source found.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around fresh seafood sourced daily from Tsukiji/Toyosu (the signature dish is a mentaiko/cod-roe mochi monja), and monjayaki batter/sauce typically relies on bonito-based dashi. No plant-based batter, dashi substitution, or vegan menu items were mentioned in any source found.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-23:00 (food L.O. 22:00, drinks L.O. 22:30), open every day
places.neighborhood: 浅草 - Hoppy Street
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Run directly by a family whose roots trace back to a 150-year-old Tsukiji fish wholesaler, sourcing seafood fresh daily from the Tsukiji and Toyosu markets. The restaurant sits on Asakusa's Hoppy Street (also called Niko-dori), the retro shitamachi (old downtown) alley of cheap i

roles: Restaurant run by a family of Tsukiji seafood wholesalers
chef_bio.origin: Asakusa / Tsukiji, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Run directly by a family whose roots trace back to a 150-year-old Tsukiji fish wholesaler, sourcing seafood fresh daily from the Tsukiji and Toyosu markets. The restaurant sits on Asakusa's Hoppy Street (also called Niko-dori), the retro shitamachi (old downtown) alley of cheap izakaya and monjayaki joints just west of Sensoji.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bring fresh, market-sourced seafood into the classic Tokyo monjayaki format, cooked tableside, in the informal, boisterous hospitality style typical of Hoppy Street's shitamachi drinking alley.
chef_bio.specialty: 明太もちもんじゃ (mentaiko-mochi monjayaki) — spicy cod roe and mochi folded into the classic Tokyo-style monja batter

anecdotes.text: The shop is run directly by a 150-year Tsukiji fishmonger business, sourcing all seafood fresh daily from Tsukiji and Toyosu; the menu features about 10 types of monjayaki plus over 40 optional toppings, and staff prepare a fluffy tableside egg appetizer before cooking the monja.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional shitamachi monjayaki spot on touristy Hoppy Street — expect Japanese-only menus and service with the general tourist-friendliness of the area, but no confirmed English-language support or dedicated allergy guidance.

places.name: 駒形どぜう 本店 (Komagata Dozeu (Honten))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation process was found in any available source. The signature dojō-nabe and related dishes are cooked in a shōyu-based broth; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed from wheat, so the broth and most seasoned dishes should be assumed to contain gluten. No online allergy menu exists (Japanese-language only, walk-in service), so anything beyond plain grilled items would need in-person confirmation with staff, which is not guaranteed to be in English. Not recommended for celiac diners without that direct, cautious confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: This is a single-protein specialty restaurant built entirely around dojō (pond loach) and related fish dishes cooked in fish/animal-based broth; no vegetarian or vegan dish was found in any menu source. Not a realistic option for vegan diners.
places.neighborhood: Komagata, Taitō-ku (Asakusa)
places.notes: Founded in 1801 (Kyōwa 1) by Echigoya Sukeshichi, a young man from Musashi Province (present-day northern Saitama) who came to Edo at 18, worked in service, and eventually opened a modest meal shop in Asakusa's Komagata district. The business has specialized in loach (dojō) cuisi

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Edo/Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Founded in 1801 (Kyōwa 1) by Echigoya Sukeshichi, a young man from Musashi Province (present-day northern Saitama) who came to Edo at 18, worked in service, and eventually opened a modest meal shop in Asakusa's Komagata district. The business has specialized in loach (dojō) cuisine ever since and still operates from a building in the Edo-period 'dashigeta-zukuri' merchant-house style.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserving Edo-period dining customs and atmosphere alongside the food itself — low tatami seating, unchanged recipes, and a building whose upper floor famously has no street-facing windows.
chef_bio.specialty: Dojō-nabe — whole pond loach simmered tableside in a shallow iron pot in a shōyu-based broth, the dish the restaurant is named for.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's distinctive four-character spelling 'どぜう' (instead of the standard five-character 'どぢやう') dates to a 1806 fire that destroyed the original shop. Considered the five-character spelling to be bad luck, the founder had a well-known signboard calligrapher write it as four characters instead; the shop's renewed popularity led other loach restaurants across Edo to copy the spelling.

During the Edo period the shogunate discouraged eating four-legged animals, so loach became an important source of animal protein for ordinary townspeople — a role the restaurant is remembered for supporting.

The main hall keeps its Edo-era merchant-house construction, and the street-facing second floor has no windows — traditionally explained as a way to avoid appearing to look down on a passing daimyo's procession.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house dojō-nabe broth is a shōyu-based warishita typical of this dish style; conventional Japanese soy sauce is wheat-brewed, so the broth is not inherently gluten-free. This is general knowledge about the dish type, not a restaurant-specific confirmed statement.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built entirely around dojō (pond loach) and other fish-based items prepared in a shared kitchen; no vegetarian or vegan dish was identified in any menu listing found online.

cultural_comfort.note: A Tokyo spot near Asakusa/Shibuya/Suidōbashi/Shinjuku; a little Japanese or pointing helps.

places.name: 浅草おでん大多福 (Asakusa Oden Otafuku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce were mentioned anywhere. The broth is seasoned with usukuchi (light) soy sauce, which contains wheat, and common oden items like chikuwa and hanpen fish cakes typically contain wheat starch as a binder. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff of every ingredient.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional, century-old oden restaurant, not vegan-oriented. The signature broth is built on katsuobushi (bonito flake) and kombu dashi, and the majority of oden ingredients on the menu are animal-based (fish cakes, eggs, octopus, meat). No vegan menu or plant-based dashi option was found in any source consulted.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sun approx. 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:00), though hours have varied seasonally in past years (e.g. 16:00-22:00 L.O.21:00 in cooler months); closed Monday. Sources partially conflict on exact current hours and day off — call ahead to confirm.
places.neighborhood: Senzoku, Asakusa, Taito-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Otafuku has operated in the same Senzoku neighborhood for over a century. Senzoku was historically the entrance district to the Yoshiwara pleasure quarter, and the shop was a haunt of geisha, their patrons, and local craftsmen — its old layout even included a separate entrance so

roles: sixth-generation owner/chef: Funadaiku Kaito (舩大工 海斗)
chef_bio.origin: Founded in Taishō 4 (1915) when the founder, granted permission by an established oden stall on the grounds of Hōsenji temple in Osaka, brought that Kansai-rooted oden craft to Tokyo and opened shop in Asakusa's Senzoku district.
chef_bio.background: Otafuku has operated in the same Senzoku neighborhood for over a century. Senzoku was historically the entrance district to the Yoshiwara pleasure quarter, and the shop was a haunt of geisha, their patrons, and local craftsmen — its old layout even included a separate entrance so guests wouldn't awkwardly encounter one another. The building was renovated in 2019, blending the shop's old-Tokyo atmosphere with a more modern interior while keeping the family recipe unchanged. It is now run by the sixth generation of the founding family.
chef_bio.philosophy: The current sixth-generation owner has said he learned the importance of preserving tradition from his grandfather (the fourth generation) and the importance of taking on new challenges from his father (the fifth generation) — a balance the shop tries to hold between its century-old recipe and a renewed, modernized space.
chef_bio.specialty: Oden simmered in a light (usukuchi) soy sauce and bonito-kombu dashi — a broth the shop describes as neither classic Kantō nor Kansai style but its own mellow, easy-to-drink signature, served from a rotating lineup of roughly 30-40 oden ingredients.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name comes from 'otafuku,' a smiling-woman motif believed to bring good fortune to a business.

Senzoku, where the shop sits, was once the gateway to the Yoshiwara pleasure district, and Otafuku was frequented by geisha, their patrons, and neighborhood craftsmen; the entrance was historically arranged so guests wouldn't run into one another.

The sixth-generation owner, Funadaiku Kaito, learned the importance of preserving tradition from his grandfather (4th generation) and the importance of taking on new challenges from his father (5th generation).
The shop uses light soy sauce and a bonito-and-kombu dashi, giving a broth that is 'mild and drinkable,' distinct from typical Kantō-style oden.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free broth option is mentioned in any source; the shop is a traditional oden restaurant with no stated allergy accommodations.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature broth is seasoned with usukuchi (light) soy sauce, a wheat-containing product, per the shop's own description of its dashi.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base dashi is made from katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and kombu, meaning the broth itself is not vegan and would flavor any vegetable ingredients cooked in the same pot.

cultural_comfort.note: A century-old neighborhood oden shop primarily serving a Japanese-speaking, local/regular clientele; English menu support is not confirmed, so some Japanese phrases or a translation app will help, especially for allergy questions.

places.name: とうふ川風 (Tofu Kawakaze)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program or wheat-free confirmation was found for this restaurant. As a traditional Japanese tofu-kaiseki/izakaya kitchen, standard soy sauce (which contains wheat) and dashi are almost certainly used in dipping sauces, simmered dishes, and hot pot broths; tofu and yuba themselves are naturally wheat-free but any accompanying tsuyu/ponzu or seasoning is a likely cross-contact and hidden-wheat risk. There is no evidence of a dedicated fryer or gluten-free menu. Celiac diners must ask staff in detail about each dish's sauce and broth before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Tofu, yuba, and simple boiled/chilled preparations could work for a vegan diner in principle, but this is not a vegan or vegetarian restaurant: course menus explicitly include chicken hot pot and other animal-protein dishes, and Japanese kaiseki/izakaya kitchens routinely use katsuo (bonito) dashi in soups, simmering liquid, and dipping sauces even for tofu dishes. This restaurant does not appear on any Asakusa vegan/vegetarian restaurant guide found in research. A vegan diner would need to specifically request dashi-free preparation and confirm dipping sauces are plant-based, with no guarantee the kitchen can fully accommodate that.
places.hours_raw: Dinner: Mon–Sat 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 20:30); Sun/holidays 17:00–21:30 (L.O. 20:00). Lunch: Sat/Sun/holidays 11:30–14:00 (weekday lunch available only by advance reservation for groups of 10 or more). Closed Mondays.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Kenban-dori, behind Sensō-ji)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tofu Kawakaze is the tofu-focused offshoot of Hamasei Hyōan, a kaiseki restaurant in Asakusa's historic Kenban (geisha registry office) quarter. The restaurant makes its own tofu and yuba in house rather than buying it in, built around a small, intimate dining room with tatami pr

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa — occupies the basement level of Hamasei Hyōan (濱清瓢庵), an established kaiseki restaurant on Kenban-dori (the old geisha-district street) just behind Sensō-ji.
chef_bio.background: Tofu Kawakaze is the tofu-focused offshoot of Hamasei Hyōan, a kaiseki restaurant in Asakusa's historic Kenban (geisha registry office) quarter. The restaurant makes its own tofu and yuba in house rather than buying it in, built around a small, intimate dining room with tatami private rooms — one seats just two people beside a lit aquarium of goldfish and tropical fish.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen makes tofu and yuba fresh using soybeans and spring water carried in from Chichibu, aiming for a sweeter, deeper soy flavor meant to be paired with sake rather than treated as a filler ingredient.
chef_bio.specialty: 汲み湯葉豆腐 (kumi-yuba dōfu) — hand-scooped yuba that diners lift themselves off the surface of gently simmering soy milk, alongside chilled and stacked house tofu.

anecdotes.text: The signature dish lets diners scoop their own yuba tableside as it forms on the surface of the simmering soy milk — a hands-on presentation the restaurant is known for.
The two-person private room beside a lit fish tank, with goldfish and tropical fish swimming past, is a popular reservation-only spot for couples.

positives.text: Tofu and yuba are made fresh in house daily from soybeans and Chichibu spring water rather than bought pre-made, which at least limits hidden mass-produced additives in the tofu itself (sauces and broths are a separate, unconfirmed question).

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional local tofu-kaiseki izakaya near Asakusa's old Kenban geisha quarter with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff — some Japanese (or a translation app) will help, especially for asking about ingredients.

places.name: 浅草もんじゃお好み焼き ぽんちゃん (Asakusa Monja Okonomiyaki Ponchan)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is advertised. Monjayaki and okonomiyaki batter is wheat-flour based by definition, and standard Japanese Worcester-style okonomi sauce and soy sauce (both typically wheat-containing) are core to the dish. Diners cook together on shared teppan grills, so cross-contact with wheat batter is essentially built into the format. Not suitable for celiac diners; no dedicated fryer, gluten-free batter, or allergy protocol was found in any source.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or certification is advertised. Traditional monjayaki dashi typically relies on bonito/katsuo-based stock and Worcester-style sauce (which can include fish/animal ingredients), and the menu features pork- and seafood-based dishes (e.g., whitebait, squid, pork curry cheese). A vegetable-heavy monja option exists and could be a starting point, but its dashi/sauce base is not confirmed to be free of animal products -- vegan diners should ask specifically about the dashi and sauce used before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 月～金・祝前日 16:00～23:00／土・日・祝日 12:00～23:00（フード・ドリンクともにラストオーダー22:30）。定休日は不定休。
places.neighborhood: Asakusa -- Hisago-dori shopping street (about 5 minutes on f
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ponchan opened in 2019 on Hisago-dori, the covered shotengai (shopping arcade) that runs just west of Sensoji toward the Hanayashiki amusement park. It's one of five Asakusa restaurants run by the same local operator (JNA Asakusa), sitting alongside sister shops covering izakaya,

roles: One of five Asakusa eateries run by the local JNA Asakusa restaurant group (alongside an izakaya, a soup-curry shop, a sweets shop, and an Italian dining spot)
chef_bio.origin: Opened in 2019 on Asakusa's Hisago-dori shopping street
chef_bio.background: Ponchan opened in 2019 on Hisago-dori, the covered shotengai (shopping arcade) that runs just west of Sensoji toward the Hanayashiki amusement park. It's one of five Asakusa restaurants run by the same local operator (JNA Asakusa), sitting alongside sister shops covering izakaya, soup curry, sweets, and Italian dining -- a small home-grown group rather than a national chain.
