chef_bio.philosophy: The shop was set up as a relaxed, welcoming teppan spot for the neighborhood -- a place where families, kids, and groups of friends gather around the hot plate together, serving the okonomiyaki and monjayaki that have long been eaten in Asakusa's shitamachi (old downtown) while also mixing in modern arrangements like tom yum kung monja and pork-curry-cheese okonomiyaki.
chef_bio.specialty: Iwanori shirasu monja (a monjayaki topped with seaweed and whitebait) is called out as the signature dish, alongside teppan-grilled sides like geso batter (grilled squid legs in butter).

anecdotes.text: A small tanuki (raccoon dog) figure marks the shop's entrance on Hisago-dori and is used as the go-to landmark in local directions -- look for the tanuki just off the arcade, close to the Hanayashiki amusement park.
The dining room is described as warm, wood-lined, and unhurried, with generously sized table seating built for groups sharing a teppan together.

positives.text: The menu includes a vegetable-forward monjayaki positioned as a lighter, healthier option, though this is a marketing description rather than a confirmed vegan or allergen-free dish.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood monja shop with an English Tabelog listing but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff -- basic Japanese (or a translation app) will help.

places.name: 紀文寿司 (Kibun-zushi)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or wheat-free soy sauce is documented. As a traditional Edomae shop, the sushi rice itself is typically simple (rice, vinegar, sugar, salt), but the signature nitsume glaze and any simmered (nimono) dishes are almost certainly built on regular wheat-containing soy sauce, and shared prep surfaces are the norm at an old-school sushi counter. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead and ask specifically about wheat-free soy sauce and cross-contact before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegetable-forward option is documented; this is a fish-focused Edomae sushi specialist, and its signature nitsume glaze and simmered dishes likely use bonito-based dashi, so it is not a realistic choice for a vegan diner.
places.neighborhood: 浅草（東武・都営・メトロ）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A century-plus-old family sushi shop near Sensoji. Its fourth-generation owner, the late Sekiya Bunkichi, was also a published food writer — his book 'Gyomi Raisan' (魚味礼讃) is a well-regarded meditation on fish and how to prepare it, and eating at the shop is described by fans as

chef_bio.chef_name: Sekiya family (5th generation: Sekiya Yoshinori / 関谷吉紀)
roles: 4th-generation owner Sekiya Bunkichi authored the fish essay collection 'Gyomi Raisan'
current owner is reported as 5th generation
chef_bio.origin: Founded in 1903 (Meiji 36) in Asakusa; the current building dates to 1948 (Showa 23), making it one of the neighborhood's oldest sushi counters.
chef_bio.background: A century-plus-old family sushi shop near Sensoji. Its fourth-generation owner, the late Sekiya Bunkichi, was also a published food writer — his book 'Gyomi Raisan' (魚味礼讃) is a well-regarded meditation on fish and how to prepare it, and eating at the shop is described by fans as a way to taste in person what he wrote about, aroma included.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keeps up old-school Edomae technique — simmering, vinegar-curing, and a house nitsume (simmered soy glaze) — rather than serving everything raw, using fish sourced from close to Tokyo Bay (Koshiba, Chiba, Kashima).
chef_bio.specialty: Chirashi sushi piled with Edomae-prepared toppings, and a nitsume glaze regulars rate among the best in the city

anecdotes.text: 紀文寿司は1903年（明治36年）創業。建物自体は昭和23年に作られたもので、浅草でもっとも古い寿司店のひとつ。
四代目店主の故・関谷文吉氏は著書『魚味礼讃』で魚や魚の調理に関する博識ぶりを披露。同店で食事をすると、味や見栄えのみならず氏が重視した『香り』も含めて追体験できる。
浅草の常連の間では、東京で一番とも言われるツメ（煮詰め）と、江戸前仕事をふんだんに使ったちらし寿司で知られる。

cultural_comfort.note: A century-old neighborhood sushi counter serving mostly local regulars in Japanese, with no confirmed English menu or website, so calling ahead in Japanese (or having your hotel call) will make the visit much smoother.

places.name: 阿娑縛（阿娑嚩） (Asaba)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated buckwheat (juwari) noodles, or dedicated fryer were mentioned anywhere. As a traditional soba-ya it almost certainly serves standard nihachi-style noodles (buckwheat blended with wheat flour) and a wheat-based shoyu dashi tsuyu. Not confirmed safe for celiac diners -- do not rely on this listing for strict gluten-free needs without calling ahead.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu, options, or accommodations were found in any source. Soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) at traditional shops like this is standard katsuobushi (bonito) dashi-based, which is not vegan. Treat as not vegan-friendly unless confirmed otherwise on site.
places.hours_raw: 火〜日 17:00〜24:00（月曜定休）／ Tue-Sun 17:00-00:00, closed Mondays
places.neighborhood: Asakusa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop's head chef trained at Rentama-an (蓮玉庵), a long-established soba restaurant in Ueno, before opening this small counter-only soba-ya near Asakusa's Tsukuba Express station in January 2012.

chef_bio.origin: Ueno soba tradition (trained at Rentama-an)
chef_bio.background: The shop's head chef trained at Rentama-an (蓮玉庵), a long-established soba restaurant in Ueno, before opening this small counter-only soba-ya near Asakusa's Tsukuba Express station in January 2012.
chef_bio.philosophy: An elegant, almost restaurant-like take on the neighborhood soba-ya, staying open past midnight so it can double as a late-night 'shime' (last bite) stop -- unusual among Asakusa soba shops, most of which close by early evening.
chef_bio.specialty: Seiro (chilled dipping soba) made from slightly coarse-ground buckwheat flour, praised for its smooth texture and aroma; goma (sesame) soba is another signature order.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name, 阿娑縛 (Asaba), was given to it by a Buddhist priest from nearby Sensoji Temple.
Unlike most Asakusa soba shops, which close in the early evening, Asaba stays open until around 1am, making it a favorite stop for a bowl of soba after a night of drinking.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, locally-oriented soba-ya with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so it suits diners comfortable ordering in Japanese.

places.name: 梅園 浅草本店 (Umezono Asakusa Honten)
places.gf_detail: The signature dish, awa-zenzai (steamed mochikibi grain + koshi-an red bean paste), and several other menu items — warabimochi, shiratama dumplings (rice flour), and plain anmitsu/kanten-based sweets — are made from ingredients that are traditionally wheat-free (rice, azuki beans, agar/kanten, sugar). However, the same kitchen also prepares clearly wheat-containing items on the same menu (dorayaki, cha-soba/tea-infused wheat noodles), so shared-kitchen cross-contact is possible. No official gluten-free labeling, dedicated-fryer statement, or allergen chart from the restaurant was found in this research — celiac diners should ask staff directly about specific items and preparation before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The most traditional items — awa-zenzai (mochikibi + koshi-an), plain or matcha anmitsu without cream, and warabimochi with kuromitsu (brown sugar syrup) — are made from ingredients that are conventionally plant-based (azuki bean paste, agar/kanten, rice-based mochi, sugar syrup), with no dashi/bonito expected in sweets preparation. However, several popular menu items (cream anmitsu, parfaits, soft-serve items like "dora soft") explicitly include dairy (ice cream/cream), and no restaurant-issued vegan menu or ingredient statement was found to confirm anko or other components are free of any animal-derived additives. Vegan diners should ask staff which specific items are dairy-free before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon-Fri): 10:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30). Weekends/holidays: 10:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30). Closed approximately 2 Wednesdays per month plus irregular additional holidays.
places.neighborhood: 浅草1丁目 / 浅草寺門前 (Asakusa, in front of Sensō-ji)
places.notes: Umezono began as a small teahouse on the grounds of Baion-in (a branch temple of Sensō-ji); the shop's name comes from the many plum (ume) trees that grew in the temple garden. The first-generation owner created an original sweet — awa-zenzai — that became so popular it has kept

roles: Family-run wagashi (Japanese sweets) shop, in business under the same name since 1854
chef_bio.origin: Founded in Ansei 1 (1854) as a teahouse inside a corner of Baion-in, a sub-temple of Sensō-ji, at the temple's front gate in Asakusa.
chef_bio.background: Umezono began as a small teahouse on the grounds of Baion-in (a branch temple of Sensō-ji); the shop's name comes from the many plum (ume) trees that grew in the temple garden. The first-generation owner created an original sweet — awa-zenzai — that became so popular it has kept the shop running as a dedicated Japanese sweets (kanmi) specialist for more than 170 years, still on the same approach to Sensō-ji.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself as balancing tradition and modernity — described by the family as roughly "eight parts guarding tradition, two parts pushing forward" — keeping the original anko recipe and decades-old mochi-making and steaming equipment while still updating the menu for new generations of visitors.
chef_bio.specialty: Awa-zenzai: despite the name ("millet zenzai"), it is made not with awa millet but with mochikibi, a plump, bright-yellow grain that is steamed, half-pounded to a sticky texture, and served with slow-simmered smooth koshi-an (strained sweet red bean paste) — the dish invented by the shop's founder.

anecdotes.text: The teahouse is mentioned in Nagai Kafū's novel 'Odoriko' (The Dancer): a passage describes trying to eat oshiruko/zenzai at Umezono but finding the shop too crowded to get in — evidence of how popular it already was in that era.

The shop's mochi-making machine in the kitchen has reportedly been in use for over 50 years, and the steamers are still sourced from the same original maker, as part of keeping the traditional preparation method for the anko and mochikibi.

cultural_comfort.note: A famous, heavily touristed Sensō-ji-front shop covered extensively by English-language travel guides (MATCHA, LIVE JAPAN, Tripadvisor), so English signage/picture menus are likely, but no confirmation of fluent English-speaking staff was found — basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will help.

places.name: 麺 みつヰ (Men Mitsui)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional wheat-noodle ramen shop. The hand-kneaded noodles are made from a blend of Hokkaido strong, medium, and whole-grain wheat flours, and the tare is soy-sauce based (standard Japanese shoyu, which typically contains wheat). No gluten-free noodle substitute, wheat-free soy sauce, dedicated prep area, or any gluten-free accommodation is mentioned in any source reviewed — not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature broths are built on whole-chicken (marudori) stock, and the shio version also carries seafood/dashi notes (鶏魚介); the menu also includes a pork-cutlet katsudon side. No plant-based broth, noodle, or vegan menu item is documented in any source reviewed — not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 火〜金 11:00-14:30／18:00-20:00、土・祝 11:00-16:00（材料がなくなり次第早仕舞いの場合あり）、定休日：日曜・月曜
places.neighborhood: Tawaramachi, Asakusa, Taito City
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The chef-owner trained for several years at Menya Shichisai (麺や七彩), the influential Tokyo ramen shop widely credited with pioneering MSG-free (無化調) ramen broths in the 2000s, before striking out on his own. The shop first opened near Nishi-Asakusa in December 2016, earned a Miche

chef_bio.origin: Fukui Prefecture
chef_bio.background: The chef-owner trained for several years at Menya Shichisai (麺や七彩), the influential Tokyo ramen shop widely credited with pioneering MSG-free (無化調) ramen broths in the 2000s, before striking out on his own.
chef_bio.philosophy: No chemical seasoning (無化調) — broths are built purely from whole chicken and aromatics, and the noodles are hand-kneaded fresh, one portion at a time, from a custom blend of Hokkaido strong, medium, and whole-grain wheat flours rather than bought pre-made.
chef_bio.specialty: Shoyu ramen with medium-thick, hand-kneaded (手もみ) crimped noodles in a clear whole-chicken (marudori) broth; the shio (salt) version layers in chicken-and-seafood depth, and a thinner straight-noodle option is also offered.

anecdotes.text: The medium-thick hand-kneaded noodles are massaged individually, portion by portion, giving them a firmer, almost wonton-like chew and a pronounced wheat aroma that the shop considers central to how the noodle carries the soup.
As a nod to his native Fukui Prefecture, the owner added Fukui-style oroshi soba (grated daikon soba) and a house-made-sauce pork katsudon to the menu alongside the ramen.
The shop closed its original Nishi-Asakusa location on July 31, 2022 and reopened in its current Tawaramachi spot on April 19, 2023, just off the Tawaramachi intersection near Ginza Line Exit 1.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu tare is standard Japanese soy sauce, which typically contains wheat; no wheat-free or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is documented for this shop.

positives.text: The broth is intentionally made without added MSG or chemical seasoning (無化調), relying on whole-chicken stock and aromatics for flavor — a plus for diners sensitive to MSG, though unrelated to gluten or animal-product content.

cultural_comfort.note: Orders and queue position are managed through a Japanese-language touch-panel ticket system before you're called to the counter; no English menu is documented, so recognizing ramen terms (shoyu, shio, noodle thickness) or pointing at photos helps.

places.name: ニュープラシッダ 浅草店 (New Prasidha Asakusa (Nyū Purashidda Asakusa)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free-soy-sauce information was found in any source. Curries are typically rice- or naan-based; naan itself is wheat and not an option for celiac diners, and Indian-style curries can be thickened or finished with wheat-containing ingredients depending on the recipe, so gluten status of any given curry is not confirmed. Rice-based orders (skipping naan) are plausible but unverified as gluten-free, and shared-kitchen cross-contact has not been addressed by the restaurant in any available source. Anyone with celiac disease should call ahead (03-6313-0040) and ask specifically about flour/soy sauce use and cross-contamination before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetable curry (ベジタブル) and dal (豆) are the vegetarian curry options, available both dine-in and as part of the takeout bento lineup, but no source confirms they are made without ghee, butter, cream, or other dairy/animal fat, which are common in North Indian-style curries. Naan is not vegan by default in most Japanese Indian restaurants (often contains dairy/egg). Vegan diners should ask staff directly whether the vegetable curry and dal can be prepared without ghee/butter/cream and whether rice can replace naan.
places.neighborhood: 浅草・花川戸
places.cuisine: インドカレー・ネパール料理
places.notes: An Indian-Nepali curry restaurant tucked into a quiet residential corner of "oku-Asakusa" (inner Asakusa), a short walk from Senso-ji in the Hanakawado neighborhood. Local write-ups describe it as a long-running, regulars-favored spot (cited as roughly a decade-plus in the neighb

chef_bio.origin: Nepal / North India (kitchen staffed by Nepali and North Indian cooks)
chef_bio.background: An Indian-Nepali curry restaurant tucked into a quiet residential corner of "oku-Asakusa" (inner Asakusa), a short walk from Senso-ji in the Hanakawado neighborhood. Local write-ups describe it as a long-running, regulars-favored spot (cited as roughly a decade-plus in the neighborhood) known for authentic North Indian and Nepali cooking rather than a tourist-facing curry chain.
chef_bio.specialty: Biryani and cheese naan are called out as the standout orders, alongside freshly baked, fluffy naan served with a lineup of about a dozen curries (chicken, mutton, keema, seafood, dal, vegetable) spiced from mild to very hot across five heat levels.

anecdotes.text: "奥浅草の静かな一角にある本格インドカレー屋さん" — described as an authentic Indian curry restaurant set in a quiet corner of inner Asakusa.
Offers home-delivery and fresh-baked naan with curry bento takeout to the Asakusa/Hanakawado area.

cultural_comfort.note: Run by Nepali/North Indian staff serving a largely local Japanese clientele near Senso-ji; delivery-platform listings (Uber Eats, Demae-can, Wolt) show some English dish names, but on-site English fluency and picture/allergy menus are unconfirmed, so expect basic communication rather than full English support.

places.name: 中華ソバ ビリケン (Chuka Soba Biliken)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles (hand-kneaded, not gluten-free) and a conventional soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which in Japanese ramen almost always contains wheat. No gluten-free noodle substitute, gluten-free menu, or dedicated preparation area was mentioned in any source found. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature broth is built from duck and chicken stock, and the chashu topping is duck — the dish is fundamentally meat-based with no vegetarian or vegan option mentioned in any source found. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:00-15:00 & 17:30-21:00; Sat–Sun 11:00-21:00 (no fixed closed day; occasional irregular closures posted on Instagram/X)
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Kaminarimon), Taitō-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kimotomoto met his wife while the two trained together at Ramen Itsuki in Ōme. After going independent as a couple, they first opened the salt-ramen shop Ramen Kai in nearby Kuramae, then in July 2019 opened this duck-broth sister shop a minute from Kaminarimon in Asakusa.

chef_bio.origin: Trained at the acclaimed ramen shop Itsuki in Ōme, western Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Kimotomoto met his wife while the two trained together at Ramen Itsuki in Ōme. After going independent as a couple, they first opened the salt-ramen shop Ramen Kai in nearby Kuramae, then in July 2019 opened this duck-broth sister shop a minute from Kaminarimon in Asakusa.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen is known for skipping chemical seasoning (no MSG/artificial flavor boosters), building the soup's savoriness from duck and chicken stock and a housemade soy tare instead.
chef_bio.specialty: Duck-and-chicken shoyu ramen with hand-kneaded noodles and duck chashu

anecdotes.text: The shop opened July 5, 2019 as the second, sister location of the well-known Kuramae salt-ramen shop Ramen Kai, run by the same husband-and-wife team, but with its own duck-and-chicken shoyu concept.

Kimotomoto and his wife trained together at Ramen Itsuki in Ōme and married after becoming independent; local food blogs describe the shop as a labor of love built around duck stock, hand-kneaded noodles and juicy duck chashu.
A ramen blogger writing under the title 'no-chemical-seasoning ramen' described the shop as arguably Asakusa's top no-MSG ramen destination.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's signature tare is a standard soy-sauce (shoyu) base built for a duck-and-chicken broth ramen; no wheat-free or tamari-based alternative was mentioned in any source, so the soy sauce used should be assumed to contain wheat as is typical for Japanese ramen tare.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering and menu are primarily Japanese-language; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, though it sits in a heavily touristed part of Asakusa near Kaminarimon.

places.name: おばんざい味ゆぅ (Obanzai Miyuu (Aji-Yuu))
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, fryer, or allergy protocol was found in any source. The kitchen uses homemade miso and traditional seasonings; standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi (which can contain wheat and bonito) are likely used unless the vegetable-forward dishes happen to avoid them. Because the menu changes daily and nothing about wheat-free preparation is documented, celiac diners should call ahead (050-5869-7791) and confirm which of the day's dishes are safe.
places.vegan_detail: No confirmed all-vegan menu. The obanzai are vegetable-heavy and change daily, but obanzai izakaya menus typically also include fish- or meat-based items and traditional dashi (often bonito-based), and no source confirms a katsuo/dashi-free or fully plant-based option. Vegan diners should ask staff which of the day's dishes are free of fish stock, bonito flakes, and animal products.
places.hours_raw: 16:00–23:00, closed Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: Asakusa, near Sensō-ji / Kaminarimon, along Kōen-dōri (Tsuku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Miyuu, the proprietress, opened this counter-only obanzai spot on Asakusa's Kōen-dōri in May 2026. She hand-cooks a daily-changing lineup of 5-8 obanzai (home-style Japanese side dishes), leaning on seasonal vegetables, homemade miso, and ingredients sourced from her home prefect

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima Prefecture
chef_bio.background: Miyuu, the proprietress, opened this counter-only obanzai spot on Asakusa's Kōen-dōri in May 2026. She hand-cooks a daily-changing lineup of 5-8 obanzai (home-style Japanese side dishes), leaning on seasonal vegetables, homemade miso, and ingredients sourced from her home prefecture of Hiroshima.
chef_bio.specialty: A rotating selection of 5-8 handmade obanzai dishes, chosen by the guest from large plates set along the counter, capped off with the chef's-recommendation 'Aji-Yuu Mankitsu' course (obanzai, a signature dish, rice, dessert, and a drink).

anecdotes.text: Guests pick their dishes from large plates lined up on the counter — a self-selection obanzai format — with the 5-8 items on offer changing daily depending on what's in season.
Miyuu curates her sake list around breweries in Fukushima and Hiroshima that she came across through personal connections, pairing them with her home-style cooking.

positives.text: The daily menu emphasizes seasonal vegetables and homemade miso, giving it a naturally plant-leaning lean-up, though this has not been documented as a formal vegan or allergy-safe program.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 8-seat counter-only izakaya with a Japanese-language menu and phone-only reservations — most comfortable with a Japanese-speaking companion or guide rather than walking in solo without any Japanese.

places.name: 甘味処 西山（旧店名：西山菓子店） (Kanmidokoro Nishiyama (formerly Nishiyama Ka)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, signage, or celiac-safety statement was found from the shop itself. The signature anmitsu, zenzai, and kakigori are built from ingredients that are traditionally wheat-free — housemade azuki paste (tsubu-an/koshi-an), kanten (agar) jelly, rice-flour shiratama dumplings, and kuromitsu (brown sugar syrup) — but this has not been confirmed with staff, and there is no information on shared prep surfaces, cross-contact, or whether any items (e.g., the shop's fukufuku manju buns, which are wheat-based) are prepared in the same space as the sweets. Celiac diners should ask staff directly before ordering; this is not a gluten-free-verified kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: This is not a vegan-focused shop and no vegan menu or labeling was found. The housemade red bean pastes, mochi/shiratama dumplings, agar jelly, and kuromitsu syrup are typically plant-based in traditional preparation, so a plain anko or mitsumame-style order may work with a modification request, but the anmitsu's signature housemade ice cream is dairy-based, and there is no confirmation of egg/dairy-free status for other components (e.g., any glaze or filling in the manju buns). As a sweets shop rather than a savory kitchen, hidden dashi/bonito is less of a concern than at a typical Japanese restaurant, but this has not been confirmed with staff.
places.hours_raw: 10:00〜19:00、定休日：主に水曜日（年末年始・浅草の行事や祝日と重なる水曜日は営業）
places.neighborhood: 浅草・雷門周辺 (Asakusa, near Kaminarimon)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1852 (Kaei 5) near Kaminarimon as a dry-goods and grain merchant trading in azuki beans and other staples; sweets made from the shop's own azuki stock gradually became the main business. In 2009 the sixth-generation owner renamed the shop from "Nishiyama Kashi-ten" (Ni

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Founded in 1852 (Kaei 5) near Kaminarimon as a dry-goods and grain merchant trading in azuki beans and other staples; sweets made from the shop's own azuki stock gradually became the main business. In 2009 the sixth-generation owner renamed the shop from "Nishiyama Kashi-ten" (Nishiyama confectionery store) to the more approachable "Kanmidokoro Nishiyama. "
chef_bio.philosophy: Carries on a tradition, dating back to the end of the Edo period, of carefully selecting quality ingredients and preparing them by hand.
chef_bio.specialty: Tokusei Nishiyama Anmitsu (特製西山あんみつ) — coarse-strained (tsubu-an) and smooth-strained (koshi-an) housemade red bean pastes served with two flavors of housemade ice cream and mochi dumplings (shiratama), alongside seasonal zenzai and kakigori (shaved ice with matcha syrup and red bean).

anecdotes.text: The shop began in 1852 as a dried-goods and grain merchant near Kaminarimon dealing in azuki beans; sweets made from that same azuki stock gradually grew into the main business, eventually becoming today's kanmi-dokoro (sweets parlor).
In 2009 the sixth-generation owner changed the shop's name from "Nishiyama Kashi-ten" to "Kanmidokoro Nishiyama" to make it feel more approachable to customers.

positives.text: Anko (red bean paste), ice cream, and other sweets are made in-house daily using carefully selected raw ingredients, per the shop's own long-stated approach to sourcing.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, Japanese-oriented neighborhood sweets shop near Sensoji with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, though its location on the main Kaminarimon approach means tourists visit regularly and pointing at photos/menu items should work fine.

places.name: くくりひめ珈琲 (Kukurihime Coffee)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, rice-flour claim, or allergy-handling statement was found in any source. Straight coffee is naturally gluten-free, but the signature parfaits layer in cake, cookie, and other wheat-based components as a matter of course, and there is no stated separation of prep areas or ingredients. Treat this as a normal cafe for GF purposes — ask staff item-by-item and do not assume any dessert is safe without checking.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or dairy-free statement was found. The signature parfaits are built on cream, ice cream and other dairy components typical of the genre, with no indication of plant-based substitutes. Black coffee itself is vegan, but do not assume any parfait or sweet is dairy-free without asking, and there's no confirmation on hidden bonito/dashi in any savory items (the menu appears to be coffee and dessert-focused, so this risk is likely low but unconfirmed).
places.hours_raw: 9:00–20:00 daily (L.O. 19:30); closed Tuesdays. A limited-run boxed dessert set ('くくりひめの贈り物', 20/day) requires advance reservation by the day before.
places.neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa), Taito-ku — about 1 minute's walk from Kaminari
places.cuisine: カフェ・自家焙煎珈琲
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in March 2021 a short walk from Asakusa's Kaminarimon Gate, the cafe pairs an in-house roaster with Japanese-Western fusion desserts.

chef_bio.background: Opened in March 2021 a short walk from Asakusa's Kaminarimon Gate, the cafe pairs an in-house roaster with Japanese-Western fusion desserts.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop is named for Kukurihime-no-Kami (菊理媛), a deity of ties and reconciliation from the Nihon Shoki, chosen with the wish that connections made here would spread outward from Asakusa to Japan and the world. The brand mark is five overlapping circles standing for en (縁, bonds) that form five petals where they meet, and the coffee blends reference the 24 sekki (solar terms) of the traditional calendar as a wish for customers' wellbeing.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal wagashi-style fruit parfaits (和パフェ) built around single-origin, in-house roasted coffee (Colombian, Honduran and Ethiopian beans roasted to each bean's character) plus original nut butters and coffee confitures.

anecdotes.text: The shop's own 'Our Thoughts' page lays out the Kukurihime naming story and the five-circle en (connection) symbolism behind the coffee blends.
A branding case study describes the shop's identity design, built around the five-petal 'en' motif and the in-house roaster's bean selection.
A travel feature describes visiting for a fruit parfait near Sensoji and 'feeling en (connection)' in the seasonal dessert.

cultural_comfort.note: A polished, Instagram/tourist-friendly cafe near Kaminarimon with online booking via multiple platforms, but no confirmed English menu — basic Japanese or pointing at photos should get you through ordering.

places.name: 釜めし むつみ (Kamameshi Mutsumi)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional kamameshi shop where the rice is seasoned with soy sauce (and typically mirin) and topped with fish, seafood, chicken, and mushrooms — soy sauce in Japan is almost always wheat-based unless labeled otherwise. No gluten-free menu, tamari substitution, or dedicated preparation process was found in any source, and it is a small traditional kitchen with no indication of allergen-separated cooking. Should be treated as unsafe for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff (in Japanese), and even then cross-contact via shared broths/rice pots is likely.
places.vegan_detail: Kamameshi at this shop is built around fish, seafood, chicken, and (per menu listings) seafood-forward varieties like crab and mixed-seafood kamameshi over dashi-seasoned rice; even vegetable/mushroom versions are very likely cooked in the same bonito-based dashi used throughout the menu. No vegan-specific dishes, dashi substitutions, or plant-based options were mentioned in any source found. Should be treated as not vegan-friendly without a very specific, in-person request to confirm a katsuo-free preparation, which is unlikely to be accommodated at a shop this traditional.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:30 (L.O.), Dinner 17:15-22:00. Closed Wednesdays and every other Tuesday.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Oku-Asakusa, near Asakusa Kemban), Taito City / Tsu
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A neighborhood kamameshi (clay-pot rice) shop that has operated in the same Asakusa backstreet since 1968, now run by a third-generation owner who has carried on the original secret rice-cooking method passed down from the shop's founder. The two-story traditional wooden building

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (near the old Asakusa Kemban geisha-registry office, in the quieter "Oku-Asakusa" backstreets)
chef_bio.background: A neighborhood kamameshi (clay-pot rice) shop that has operated in the same Asakusa backstreet since 1968, now run by a third-generation owner who has carried on the original secret rice-cooking method passed down from the shop's founder. The two-story traditional wooden building, with tatami seating throughout, sits near the historic Asakusa Kemban geisha-registry office in the quieter western part of the neighborhood, away from the main Nakamise/Sensoji tourist crush.
chef_bio.philosophy: Each pot is cooked individually to order — taking roughly 30 minutes — using a closely guarded cooking technique (rather than the classic folk rhyme for rice-cooking) said to produce fragrant broth-infused rice with a good crust without sticking or burning; guests are expected to settle in with a drink and appetizers while they wait rather than expect fast service.
chef_bio.specialty: Individually cooked kamameshi (clay-pot rice) — the menu runs to nearly 20 varieties, from gomoku (mixed vegetable) and seafood kamameshi to crab and octopus versions, each pot made to order with seasonal fish, vegetables, mushrooms, or chicken over soy-seasoned rice.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1968, the shop is now on its third generation of family ownership, with the current owner carefully preserving the founder's secret rice-cooking method.

The restaurant is a two-story traditional Japanese house near the old Asakusa Kemban (geisha registry office), with roughly 50 tatami-mat seats across both floors and autographed shikishi from past celebrity visitors on the walls.
A kamameshi order takes about 30 minutes to cook, so regulars typically order appetizers and drinks to enjoy while they wait; the shop offers close to 20 original kamameshi varieties.

cultural_comfort.note: A local, old-school kamameshi shop near Asakusa Kemban with no evidence of English menus, English-speaking staff, or tourist-oriented signage, so a translation app or basic Japanese is recommended, especially for explaining any dietary restrictions.

places.name: 浅草来々軒 (Asakusa Rairaiken)
places.gf_detail: The signature dish, 百年醤油らうめん, is a wheat-noodle ramen made with a heritage wheat variety, and the broth is seasoned with standard (wheat-containing) Yamasa soy sauce, the same brand used since the shop's 1910 founding. The menu also features shumai and wonton, both traditionally wheat-wrapped. No gluten-free noodle, sauce substitution, dedicated fryer, or allergy-menu option was mentioned in any source found.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on pork- and seafood-based dishes: pork shumai and wonton, and Tenshindon topped with crab and egg. No vegetarian/vegan broth, dashi substitute, or plant-based menu item was mentioned in any source found; assume the ramen broth and most dishes contain animal products.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa, near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station (浅草三丁目)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ozaki Kan'ichi left his post at the Yokohama customs house and, drawing on ties to Yokohama Chinatown, brought a team of Cantonese cooks to Asakusa to open 来々軒 in 1910. He adapted the pork-and-noodle 'buta soba' already served in Yokohama's Chinatown into a soy-sauce-based broth

chef_bio.origin: Founded 1910 (Meiji 43) in Asakusa by Ozaki Kan'ichi, a former Yokohama customs official
chef_bio.background: Ozaki Kan'ichi left his post at the Yokohama customs house and, drawing on ties to Yokohama Chinatown, brought a team of Cantonese cooks to Asakusa to open 来々軒 in 1910. He adapted the pork-and-noodle 'buta soba' already served in Yokohama's Chinatown into a soy-sauce-based broth suited to Japanese palates -- widely credited as the origin point of Tokyo (shoyu) ramen and of Japan's 'machi-chuka' neighborhood Chinese-restaurant culture. The shop was hugely popular, reportedly serving 2,500-3,000 customers a day at its peak and touching off Japan's first ramen boom. It closed during WWII, reopened near Tokyo Station in 1945, and the family's operation finally ended in 1976. For years afterward the Shin-Yokohama Ramen Museum researched the shop's history, tracing the original wheat variety and the soy sauce brand used in the broth. On July 2, 2026, the founder's 92-year-old grandson Takahashi Kunio and his 38-year-old great-grandson Takahashi Yūsaku reopened the shop in Asakusa, reviving the family name after an 80-year absence.
chef_bio.philosophy: To recreate, as faithfully as research allows, the exact flavor Ozaki Kan'ichi served in 1910 -- treating the noodles, broth and soy sauce as a piece of food heritage to be preserved and passed to a new generation of the family.
chef_bio.specialty: 百年醤油らうめん (Hyakunen Shōyu Raumen, "hundred-year soy sauce ramen") -- a soy-sauce ramen made with a wheat variety said to carry the genetics of Meiji-era flour and the same Yamasa soy sauce the original shop used from its founding; also known for oversized (60g) shumai and for Tenshindon, a crab-and-egg rice bowl the shop is credited with inventing.

anecdotes.text: Founder Ozaki Kan'ichi, a former Yokohama customs official, brought a dozen Chinese cooks from Yokohama's Nankinmachi (Chinatown) to open the shop in Asakusa in 1910, converting Yokohama's pork noodle soup into a soy-sauce broth familiar to Japanese diners.
At its peak the Meiji-era shop is said to have served 2,500 to 3,000 customers a day, touching off Japan's first ramen boom.

After the original shop closed, the Shin-Yokohama Ramen Museum spent years researching its history, tracing the heritage wheat variety and the original Yamasa soy sauce used in the broth for the revived shop's signature ramen.

The revival was driven by the founder's 92-year-old grandson Takahashi Kunio and his 38-year-old great-grandson Takahashi Yūsaku, who reopened the shop in Asakusa on July 2, 2026, 80 years after the family last ran a shop there.
Tenshindon, the crab-and-egg rice bowl now on the menu, is said to have originated at the shop in response to customers who wanted something they could eat quickly.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's signature ramen broth uses standard Yamasa soy sauce, the same wheat-containing shoyu the original 1910 shop used, rather than a gluten-free/tamari alternative.

cultural_comfort.note: A newly reopened, media-spotlighted heritage noodle shop in a very touristy pocket of Asakusa; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service was found, so expect a Japanese-language ordering experience (pointing at photos or menu items should work fine).

places.name: あかし (Akashi)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional tempura restaurant: shrimp and vegetables are fried in a wheat-flour batter, and no source mentions a gluten-free flour, a dedicated fryer, or any celiac-safe procedure. The tentsuyu dipping sauce is a soy-sauce-based sauce, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat. No gluten-free accommodation was found in any source, so this is not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on shrimp and other seafood tempura sets; vegetable tempura pieces are typically included in sets, but they are fried in the same oil as shrimp/fish, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is dashi-based, traditionally made with bonito (katsuo) flakes. No vegan-specific menu, substitution, or accommodation was found in any source, so this cannot be recommended as vegan-safe.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa / Tawaramachi, Taito Ward
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, counter-only Edo-mae tempura shop tucked just off Kaminarimon-dori near Tawaramachi Station, in the Asakusa neighborhood. It has operated for decades as a family-style tempura counter rather than a large restaurant, with seating limited to a ground-floor counter and a se

chef_bio.background: A small, counter-only Edo-mae tempura shop tucked just off Kaminarimon-dori near Tawaramachi Station, in the Asakusa neighborhood. It has operated for decades as a family-style tempura counter rather than a large restaurant, with seating limited to a ground-floor counter and a second counter upstairs.
chef_bio.philosophy: A commitment to Edo-mae tempura technique — wrapping the natural flavor of fresh, seasonal ingredients in a light, crisp batter and frying each piece to order rather than in advance.
chef_bio.specialty: The tempura teishoku (set meal) and tendon (tempura rice bowl), fried to order at the counter with shrimp and seasonal vegetables in a light Edo-style batter.

anecdotes.text: Described by local food blogs as devoted to Edo-style tempura for around 27 years, coating quality ingredients in a light batter and frying them fresh at the counter.
Known as a small, old-school tempura counter that draws a lunchtime queue for its reasonably priced tempura set and tendon, served fresh from the fryer.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, cash-only counter shop with no evidence of an English menu — visitors comfortable pointing at a menu or using basic Japanese (and prepared to queue at lunch) will have the easiest time.

places.name: 来集軒 (Raishūken)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation found in any source. The noodles are wheat noodles made at the shop's own attached noodle factory, and the broth is soy-sauce (shoyu) based, which in Japan almost always contains wheat. No GF-alternative noodle, no allergy menu, and no cross-contamination information were mentioned anywhere. This is not a safe option for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan accommodation found. The broth is made from pork bone and chicken bone simmered with vegetables — an animal-based stock throughout — and there is no plant-based broth or noodle option mentioned in any source. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-19:00 (one source lists 12:00-18:00); closed Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Asakusa, Taitō-ku — near Asakusa Station (Tsukuba Expr
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 来集軒 traces its roots to a noodle-making workshop (製麺所) founded in the Meiji era (around 1910) — the same year as Rairaitei, generally considered Japan's first ramen restaurant. The ramen shop itself opened in 1950 (Showa 25), and has been run as a family, counter-style shop ever

chef_bio.background: 来集軒 traces its roots to a noodle-making workshop (製麺所) founded in the Meiji era (around 1910) — the same year as Rairaitei, generally considered Japan's first ramen restaurant. The ramen shop itself opened in 1950 (Showa 25), and has been run as a family, counter-style shop ever since, attached to its own noodle-making operation.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop prides itself on keeping the same gentle, nostalgic flavor essentially unchanged for over 50 years — a straightforward, old-fashioned Tokyo shoyu ramen rather than a trend-chasing bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: Shoyu ramen (soy sauce ramen): a clear, soy-sauce-based broth simmered each morning from pork bone, chicken bone, and vegetables (onion, carrot, cabbage), paired with flat, medium-thin curly noodles made in-house at the shop's own noodle factory.

anecdotes.text: Described by the walking-magazine さんたつ (Sanpo no Tatsujin) as 'gentle and deeply flavorful — this is truly Japanese ramen,' highlighting the shop's Showa-era atmosphere, walls decorated with celebrity autographs, and its role as a beloved neighborhood gathering spot.
A ramen blogger visiting the shop noted the soup's clean, soy-sauce aroma and the noodles' smooth, wheat-forward texture, calling it classic old-style Tokyo ramen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's broth is a shoyu (soy sauce) base; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, and no wheat-free soy sauce or GF alternative was mentioned in any source.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, old-school, locals' counter ramen shop with a Showa-era atmosphere; no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese ordering (or pointing at the menu) should be expected.

places.name: 甘味みつや (Kanmi Mitsuya)
places.gf_detail: The signature shiratama dango is described as made only from stone-ground shiratamako (glutinous rice flour) and water, and the kuromitsu/shiromitsu syrups are sugar-based (brown sugar, wasanbon, kibi sugar) — no soy sauce, dashi, or tempura appear on this sweets-only menu, so wheat is not an obvious core ingredient. However, this is inferred from published ingredient descriptions rather than a shop statement on gluten-free practice; there is no confirmation of a dedicated wheat-free prep area, and some seasonal toppings or kakigori add-ons were not itemized in available sources. Not verified for celiac-level safety — ask staff before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Core items — rice-flour shiratama dango, the house kuromitsu/shiromitsu cane-sugar syrups, and kanten (agar) jelly — are plant-based per published ingredient descriptions (no dairy, egg, or gelatin mentioned in those). However, anmitsu-style bowls and seasonal kakigori toppings at kanmi shops commonly include dairy add-ons (e.g., condensed milk, ice cream) that were not individually itemized in available sources for this shop's full menu, so a fully vegan order is not guaranteed across every dish. Confirm specific toppings with staff.
places.neighborhood: 浅草（浅草駅）
places.cuisine: 和菓子・かき氷・白玉団子
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kanmi Mitsuya is a small traditional Japanese sweets (kanmi-dokoro) shop in Asakusa that opened in August 2018, specializing in hand-kneaded shiratama dango (rice-flour dumplings) served warm and freshly boiled throughout the day. It became popular enough to draw regular queues,

roles: Kanmi-dokoro (traditional Japanese sweets shop) specializing in shiratama dango
chef_bio.background: Kanmi Mitsuya is a small traditional Japanese sweets (kanmi-dokoro) shop in Asakusa that opened in August 2018, specializing in hand-kneaded shiratama dango (rice-flour dumplings) served warm and freshly boiled throughout the day. It became popular enough to draw regular queues, and in 2022 the owners opened a second, takeout-only branch on Sensoji's Nakamise-dori selling a boxed dango set called 'Shiratama Tebako'.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop is built around two house-made staples: the dango and the syrups. The dumpling dough uses stone-ground shiratamako made from Saga-grown Hiyoku rice and water from springs originating on Mt. Aso; staff hand-knead it fresh each morning, adjusting the water ratio 'by feel' to account for daily shifts in temperature and humidity, then boil it to order rather than pre-making it. The kuromitsu is made by tasting and comparing brown sugars from different islands of Kagoshima and Okinawa before settling on Hateruma-jima brown sugar blended with wasanbon for a lighter syrup, while the shiromitsu uses Kikaijima kibi sugar; both are slow-simmered in copper pots.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly hand-kneaded, just-boiled shiratama dango (soft rice-flour dumplings), served with the shop's own kuromitsu (brown sugar syrup) and shiromitsu (white syrup); also anmitsu, kanten (agar jelly), kuzukiri, and seasonal shaved ice (kakigori) in summer.

anecdotes.text: The shop draws long queues in Asakusa for its freshly boiled, mochi-mochi shiratama dango, and has been featured on Japanese TV programs such as Hiru Nandesu.
In 2022 the operators opened a takeout-only second location on Sensoji's Nakamise-dori shopping street, introducing a boxed dango set ('Shiratama Tebako') as a new way to enjoy the dumplings.

positives.text: Menu is dessert-focused (rice-flour dumplings, sugar syrups, agar jelly) with no soy sauce or dashi-based savory items described, reducing the most common hidden-wheat/hidden-fish risks found in Japanese savory dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, popular local kanmi shop that draws queues of both Japanese and tourist customers, but no English menu or English-language allergy/dietary signage was found in available sources, so expect a Japanese-only ordering experience.

places.name: 竹三郎 (Takesaburo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free option, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation is mentioned in any available source. This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop with a soy-sauce-based (shoyu) tonkotsu tare, which almost certainly contains wheat, plus thick wheat noodles as the signature format (including the abura soba/oil-noodle dishes). Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners based on available information.
places.vegan_detail: No vegetarian or vegan menu option was found in any available source. The restaurant's entire menu is built around pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth and pork-based ramen/abura-soba, with meat as the featured element of its signature dishes. Not suitable for vegan diners based on available information.
places.neighborhood: 花川戸・浅草 (Hanakawado, Asakusa), Taito-ku
places.cuisine: 豚骨ラーメン・アブラソバ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 竹三郎(Takesaburo)は東武線・浅草駅から徒歩2〜3分、馬道通り沿いにある濃厚豚骨（家系）ラーメン店で、2013年12月23日に開業した。浅草寺にほど近い立地で、地元ブログでは「浅草寺にもっとも近い家系」として紹介されている。

roles: ramen shop / 濃厚豚骨・家系ラーメン専門店
chef_bio.origin: Opened December 23, 2013 in Hanakawado, Asakusa, along Umamichi-dori, a short walk from Sensoji.
chef_bio.background: 竹三郎(Takesaburo)は東武線・浅草駅から徒歩2〜3分、馬道通り沿いにある濃厚豚骨（家系）ラーメン店で、2013年12月23日に開業した。 浅草寺にほど近い立地で、地元ブログでは「浅草寺にもっとも近い家系」として紹介されている。
chef_bio.specialty: 満州トロトロ担々汁あり油そば（ゴマだれ入りの担々風あぶらそば）と、同じ具でコチジャンを効かせた辛口の「汁あり油そば 燃えよドラゴン」。 鉄鍋の余熱でスープが煮詰まりソース状になって極太麺に絡むのが特徴。 濃厚豚骨醤油ラーメンも看板メニュー。

anecdotes.text: 浅草寺にもっとも近い家系〜竹三郎＠浅草 (blog post noting the shop's proximity to Sensoji temple)
Rich tonkotsu-shoyu broth described as thick yet mellow, with thick straight noodles that are chewy and smooth.

cultural_comfort.note: Neighborhood-style Japanese ramen counter near Asakusa Station with no confirmed English menu, English signage, or English-speaking staff in any available source, so basic Japanese or pointing at photos may be needed to order.

places.name: うなぎの蒲の穂焼 牛タン 焼鳥 馬刺し いづも 浅草横町 (Izumo (Asakusa Yokocho))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, wheat-ingredient disclosure, or dedicated-fryer information was found. As a traditional unagi/izakaya menu, the eel glaze (tare) and dipping sauces are almost certainly soy-sauce based (wheat-containing). Treat as unsafe for celiac diners absent direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No plant-based or vegan menu items were found. The menu centers on eel, beef tongue, chicken yakitori, and horse sashimi, and dashi/tare bases are unlikely to be free of fish/bonito. This does not appear to be a viable option for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Sources disagree: Asakusa Yokocho's own shop page lists 11:00–22:00 daily; one aggregator lists weekdays 12:00–22:00 / weekends & holidays 12:00–23:00; another lists 12:00–23:00 daily, matching the overall Asakusa Yokocho facility hours (12:00–23:00, last order 22:00).
places.neighborhood: 浅草（浅草横町）
places.notes: いづも is a small unagi (eel) specialty izakaya concept that began at a honten in Osaka's Fukushima district before expanding to a branch inside Osaka Station's LUCUA shopping complex and to this Asakusa Yokocho branch in Tokyo, a festival-themed food hall on the 4th floor of the To

roles: Eel (unagi) specialty izakaya, one of several branches of the いづも eel-skewer concept
chef_bio.origin: Osaka (Fukushima)
chef_bio.background: いづも is a small unagi (eel) specialty izakaya concept that began at a honten in Osaka's Fukushima district before expanding to a branch inside Osaka Station's LUCUA shopping complex and to this Asakusa Yokocho branch in Tokyo, a festival-themed food hall on the 4th floor of the Tokyo Rakutenchi Asakusa Building.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chain's stated aim is to make traditionally pricey, formal unagi more affordable and approachable by serving it izakaya-style in a lively, casual setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Unagi tamadon and unagi gyudon (eel rice bowls with egg or beef), unagi kamahoyaki (semi-grilled eel fillet), and eel kushiyaki skewers, served alongside grilled beef tongue, yakitori, and horse sashimi.

anecdotes.text: The Osaka honten relocated and relaunched with new menu items in 2024, described as an effort to carry Japanese eel food culture forward while keeping it reasonably priced ('大阪一リーズナブルな鰻屋').
The Asakusa branch sits inside 'Asakusa Yokocho,' a food-and-festival themed dining hall built to let visitors experience Japanese festival culture alongside food from multiple genres.

cultural_comfort.note: Sits inside a tourist-oriented "food and festival" dining hall a minute from Asakusa Station with an English Tabelog listing, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found.

places.name: からあげ 縁 浅草総本店 (Karaage Yukari Asakusa Sohonten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free preparation were found anywhere in this shop's materials. The chicken is coated before frying and marinated overnight in a secret tare described as soy-sauce (shoyu) based, which contains wheat; all items are fried in shared equipment. Not suitable for a wheat/gluten allergy or celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-only fried-chicken specialty shop with no vegetarian or plant-based items found in any menu source. The chicken is marinated in a soy-sauce-based tare (no confirmed bonito/katsuo dashi mentioned, but a meat-based product throughout). Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-22:00, open daily (売り切れ次第終了 — closes early if sold out); some older listings state 10:00-22:00
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (near Asakusa ROX / Shin-Nakamise area)
places.notes: Karaage Yukari's Asakusa store is the brand's original flagship ("sohonten"), opened in May 2010 as a takeout-only fried-chicken specialty counter. It grew from this single Asakusa shop into a nationwide chain of roughly 40 locations run by Arcland Service Holdings, the same comp

roles: founder/operator: Arcland Service Holdings Co., Ltd.
chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (opened May 2010)
chef_bio.background: Karaage Yukari's Asakusa store is the brand's original flagship ("sohonten"), opened in May 2010 as a takeout-only fried-chicken specialty counter. It grew from this single Asakusa shop into a nationwide chain of roughly 40 locations run by Arcland Service Holdings, the same company behind the "Karayama" karaage-set-meal chain.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop is named 縁 ("Yukari," meaning bond/connection) to express the wish to cherish every encounter (ご縁) with a customer.
chef_bio.specialty: "Legend Karaage" (伝説のからあげ) — juicy chicken thigh marinated for a full day in a secret soy-sauce-based tare made without any garlic or ginger, then fried fresh to order until crisp and fragrant.

anecdotes.text: The marinade recipe reportedly took about two and a half years of trial and development before the shop opened, with the aim of creating karaage customers would one day call "legendary."

Unlike most karaage recipes, the tare deliberately leaves out garlic and ginger; the shop instead uses its own method for removing gaminess from the meat so the chicken's natural flavor comes through.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chicken is marinated overnight in a secret sauce based on several types of soy sauce (shoyu) before frying, which contains wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A well-known, tourist-friendly takeout counter on Asakusa's Shin-Nakamise-area street facing Asakusa ROX — order by pointing at the photo/case menu and pay at the register, no reservation or Japanese fluency needed.

places.name: ニクノイロ (Niku no Iro)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles (choice of thick or thin, no alternative noodle offered) served in a soy-sauce (shoyu) or salt (shio) tare broth. No gluten-free noodle substitution, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated allergen-safe preparation process was mentioned in any source — not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-ramen specialty shop built around a pork clear (chintan) broth with generous meat toppings central to every menu item (ニクラーメン, 〉得ラーメン). No vegetable-based broth or vegan protein option was found in any menu listing or coverage of the shop.
places.hours_raw: 平日 11:30〜15:00、17:00〜22:00（日曜は11:30〜18:00）、火曜・水曜定休。土曜の単独の記載は見つからず、平日のパターンを適用したと推定。
places.neighborhood: 台東区浅草（浅草地下街） — Asakusa, directly connected to Asakusa Statio
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Niku no Iro (ニクノイロ) opened on May 13, 2026 inside the Asakusa Underground Shopping Street — a retro subterranean arcade directly connected to Exit 6 of the Ginza Line at Asakusa Station, just steps from Kaminarimon.

chef_bio.background: Niku no Iro (ニクノイロ) opened on May 13, 2026 inside the Asakusa Underground Shopping Street — a retro subterranean arcade directly connected to Exit 6 of the Ginza Line at Asakusa Station, just steps from Kaminarimon.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept is volume and clarity — generous meat portions served in a clean-tasting, slowly simmered pork clear broth, with an add-on 』たっぷりニク』 (extra meat) option for those who want even more.
chef_bio.specialty: 名代！ ニクラーメン (Meat Ramen) — a bowl piled with meat in a slow-simmered pork clear (chintan) broth; diners choose shoyu or shio tare and thick or thin noodles

anecdotes.text: The shop opened May 13, 2026 directly connected to the Ginza Line's Exit 6 in the Asakusa underground shopping street, a retro arcade setting near Kaminarimon.
The restaurant's calling card, as covered by local business news, is generous meat portions served in a pork clear (chintan) broth simmered slowly for a clean flavor.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu is built around shoyu (soy sauce) or shio (salt) tare as standard ramen bases; no wheat-free soy sauce or gluten-free broth preparation was mentioned in any source covering the shop.

cultural_comfort.note: A compact 7-seat counter in an underground shopping arcade where you order in Japanese ramen terms (shoyu/shio flavor, thick/thin noodles); no confirmed English menu or signage, so a phrasebook or translation app helps.

places.name: ちゃこーる (Chacole)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, options, or protocol are documented anywhere in available sources. As a traditional charcoal-grilled yakitori restaurant, the tare glazing sauce almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce, and even salt-grilled (shio) skewers are cooked on the same shared charcoal grill as sauce-glazed items, creating a real cross-contact risk. Not recommended for strict celiac/gluten-free needs without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-focused yakitori restaurant built around specialty poultry, quail, and pigeon; no vegan menu, dish, or accommodation has been documented. Seasonal vegetable skewers may be offered but would be grilled on the same shared charcoal grill as meat, and no confirmation exists that broths or sides avoid bonito-based dashi. Not recommended for strict vegans without direct confirmation from staff.
places.neighborhood: 浅草（観音裏）Sensoji近郊
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 2013 down a quiet residential backstreet of Asakusa's Kannon-ura district, ちゃこーる (Chacole) is a small charcoal-grilled yakitori counter run by chef-owner Hisako Takahashi, known by the nickname "Chako." She trained as a grill hand at Mantori (萬鳥), an inventive yakitori

chef_bio.origin: Opened 2013, Asakusa, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Opened in 2013 down a quiet residential backstreet of Asakusa's Kannon-ura district, ちゃこーる (Chacole) is a small charcoal-grilled yakitori counter run by chef-owner Hisako Takahashi, known by the nickname "Chako. " She trained as a grill hand at Mantori (萬鳥), an inventive yakitori offshoot launched by the French restaurant La Chèvre — a lineage food writers say still shows in her style.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unassuming, café-like storefront hiding a serious commitment to sourcing — prized domestic poultry breeds, imported French quail and pigeon for variety, and seasonal vegetables — combined with technique picked up at a French-restaurant-run yakitori spinoff.
chef_bio.specialty: Half-grilled quail (uzura), plus charcoal-grilled French quail and pigeon and Amagi black chicken from Izu

anecdotes.text: The shop's name blends the English word "charcoal" with the owner's nickname, Chako.

Takahashi trained as a grill hand at Mantori (萬鳥), a yakitori shop created by the French restaurant La Chèvre — a background the food magazine dancyu highlighted as giving her yakitori a distinct French inflection.
The signature half-grilled quail is described as a dish that wins over essentially every guest who tries it.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shio (salt) and tare (sauce) skewers are cooked on the same shared charcoal grill; no separation between wheat-sauce-glazed and plain items is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare glazing sauce used on charcoal-grilled yakitori is, per standard preparation, soy-sauce based and therefore contains wheat; no gluten-free tare alternative is documented for this shop.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Any vegetable skewers would be grilled on the same charcoal grill used for chicken, quail, and pigeon.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, locals'-backstreet counter shop with phone-only reservations in Japanese and no evidence of an English menu — best suited to diners comfortable navigating in Japanese or with a local guide/translation help.

places.name: 松竹圓カフェ (Shochiku-en Cafe)
places.gf_detail: The cafe states its entire kitchen is gluten-free and every menu item is gluten-free — no wheat is used anywhere in the food. No specific public statement was found confirming whether a dedicated fryer is used for fried items (e.g., the cafe's pizza) or how soy sauce/seasonings are sourced, though the "completely gluten-free kitchen" claim implies wheat-based soy sauce is avoided by design.
places.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based menu, vegan-certified by NPO Vege Project Japan; no dashi, katsuobushi, egg, dairy, or honey. Signature dish is a vegan version of Taiwanese luròu fàn (braised "pork" rice); other dishes include vegan sweet-and-sour "pork," a mapo tofu bowl, and gapao rice, plus vegan/gluten-free cakes and a chocolate sandwich made with organic ingredients.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat, Sun & holidays: 11:00-20:00 (food L.O. 19:30, drink L.O. 19:30); Wed, Thu: 11:00-15:00 (food L.O. 14:30, drink L.O. 14:30)
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Asakusa / Kappabashi, Asakusa
places.notes: Owner Haruka Iwahashi, who grew up in Taiwan, founded a company focused on Oriental vegetarian cooking in April 2019, then opened Shochiku-en Cafe in Nishi-Asakusa near Kappabashi that December. She wanted to share the appeal of natural, additive-free plant-based cooking — and he

chef_bio.origin: Taiwan (the owner grew up in Taiwan)
chef_bio.background: Owner Haruka Iwahashi, who grew up in Taiwan, founded a company focused on Oriental vegetarian cooking in April 2019, then opened Shochiku-en Cafe in Nishi-Asakusa near Kappabashi that December. She wanted to share the appeal of natural, additive-free plant-based cooking — and her own Taiwanese home cooking — with more people in Japan.
chef_bio.philosophy: To convey the wonder of natural food and vegan cuisine to as many people in Japan as possible, using carefully selected organic, domestic ingredients in a kitchen that excludes both animal products and wheat entirely.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegan Taiwanese luròu fàn (braised-'pork' rice bowl), the cafe's most popular dish, made plant-based in place of the pork used in the traditional version

anecdotes.text: The cafe's signature dish reimagines luròu fàn, the Taiwanese braised pork rice bowl the owner grew up eating, recreated with plant-based ingredients instead of meat.
Iwahashi opened an Oriental-vegetarian company in April 2019 before opening Shochiku-en Cafe in Asakusa that December, aiming to spread appreciation of natural/vegan food in Japan.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The cafe describes its kitchen as completely gluten-free, with no wheat brought into the kitchen at all — every menu item is gluten-free by design rather than by substitution.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The cafe is 100% plant-based across its entire menu and holds vegan certification from NPO Vege Project Japan, with no animal-derived ingredients (including dairy, egg, honey) used anywhere.

positives.text: Because every dish on the menu is both vegan and gluten-free, diners don't need to cross-check individual items for hidden animal or wheat ingredients.
The owner built the cafe specifically around natural, additive-free vegan cooking, using carefully selected organic and domestic ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, Japanese-run neighborhood cafe near Kappabashi's kitchenware street — menu and staff are primarily Japanese-oriented, but the shop is well covered by vegan/gluten-free guide sites and used to visitors seeking plant-based food, so simple English or pointing at photos should get you through.

places.name: ヴィーガン イート トウキョウ (Vegan Eat Tokyo)
places.gf_detail: No source describes gluten-free accommodation, a gluten-free menu, or a dedicated fryer. To the contrary, the restaurant's own most-cited signature dish is "kuruma-fu katsu," a breaded, fried cutlet made from kuruma-fu — a wheat-gluten product — meaning wheat gluten is a headline ingredient rather than something avoided. Another signature, mushroom carbonara, implies conventional wheat pasta. No information was found on whether soy sauce used is wheat-free or on any cross-contamination protocols. This is not a suitable choice for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Reported as a fully plant-based, French-chef-supervised menu (organic beer, wine, and sake also offered), recognized/certified by NPO Veg Project Japan. Signature items include lotus-root burger, chickpea and tomato stew, vegan curry, mushroom carbonara, and kuruma-fu katsu — no meat, fish, dairy, or egg reported anywhere in sourced descriptions. No specific statement was found confirming absence of hidden bonito/katsuo dashi, but given the restaurant's vegan-certification and full plant-based positioning, this is a lower-risk concern than at a typical omnivore restaurant offering "vegan options."
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:45-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / Dinner 17:00-21:45 (L.O. 21:00), reported as daily through 2024; sources from 2025 note a shift to irregular closures announced on Instagram
places.neighborhood: Asakusa, Taitō-ku (near Asakusa Station)
places.notes: Vegan Eat Tokyo opened on February 3, 2024 in Asakusa, in a space that previously housed a kimono shop. The restaurant's plant-based menu is supervised by a French chef, blending French technique with an Italian-leaning, fully vegan menu using organic ingredients. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Vegan Eat Tokyo opened on February 3, 2024 in Asakusa, in a space that previously housed a kimono shop. The restaurant's plant-based menu is supervised by a French chef, blending French technique with an Italian-leaning, fully vegan menu using organic ingredients.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant positions itself as proof that vegan food can be indulgent and French/Italian-inspired rather than ascetic, aiming to win over both vegan and non-vegan diners (including many international visitors) with familiar comfort-food formats reimagined as 100% plant-based.
chef_bio.specialty: Kuruma-fu katsu (breaded and fried cutlet made from kuruma-fu, a traditional wheat-gluten "wheel-shaped" fu) and smoked mushroom vegan carbonara are the most frequently cited signature dishes, alongside a lotus-root burger, chickpea and tomato stew, and vegan curry.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant opened February 3, 2024 in a location that was formerly a kimono shop, and has since drawn a large international clientele, reportedly over 1,300 visitors a month via the HappyCow app.
A food-industry equipment trade report profiled the shop's opening and kitchen setup as case #48 in its restaurant report series.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Described in multiple listings as a fully vegan kitchen (French-chef-supervised, organic, plant-based menu) with no animal products on the menu, which by design limits vegan cross-contact risk, though no explicit cross-contact protocol statement was found.

positives.text: Listed as a certified vegan restaurant recognized by NPO Veg Project Japan, and highly rated (around 4.7/5 on Google Maps as of late 2024) by a large volume of international visitors via the HappyCow app.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant is well used to international/vegan-traveler visitors (cited as drawing over a thousand foreign visitors a month via HappyCow) but no explicit mention of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found in available sources.

places.name: 浅草むぎとろ 本店 (Asakusa Mugitoro (Honten))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated fryer was found in any available source. This is a traditional Japanese restaurant relying on conventional soy sauce and dashi seasonings and offering tempura-style dishes on some menus, so wheat exposure and cross-contamination should be assumed likely. Diners with celiac disease or severe wheat allergy should not treat this as a safe option without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant's signature seasoning is a secret dashi (broth) based on bonito flakes (katsuobushi) and kelp, used since the 1929 founding, so the core mugitoro dish and much of the kaiseki menu are not vegan. No vegan-specific menu, substitutions, or plant-based dashi option were found in available sources.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Kaminarimon, by the Sumida River, near Sensō-ji)
places.notes: Founded on June 10, 1929 (Shōwa 4) beside the Sumida River near the Kaminarimon/Komagata-bashi area of Asakusa. According to the company's own history, founder Tazō Nakajima had worked for a produce (vegetable/fruit) wholesaler and wanted to start a food business of his own; he i

chef_bio.background: Founded on June 10, 1929 (Shōwa 4) beside the Sumida River near the Kaminarimon/Komagata-bashi area of Asakusa. According to the company's own history, founder Tazō Nakajima had worked for a produce (vegetable/fruit) wholesaler and wanted to start a food business of his own; he is said to have traveled the country searching for the best mountain yam before settling on a strain and opening a shop built entirely around tororo (grated yam). The restaurant has been run by the same family and stayed on essentially the same stretch of riverbank for nearly a century, expanding over the decades into a multi-floor kaiseki restaurant while keeping the original tororo recipe at its core.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's founding wish was simply 'we want everyone to be able to eat one bowl of mugitoro' — a focus on making a single humble, health-associated dish available to ordinary diners, which still shapes the menu today.
chef_bio.specialty: Mugitoro (麦とろ) — barley-mixed rice topped with tororo (grated Japanese mountain yam, made from a strong, low-astringency yamato-imo), seasoned with a secret bonito-and-kelp dashi recipe passed down since the 1929 founding. The restaurant also serves broader tororo-kaiseki courses and a 'guro-don' (fatty tuna) rice bowl.

anecdotes.text: Founder Tazō Nakajima worked at a produce company and, wanting to go into a food business of his own, is said to have traveled the country before finding what he considered the finest mountain yam — the discovery that became the basis for the restaurant.
The shop hung its noren for the first time on June 10, 1929, at the foot of the Sumida River, with the founding wish that 'everyone could eat a single bowl of mugitoro.'

cultural_comfort.note: A long-running, high-traffic tourist destination steps from Sensō-ji with an English-language site/menu and English-language press coverage, so English-speaking visitors should find it easy to navigate even without Japanese.

places.name: 権八 浅草吾妻橋 (Gonpachi Asakusa Azumabashi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is advertised at this location. The soba dipping sauce (tsuyu) and yakitori tare are soy-sauce based (soy sauce traditionally contains wheat), tempura here is battered in wheat flour, and there is no evidence of a dedicated gluten-free soba option or a dedicated fryer. Celiac diners should not consider this restaurant safe without in-person confirmation (in Japanese) from staff, and even then cross-contact risk in a shared kitchen serving soba, tempura, and yakitori is high.
places.vegan_detail: Gonpachi's dedicated vegan menu (vegan nigiri, vegan tempura rice bowl, etc.) has only been confirmed at the group's Ginza (G-ZONE), Nishi-Azabu, and Shibuya branches — not at this Asakusa Azumabashi location. Here, vegetable tempura and vegetable-filled hand-rolls (temaki) could work as a starting point, but the soba tsuyu and most sauces at Gonpachi locations are traditionally dashi-based (bonito/katsuobushi), so a fully vegan meal is not guaranteed without asking staff directly and being prepared for limited options.
places.hours_raw: Daily, including weekends and holidays: 11:30-23:00 (food last order 22:00, drinks last order 22:30)
places.neighborhood: Asakusa / Azumabashi, Taito-ku (riverside, at the foot of Az
places.cuisine: Izakaya / Yakitori / hand-made soba with Sum
places.notes: Gonpachi Asakusa Azumabashi opened in January 2017 as one of the newer branches of the long-running Gonpachi izakaya group. It sits in a quiet spot on the bank of the Sumida River at the foot of Azumabashi bridge, about a 30-second walk from Exit 4 of Asakusa Station (Ginza Line) · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Gonpachi Asakusa Azumabashi opened in January 2017 as one of the newer branches of the long-running Gonpachi izakaya group. It sits in a quiet spot on the bank of the Sumida River at the foot of Azumabashi bridge, about a 30-second walk from Exit 4 of Asakusa Station (Ginza Line) and roughly a minute from the Toei Asakusa Line exit.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made soba — buckwheat that is stone-ground fresh each morning and hand-cut in house — alongside binchotan-charcoal-grilled yakitori skewers and hand-rolled temaki sushi made with premium nori.

anecdotes.text: The first floor is built around a communal counter cut from a single piece of kusu (camphor) wood from Kyushu, said to be around 200 years old, while the second floor has a U-shaped ash-wood counter; large windows on both floors look straight onto the Sumida River, giving a completely different mood by day and by night.

cultural_comfort.note: It's part of a well-known Japanese restaurant group in the heart of tourist-heavy Asakusa, so some visitor-friendly accommodation is likely, but English menus/staff for this specific branch were not directly confirmed in available sources.

places.name: 丹想庵 健次郎 (Tansouan Kenjiro)
places.gf_detail: The kitchen hand-makes two soba styles fresh daily — a nihachi noodle (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat flour) and an inaka (country-style) noodle — both of which contain wheat flour by recipe. No dedicated 100%-buckwheat (juwari) or gluten-free noodle option was found in any source, and both noodle types are almost certainly boiled in shared water. This is not a safe choice for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional soba-and-sake restaurant offering tempura and other seasonal side dishes alongside the noodles. No vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or bonito-free broth option was found in any source, and traditional soba kaeshi/dashi in shops of this style is typically bonito-based. Not confirmed safe or accommodating for vegan diners without direct inquiry.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Kannon-ura), Taito-ku — near Tsukuba Express Asakus
places.notes: Suzuki apprenticed in soba-making at a temple in Tendo, Yamagata, before opening his own shop behind Sensoji's Kannon Hall in Asakusa's quiet Kannon-ura backstreets. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Trained at a soba-focused Buddhist temple in Tendo City, Yamagata Prefecture — one of northeastern Japan's most celebrated buckwheat-growing regions.
chef_bio.background: Suzuki apprenticed in soba-making at a temple in Tendo, Yamagata, before opening his own shop behind Sensoji's Kannon Hall in Asakusa's quiet Kannon-ura backstreets.
chef_bio.philosophy: Suzuki insists on sourcing the season's best domestic raw buckwheat groats and stone-grinding them by hand through the entire process, believing that freshly milled flour is what gives the noodles their aroma and character.
chef_bio.specialty: Two hand-made soba served fresh daily: a delicate nihachi noodle (80% buckwheat, 20% wheat flour) prized for its faint aroma and smooth finish, and a heartier inaka (country-style) noodle milled for a deeper buckwheat fragrance and firmer bite.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name, Tansouan (丹想庵), was given to Suzuki by the head priest of the soba temple in Tendo, Yamagata where he trained; '丹想' points to sensing one's feelings from the body's core.
The kitchen grinds top-grade domestic buckwheat with a stone mill and hand-cuts two distinct soba styles fresh every day, pairing them with a range of sake and seasonal accompaniments.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Two soba noodle types are hand-cut fresh each day — a nihachi (80% buckwheat/20% wheat) and an inaka (country-style) noodle — both containing wheat by recipe; no gluten-free or 100%-buckwheat noodle was found offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, Japanese-oriented soba-and-sake restaurant in a quiet Asakusa backstreet; no English-language service was confirmed, so a translation app and some patience with a Japanese-only menu are recommended, though phone/Tabelog reservations are available.

places.name: 浅草鳩家 ヴィーガンフルーツサンド (Asakusa Hatoya Vegan Fruits Sand)
places.gf_detail: The bread is a standard soft white bread (soy milk is used to replace dairy/egg, not to make it gluten-free) and no source reviewed confirmed a gluten-free bread option or any allergen/celiac protocol. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free needs without calling ahead to confirm directly.
places.vegan_detail: Dedicated all-vegan fruit-sandwich specialty stall: no egg or dairy anywhere on the menu, with both the bread and the whipped cream made from soy milk. No dashi, katsuo/bonito, or other hidden animal ingredients are mentioned in any source (the menu is fruit sandwiches only, not a savory/dashi-based menu).
places.neighborhood: 浅草 伝法院通り
places.cuisine: ヴィーガンフルーツサンド・サンドイッチ
places.notes: A small specialty takeout counter on Denpo-in-dori, the lantern-lined shopping street that leads to the gates of Sensoji Temple in Asakusa, dedicated entirely to fruit sandwiches (furutsu sando) made without any egg or dairy. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A small specialty takeout counter on Denpo-in-dori, the lantern-lined shopping street that leads to the gates of Sensoji Temple in Asakusa, dedicated entirely to fruit sandwiches (furutsu sando) made without any egg or dairy.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's premise is that a fruit sandwich does not need dairy cream or egg to taste rich: both the bread and the whipped cream are made from soy milk instead, so vegans and people with egg or milk allergies can eat the same treat as everyone else strolling past the temple.
chef_bio.specialty: Fruit sandwiches piled generously with seasonal fresh fruit (strawberry, kiwi, mandarin orange, and other seasonal fruit) in soy-milk whipped cream between soft white bread, priced from about 600 yen.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the sandwiches as packed full rather than skimpy — whole large strawberries and kiwi tucked inside as well as arranged on top — calling it 'nothing like the ordinary fruit sandwiches that go easy on the filling.'
The stall sits immediately beside the entrance to Sensoji Temple on Denpo-in-dori (on the left just after passing through Kaminarimon), making it a popular walk-and-eat stop for temple visitors.
The shop has run a plant-based-egg collaboration menu with another maker, described in local food press as a well-known Asakusa vegan fruit-sandwich specialist.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source reviewed confirms a gluten-free bread option; the standard bread appears to be conventional wheat bread with only the dairy/egg removed, not the gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop's stated selling point is that it uses no egg or dairy anywhere in its bread or cream, since it is a dedicated all-vegan fruit-sandwich specialty stall (not a mixed menu alongside animal-based items).

positives.text: Both the bread and the whipped cream are made from soy milk instead of dairy and egg, so the shop is positioned as safe to eat for vegans and for people with egg or milk allergies.
