places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yadoroku was opened in Showa 29 (1954) just behind Sensō-ji's main hall by the grandmother of the current owner, who started selling rice balls to support her family after her husband was out of work. It has been run by the same family for three generations and is widely cited as · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — operating on the same spot since 1954
chef_bio.background: Yadoroku was opened in Showa 29 (1954) just behind Sensō-ji's main hall by the grandmother of the current owner, who started selling rice balls to support her family after her husband was out of work. It has been run by the same family for three generations and is widely cited as Tokyo's oldest surviving onigiri specialty shop. The current owner, Yosuke Miura, is the third-generation proprietor.
chef_bio.philosophy: Every November when the new rice crop comes in, Miura tastes around 20 varieties and commits to a single one for the whole year (currently Niigata-grown Koshihikari). The rice is still cooked the way it was in 1954 — in traditional iron pots, in small 1. 5-cup batches, four times a day — and Miura has studied academic research on starch and enzyme chemistry to refine the cooking process while keeping the shop's original character.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-shaped onigiri built around a single, carefully chosen rice and roughly 20 traditional fillings — the shop's own most-popular picks are salted salmon, tarako (cod roe), and ami (tiny dried shrimp simmered in a savory sauce).

anecdotes.text: The shop's name plays on a double meaning: Asakusa is just the neighborhood, but 'roku' echoes 'rokudenashi' (good-for-nothing) — a nod to the out-of-work husband the founder, Miura's grandmother, opened the shop in 1954 to support.
Third-generation owner Yosuke Miura tastes about 20 rice varieties every November when the new crop arrives, then selects a single type to use for the entire year.
The rice is still cooked in traditional iron pots, in small batches four times a day, the same method used since the shop opened in 1954.

Onigiri Asakusa Yadoroku became the first onigiri specialist ever awarded a Michelin Bib Gourmand, starting with the 2019 Tokyo guide, and remains the only onigiri shop with a Michelin distinction in Tokyo.

positives.text: An English menu with photos is available, and the shop regularly serves foreign visitors, making it easier to identify and ask about individual fillings.

cultural_comfort.note: An English-language, photo-illustrated menu is available and the shop is very used to foreign visitors, but there's no reservation system — you queue outside and are called in by order of arrival, so basic patience (and sometimes a real wait) is part of the experience.

places.name: 浅草もんじゃ 風鈴 (Asakusa Monja Furin)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, rice-flour batter option, or allergy-specific process is mentioned in any listing found. Both okonomiyaki and monjayaki here use a standard wheat-flour (komugiko) batter, and the monjayaki broth is a chicken-bone-and-dashi base, which in Japanese kitchens typically involves soy sauce (contains wheat) and other seasonings that are not gluten-free by default. No dedicated fryer, gluten-free menu, or celiac-safe process is documented, so this is not a safe choice for celiac or strict gluten-free diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: Menu descriptions center on a homemade chicken-bone broth (鶏ガラ) combined with Japanese dashi for the monjayaki base, plus signature items like mentaiko (cod roe) mochi-cheese monja and seafood monja — none of these are vegan. No vegetarian or vegan menu option, plant-based dashi, or ingredient substitution is documented in any source found, so this is not a suitable choice for vegan diners as currently listed.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa — Shin-Nakamise-dori, near Asakusa Station
places.cuisine: もんじゃ焼き・お好み焼き
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in November 2023 on Asakusa's Shin-Nakamise shopping street, a few minutes' walk from Asakusa Station; the monjayaki base is a homemade chicken-bone broth combined with Japanese dashi, and the okonomiyaki batter is made to be light and fluffy inside with a crisp exterior. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: staff cook monjayaki tableside for guests
chef_bio.background: Opened in November 2023 on Asakusa's Shin-Nakamise shopping street, a few minutes' walk from Asakusa Station; the monjayaki base is a homemade chicken-bone broth combined with Japanese dashi, and the okonomiyaki batter is made to be light and fluffy inside with a crisp exterior.
chef_bio.specialty: Mentaiko mochi-cheese monjayaki (明太餅チーズもんじゃ), described in visitor accounts as the shop's most popular dish

anecdotes.text: A food writer covering the November 2023 opening on Asakusa's Shin-Nakamise-dori highlighted the staff's lightning-fast spatula technique, cooking monjayaki right in front of guests at the table.

A December 2024 visitor trying monjayaki for the first time ordered the mentaiko mochi-cheese monja and a seafood monja, found the mentaiko mochi-cheese dish lived up to its reputation as the most popular item, and enjoyed watching the texture change as the broth reduced on the griddle over time.

cultural_comfort.note: Listings for this shop are all in Japanese with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service, so bringing a translation app or key Japanese phrases will help, especially when explaining dietary restrictions.

places.name: らぁめん めん○（めんまる） (Raamen Men-maru)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) based chicken-bone broth are used; no source mentions gluten-free noodles, a dedicated fryer, or any wheat-free accommodation. Not a safe choice for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is chicken-bone (torigara) based and standard toppings include pork chashu; no vegetarian, vegan, or plant-based broth option is mentioned in any available source.
places.hours_raw: Sources disagree: one blog reports weekdays 10:00–16:00 and weekends 9:00–18:00 with no fixed closed day; another older mention says opening around 11:00 with Wednesdays closed. Exact current hours could not be confirmed this session — call ahead or check posted signage.
places.neighborhood: 浅草・ひさご通り (Asakusa, Hisago-dori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A retro, no-frills stand-up ramen stall right at the entrance to Asakusa's Hisago-dori shopping street, a few minutes' walk from Sensō-ji. It's known locally as one of the cheapest bowls of ramen in central Tokyo, drawing both neighborhood regulars and curious visitors exploring  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A retro, no-frills stand-up ramen stall right at the entrance to Asakusa's Hisago-dori shopping street, a few minutes' walk from Sensō-ji. It's known locally as one of the cheapest bowls of ramen in central Tokyo, drawing both neighborhood regulars and curious visitors exploring the old shopping arcade.
chef_bio.specialty: Shoyu ramen: a clear, chicken-bone (torigara) soy-sauce broth with soft medium-curly noodles, chashu pork, menma, nori, bean sprouts and scallion

anecdotes.text: A basic bowl runs about 350–400 yen and a version topped with a seasoned soft-boiled egg is about 470 yen — startlingly cheap for the Asakusa tourist district.
Described as a classic tachigui (stand-and-eat) counter with a couple of outdoor tables for those who want to sit, right by the Hisago-dori entrance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a clear soy-sauce (shoyu) base on a chicken-bone stock, made with standard wheat-based soy sauce and wheat noodles; no gluten-free alternative is mentioned in any available source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-bone (torigara) based and bowls come topped with pork chashu; no vegetarian or vegan broth option is mentioned in any available source.

cultural_comfort.note: The menu is essentially just one or two ramen variations at clearly posted prices, so pointing and paying works fine without Japanese — but don't expect an English menu, English-speaking staff, or seating comfort beyond a standing counter and a couple of outdoor tables.

places.name: 大黒家天麩羅 (Daikokuya Tempura)
places.gf_detail: Traditional Edo-style tempura restaurant: tempura is coated in a standard wheat-flour batter and deep-fried in shared sesame oil alongside shellfish, and the proprietary dipping tsuyu is described as a soy-sauce-based sauce (ordinary Japanese shoyu contains wheat). No gluten-free batter, wheat-free soy sauce, allergen-free fryer, or any celiac/gluten-free accommodation is mentioned by the restaurant or in any source reviewed. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The house specialty is deep-fried shrimp tempura (ebi tendon), and the menu is built around seafood tempura. Vegetable tempura/kakiage exists on the menu but shares the frying oil with shrimp and other seafood, and no information confirms whether the tsuyu sauce or any broth is free of bonito/fish-derived dashi. No vegan menu, labeling, or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:10-20:30 (Mon-Fri, Sun); 11:10-21:00 (Sat & holidays); open every day, no regular closed days
places.neighborhood: 浅草（浅草1丁目）
places.notes: The shop's founder, Maruyama Hiromasa, was a Kyoto court samurai (公家侍) in the late Edo period. When the Meiji Emperor relocated from Kyoto to Tokyo, Hiromasa came along in service protecting court nobles, and worked as a secretary at the Asakusa residence of court noble Sanjō San · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founded in 1887 (Meiji 20) on Denbōin-dori in Asakusa, originally as a soba noodle shop
chef_bio.background: The shop's founder, Maruyama Hiromasa, was a Kyoto court samurai (公家侍) in the late Edo period. When the Meiji Emperor relocated from Kyoto to Tokyo, Hiromasa came along in service protecting court nobles, and worked as a secretary at the Asakusa residence of court noble Sanjō Sanetomi. He then married into the Daikokuya soba-shop family and was adopted into it, beginning the family's restaurant lineage. Around the end of the Meiji era (c. 1910) the shop switched from soba to tempura, and the name changed from Daikokuya (大黒屋) to Daikokuya (大黒家). It has been passed down through five generations of the same family to the present-day owner.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserving the same Edo-style tempura technique, the secret tsuyu recipe, and the atmosphere of old Asakusa across five generations of family ownership.
chef_bio.specialty: Ebi tendon (海老天丼) — a rice bowl topped with four large shrimp tempura pieces, deep-fried in sesame oil and dipped in the shop's proprietary dark, sweet-savory tsuyu sauce, a recipe handed down through the generations and traditionally prepared only by the head of the family.

anecdotes.text: Founder Maruyama Hiromasa was a Kyoto court samurai who came to Tokyo during the Meiji Restoration while protecting court nobles, worked as a secretary for the noble Sanjō Sanetomi in northern Asakusa, and then married into and was adopted by the Daikokuya soba-shop family, beginning the restaurant's history.

The restaurant opened in 1887 as a soba shop on Denbōin-dori and converted to a tempura specialist around the end of the Meiji era, changing its characters from 大黒屋 to 大黒家; it is now run by the fifth generation.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant fries all tempura — shrimp, fish, and vegetable/kakiage items — in a shared sesame-oil fryer at a single tempura station; no allergen-segregated or dedicated gluten-free fryer is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tsuyu dipping sauce is described as a dark, concentrated, soy-sauce-based sauce; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat and no wheat-free (tamari) alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable tempura/kakiage is fried in the same oil and at the same station as shrimp and other seafood tempura, so cross-contact with shellfish is likely for any vegetable items ordered.

cultural_comfort.note: Sits in the heart of tourist Asakusa on Denbōin-dori steps from Sensōji, with an English-language Tabelog listing, but the restaurant is cash-only and no English menu or English-speaking-staff guarantee was found, so bring some basic Japanese or pointing/translation-app patience.

places.name: 御膳蕎麦 雷門田川 (Gozen Soba Kaminarimon Tagawa)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free noodle preparation were mentioned by any source. This is a traditional soba/udon shop — udon is wheat-based, and the "gozen-soba/sarashina" specialty, while using a fine buckwheat cut, is not advertised anywhere as 十割 (100% buckwheat); traditional tsuyu dipping sauce made with regular shoyu (which contains wheat) should be assumed. Not suitable for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan-adapted preparation was mentioned by any source. As a traditional soba/udon shop, the dipping sauce (tsuyu) and broths should be assumed to use bonito (katsuobushi) dashi. Not suitable for vegan diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri: 11:00–15:30 (L.O. 15:00), 16:30–20:00 (L.O. 19:30). Sat/Sun/Holidays: 10:30–19:00 (L.O. 18:30). Closed Tuesdays and the 3rd Wednesday of each month.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (30 seconds from Sensoji's Kaminarimon Gate)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop opened in 1965 on the little lane just off Kaminarimon Gate and has served Asakusa's temple-district crowds — neighborhood regulars as well as visitors passing under the great lantern — for six decades. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — opened in 1965 a few dozen steps from Sensoji's Kaminarimon Gate.
chef_bio.background: The shop opened in 1965 on the little lane just off Kaminarimon Gate and has served Asakusa's temple-district crowds — neighborhood regulars as well as visitors passing under the great lantern — for six decades.
chef_bio.specialty: Gozen-soba (also called sarashina soba): noodles hand-kneaded with hot water using only the pale, starchy center of the buckwheat kernel, prized for their light color, delicate aroma, and smooth texture. The house's 'yonshoku' (four-color) assortment pairs classic sarashina with sesame-, matcha-, and seasonally flavored noodles on one tray.

anecdotes.text: Gozen-soba (sarashina) is made from only the innermost part of the buckwheat groat; because this flour is high in starch and does not bind easily with cold water, it is hand-worked with hot water, giving the noodles their characteristic pale color and refined texture.
Located about 30 seconds' walk from Sensoji's Kaminarimon Gate and about 2 minutes from Ginza Line Asakusa Station; open since 1965.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu was confirmed by any source, but the shop sits directly on the main tourist path 30 seconds from Kaminarimon Gate, so basic greetings and pointing at a Japanese menu should suffice.

places.name: 鳥なお (Torinao)
places.gf_detail: This is an omakase-only yakitori counter with no menu substitutions and no gluten-free procedures mentioned in any source found. Yakitori tare (the dipping sauce used on many skewers) is typically soy-sauce based and therefore contains wheat, and the popular tsukune (chicken meatballs) commonly uses breadcrumb-style binders in this style of cooking — neither has been confirmed allergen-free here. Because the course is entirely chef's-choice with no listed way to opt out of sauced items, this should not be considered celiac-safe without a direct, in-Japanese conversation with the restaurant beforehand.
places.vegan_detail: This is a chicken-yakitori specialist built entirely around an omakase course of charcoal-grilled chicken skewers and related dishes (tsukune, raw egg yolk, chicken skin). No vegetarian or vegan menu, substitution, or plant-based option was mentioned in any source found.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Fri: 17:30-24:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drink L.O. 23:00); Sat/Sun/holidays: 17:00-23:00 (food L.O. 22:30, drink L.O. 22:30); closed Mondays. One source lists weekend opening as 17:30 rather than 17:00.
places.neighborhood: Tawaramachi / Kaminarimon, Asakusa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This is an omakase-only yakitori counter with no menu substitutions and no gluten-free procedures mentioned in any source found. Yakitori tare (the dipping sauce used on many skewers) is typically soy-sauce based and therefore contains wheat, and the popular tsukune (chicken meat · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen relies on the clean, low-smoke heat of Kishu binchotan charcoal to let the quality of the Koshu Kenmidori chicken speak for itself, basting each skewer in its own rendered fat as it grills for concentrated flavor and aroma.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled skewers of Koshu Kenmidori-brand chicken over Kishu binchotan, especially the signature tsukune (chicken meatballs), plus counter dishes like u-tama (raw egg yolk) and kawa-ponzu (charcoal-grilled chicken skin with ponzu)

anecdotes.text: The restaurant was selected for the Bib Gourmand in the Michelin Guide Tokyo 2022, one of ten Tokyo yakitori spots to receive the distinction.
Regulars single out the tsukune for its notably fluffy, soft texture, and describe the room as a small, homey counter of about 12-14 seats with an intimate, unpretentious atmosphere.
It is noted as a convenient hidden-gem yakitori spot near Tawaramachi Station that takes same-day reservations.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, Japanese-language backstreet counter running an omakase-only course, with no confirmed English menu or signage — book ahead (reservations are effectively required for the small counter) and consider a translation app or local help when calling or ordering.

places.name: 十和田 メトロ通り店（手打ちそば十和田） (Teuchi Soba Towada (Metro-dori Branch))
places.gf_detail: No source found mentions gluten-free options, a dedicated fryer, or celiac accommodations. This is a traditional handmade-soba and tempura shop; the flour blend ratio (100% buckwheat vs. wheat-blended) for the noodles isn't stated in any source found, tempura batter is wheat-based, and the tsuyu dipping sauce is soy-sauce based (soy sauce traditionally contains wheat) per the shop's own description of its bonito-based, MSG-free tsuyu. Not recommended for celiac diners without calling ahead to confirm.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or substitutions are mentioned in any source found. The shop's signature tsuyu (dipping sauce) is explicitly made with real bonito (katsuobushi) dashi, not a plant-based stock, so the standard soba here is not vegan or vegetarian as served.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa
places.notes: Towada is part of an old Asakusa soba lineage associated with the Tominaga family. A frequently cited figure, okami (proprietress) Tomoe Tominaga, is remembered locally for helping lead Asakusa's revival after visitor traffic declined following the 1964 Tokyo Olympics — founding  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: The Towada name traces back to roots as far as Meiji 17 (1884) on Asakusa's Nakamise-dori; accounts differ on the exact modern founding (one source ties it to a Taisho-era (1924) confectionery, Kikusuido, that later became a soba shop; another gives Showa 44/1969). What's consistent across sources is that it is a long-running, family-rooted Asakusa soba house with more than one branch, including this Metro-dori location.
chef_bio.background: Towada is part of an old Asakusa soba lineage associated with the Tominaga family. A frequently cited figure, okami (proprietress) Tomoe Tominaga, is remembered locally for helping lead Asakusa's revival after visitor traffic declined following the 1964 Tokyo Olympics — founding the Asakusa Okamisan-kai (proprietresses' association) and helping launch the Asakusa Samba Carnival. The shop's soba is handmade by craftsman Kawamura Takashi, who has spent 28 years training and working as a soba artisan and is now described as 'the face of Towada. '
chef_bio.philosophy: Towada sources its buckwheat (Nanbu soba flour) from contract farms in the mountains around Lake Towada, straddling Aomori/Akita/Iwate, with flour delivered fresh to Tokyo daily. The tsuyu dipping sauce is made in-house using only bonito (katsuobushi) flakes, with no MSG or chemical additives, and the shop uses spring water drawn from the Ryusendo limestone cave in Iwate's Iwaizumi. Other ingredients are sourced directly from Tohoku producers — Tono-area vegetables, Aomori duck, Akita hinai-jidori chicken.
chef_bio.specialty: Kuretsubo soba (暮坪そば) — soba noodles served with grated kuretsubo kabu, a rare, sharply pungent mountain turnip from the Kuretsubo district of Tono, Iwate, used in place of wasabi as a condiment. It's cited repeatedly as a dish essentially unique to this shop in Tokyo.

anecdotes.text: Chef Kawamura Takashi has been a soba craftsman, including his apprenticeship, for 28 years and is now the public face of the Towada shop in Asakusa.

Okami Tomoe Tominaga is remembered as a 'legendary proprietress' who helped revive Asakusa after the 1964 Olympics diverted tourism away from the neighborhood, founding the Asakusa Okamisan-kai and helping start the Asakusa Samba Carnival.
The shop's roots are described as tracing back to a Meiji 17 (1884) establishment on Nakamise-dori.
Kuretsubo soba, topped with a rare mountain turnip sourced directly from Tono, Iwate, is called a dish you can only get at this shop in Tokyo.

positives.text: The tsuyu (dipping sauce) is made fresh in-house daily using only real bonito flakes, with no chemical seasonings or additives — a point of pride for flavor quality, though it is fish-based, not vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: A well-known, long-established soba shop two minutes from Asakusa Station that regularly serves tourists, but no source confirms an English or picture menu, so basic Japanese or pointing may be needed to ask about ingredients.

places.name: 粥や佐藤 (Kayuya Sato (Kayu-ya Sato))
places.gf_detail: The congee itself is rice-based, but the standard sets are served with steamed dumplings/dim sum (点心) made with wheat wrappers, and no source mentions a wheat-free soy sauce, dedicated gluten-free prep area, or any formal gluten-free protocol. This is a small 8-seat open counter kitchen where wheat-containing items are prepared alongside everything else, so cross-contact should be assumed. Celiac diners should call ahead (03-6700-9888) to ask specifically about the dashi ingredients and dumpling handling rather than assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: The signature dashi that forms the base of every bowl of congee is made from niboshi (dried sardines), chicken, katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and dried shrimp — all animal-derived — and no source mentions a plant-based broth alternative. Steamed side dishes (dumplings/dim sum) also appear to use conventional, non-vegan fillings. There is no indication this restaurant can accommodate a vegan diner.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30), midday break 15:30-16:30; closed Sunday, Monday, Tuesday
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Kannonura, behind Sensoji), Taito-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner, Mr. Sato, tasted a mushroom-and-pork congee (三娘香菇肉粥) while in Taipei and was so struck by its flavor that he spent years testing his own recipe at home before opening a dedicated porridge shop. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Opened in Musashi-Kosugi (Kawasaki) in December 2018; relocated to Asakusa's 'Kannonura' backstreet (behind Sensoji) in March 2024 after five years at the original location.
chef_bio.background: The owner, Mr. Sato, tasted a mushroom-and-pork congee (三娘香菇肉粥) while in Taipei and was so struck by its flavor that he spent years testing his own recipe at home before opening a dedicated porridge shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: A belief that a MSG-free, painstakingly made natural dashi is the soul of a good bowl of congee; the menu centers on two evolving porridge sets rather than an extensive menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Taiwanese-style rice porridge (congee) simmered in a self-made 'natural dashi' built from kombu, dried shiitake mushroom, niboshi (dried sardines), chicken, katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and dried shrimp, served as set menus with small steamed side dishes.

anecdotes.text: Owner Sato first tasted a mushroom-and-pork congee (三娘香菇肉粥) in Taipei and, unable to forget the flavor, repeatedly tested his own recipe before opening a porridge specialty shop.
The shop opened in Musashi-Kosugi in December 2018 and relocated to Asakusa's Kannonura backstreet in March 2024 after five years at the original site.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny 8-seat counter shop on a quiet backstreet behind Sensoji with no confirmed English menu or signage, so visitors should expect a Japanese-only ordering experience, cash-only payment, and a possible wait at busy times.

places.name: 並木藪蕎麦 (Namiki Yabu Soba)
places.gf_detail: The soba noodles themselves are juwari (十割) — hand-cut from 100% buckwheat flour milled fresh each day, with no wheat used as a binder in the dough. However, the tsuyu dipping sauce is built from a traditional kaeshi (soy sauce, mirin, sugar) using standard Japanese shoyu, which is wheat-based, and this sauce is what the noodles are dipped in for every order. The menu also includes tempura dishes (tenzaru, ten-nuki) made with wheat-flour batter, prepared in the same kitchen. No gluten-free soy sauce alternative, dedicated fryer, or celiac protocol was found in any source. One visitor guide explicitly flags this: standard Japanese soy sauce is not wheat-free, and celiac travelers are advised to confirm ingredients with staff directly rather than assume the 100%-buckwheat noodles make the full meal safe.
places.vegan_detail: The tsuyu (dipping sauce) used for cold soba, and the broths used for hot noodle dishes, are built on a katsuobushi (bonito) dashi combined with a soy-sauce-based kaeshi; this fish stock underlies essentially every standard order on the menu. No vegetarian- or vegan-adapted broth, or plant-based dashi alternative, was mentioned in any source found, and the seasonal specialty (kamo nanban) is duck-based. This is a traditional Edo-style soba shop built around its bonito-based tsuyu, not a place with vegan accommodation.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-19:30 (closed Wednesdays and Thursdays)
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Kaminarimon / Namiki-dōri), Taitō
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Namiki Yabu Soba opened in 1913 on Namiki-dōri, a short walk from Sensō-ji's Kaminarimon gate, as a branch of Kanda Yabu Soba — making it one of the three storied "Yabu" soba houses of old Tokyo (the others being the original Kanda Yabu Soba and the now-closed Ikenohata Yabu Soba · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Founded in 1913 (Taishō 2) as a branch of the Kanda Yabu Soba lineage, opened by a member of that founding family
chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — direct branch of the original Kanda Yabu Soba
chef_bio.background: Namiki Yabu Soba opened in 1913 on Namiki-dōri, a short walk from Sensō-ji's Kaminarimon gate, as a branch of Kanda Yabu Soba — making it one of the three storied "Yabu" soba houses of old Tokyo (the others being the original Kanda Yabu Soba and the now-closed Ikenohata Yabu Soba). Regulars have long called it "the Yabu of Kaminarimon. " It has stayed on the same stretch of Namiki-dōri through more than a century of Asakusa's ups and downs, and today still draws lunchtime queues of both neighborhood regulars and visitors.
chef_bio.philosophy: The founder built the shop's tsuyu to be sipped in tiny amounts — diners are meant to dip only the tip of the noodles rather than submerge them — so the soba's own aroma, not the sauce, stays in the foreground. The kaeshi (soy sauce base) is aged for about a week in buried earthen pots, then combined with a dashi of deeply cured, mold-ripened bonito simmered each morning for about 90 minutes, and rested a further two days before use.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-cut juwari (十割) soba, made from 100% buckwheat milled fresh each day, served with an unusually dark, concentrated dipping sauce; the seasonal kamo nanban (duck soba), available roughly November–March, is a signature cold-weather order.

anecdotes.text: The novelist Shōtarō Ikenami was a devoted regular who favored the kamo nanban when it appeared in early winter, writing that settling into the tatami seating on a quiet weekday afternoon once the lunch rush had cleared, and drinking sake slowly, was one of life's great small pleasures.

The essayist Hitomi Yamaguchi would order "kamo-nuki" (the duck soba's toppings without the noodles) in winter and "ten-nuki" (tempura without noodles) the rest of the year, treating them as a sake companion before finishing with a plate of soba.

The shop is widely regarded as the model for the tsuyu-obsessed soba stall in volume 2 of the manga Oishinbo ("The Depth of Soba Broth"), a story built around the painstaking kaeshi-and-dashi process this shop is known for.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A visitor guide notes that although the noodles are 100% buckwheat, "soy sauce in Japan these days is not entirely wheat-free," and recommends confirming the exact ingredients with the restaurant before dining if celiac disease is a concern.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's tsuyu is built from a kaeshi made with soy sauce, mirin, and sugar, aged in buried earthen pots for about a week before being combined with the dashi — this is standard shoyu, not a wheat-free formulation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi used for both the cold dipping sauce and hot noodle broths is made from hon-karebushi, a deeply cured, mold-ripened bonito, simmered fresh each morning for about 90 minutes — meaning the standard tsuyu used across the menu is fish-based.

positives.text: The soba itself is juwari (十割) — cut from 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat flour used as a binder, and the buckwheat is milled fresh each day rather than pre-ground and stored.

cultural_comfort.note: A century-old, tourist-visible soba house just off Kaminarimon where ordering standard dishes is easy even without Japanese, but the traditional lightly-dip tsuyu etiquette and any gluten-free/vegan requests will go more smoothly with a little Japanese or patience with pointing, since no English menu or staff-language info was found.

places.name: 福ちゃん (Fukuchan)
places.gf_detail: The entire menu is built around wheat yakisoba noodles pan-fried with a Worcestershire-style sauce (also offered in curry-sauce and beef-tendon versions). No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation is mentioned in any source found. The sauce itself is wheat-based/contains soy sauce, and toppings like tenkasu (fried tempura scraps) add further wheat/gluten exposure. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian option is mentioned anywhere. The listed dishes include beef-tendon (gyūsuji) yakisoba, gyoza, and gyūsuji nikomi (beef tendon stew), and optional raw/fried egg toppings; the yakisoba sauce is a typical Japanese Worcestershire-style sauce that commonly contains animal-derived extracts. No dashi/katsuobushi disclosure was found, but nothing suggests a vegan-aware kitchen.
places.hours_raw: 火〜金 11:30-21:00、土・日 11:00-20:00、月曜定休（一部情報源では土曜も不定休の場合ありと記載）
places.neighborhood: 浅草 / 浅草地下街（昭和地下街）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A tiny, no-frills yakisoba counter that has fed neighborhood regulars and passersby for six decades from the same spot in the retro underground arcade near Kaminarimon and Sensōji. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Opened around 1964-1965 (Shōwa 39-40) inside Asakusa Chika-gai, Japan's oldest surviving underground shopping street, right by the Tokyo Metro Ginza Line Asakusa Station exit.
chef_bio.background: A tiny, no-frills yakisoba counter that has fed neighborhood regulars and passersby for six decades from the same spot in the retro underground arcade near Kaminarimon and Sensōji.
chef_bio.specialty: Pan-fried sauce yakisoba, offered in three versions (classic Worcestershire-style sauce, curry sauce, and beef-tendon/gyūsuji), topped simply with cabbage and crispy tenkasu, with optional raw or fried egg.

anecdotes.text: The shop appears four times in Wim Wenders' 2023 film PERFECT DAYS as the favorite izakaya of protagonist Hirayama, who cycles there over Sakura-bashi bridge after his public-bath visits; the director reportedly spent over an hour scouting the interior before filming.
Sits inside Japan's oldest surviving underground shopping street (opened 1955), and is noted for a distinctly strong-flavored curry-sauce yakisoba among its three sauce variations.

cultural_comfort.note: A cash-only, Japanese-menu retro stall inside a local underground arcade — approachable with basic Japanese or pointing at the menu, but no English menu or vegan/gluten-free signage was found.

places.name: お米とごはん 隅田屋 浅草店 (Okome to Gohan Sumidaya Asakusa (Rice and Me)
places.gf_detail: The core product is plain rice hand-molded into onigiri, which is naturally wheat-free, but no gluten-free policy, ingredient list, or cross-contamination protocol was published by the shop. Typical onigiri fillings and seasonings (e.g., soy sauce-based preparations) commonly contain wheat, and nothing confirms a dedicated gluten-free process — celiac diners should ask staff about each filling and any soy sauce/seasoning used before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu or ingredient disclosure was found. The signature omusubi set offers a choice of 7 fillings; company materials for the brand's other outlets describe a mix of fillings including some that appear plant-based (e.g., pickled plum, kelp) alongside others that are clearly not (e.g., tuna, fried-tofu mixes at a sister branch), and Japanese rice cooking commonly uses bonito-based dashi or seasonings behind the scenes. Vegan diners should ask which fillings and any seasoning liquids are free of fish, meat, and dashi before ordering.
places.neighborhood: 花川戸 / 浅草 (Hanakawado, Asakusa), Taito-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 隅田屋商店は1905年、初代・松五郎が浅草の米屋で修業したのちのれん分けを受け、隅田川ほとりの地に開いた米穀店。関東大震災や戦後の米の大凶作を経て信用を築き、2代目・三郎の代には太平洋戦争と配給統制という食糧難の時代を経験した。現在は5代目にあたる片山真一氏が代表を務める。2026年3月17日、創業120年を機に浅草に直営のイートインスペースを備えたアンテナショップ「お米とごはん 隅田屋 浅草店」をオープンし、米穀店として初めて飲食業に進出した。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: 1905年（明治38年）創業の老舗米穀店「隅田屋商店」が初めて手がけた飲食業態（アンテナショップ）
chef_bio.origin: 東京都墨田区東駒形（隅田川ほとり）で1905年創業
chef_bio.background: 隅田屋商店は1905年、初代・松五郎が浅草の米屋で修業したのちのれん分けを受け、隅田川ほとりの地に開いた米穀店。 関東大震災や戦後の米の大凶作を経て信用を築き、2代目・三郎の代には太平洋戦争と配給統制という食糧難の時代を経験した。 現在は5代目にあたる片山真一氏が代表を務める。 2026年3月17日、創業120年を機に浅草に直営のイートインスペースを備えたアンテナショップ「お米とごはん 隅田屋 浅草店」をオープンし、米穀店として初めて飲食業に進出した。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「お米を嗜好品として楽しんでもらいたい」という思いのもと、商標登録された独自の「古式精米製法」（一般的な一回精米に対し、弱い圧力で7〜10回に分けてじっくり精米する手法）で用途に応じて米の旨みを最大限に引き出すことにこだわる。
chef_bio.specialty: 看板メニューの「おむすび御膳」（2,000円・税込）。 お櫃で炊きたてのご飯を用意し、オリジナルの型を使って客自身がおむすびを握り、7種類の具から自由に組み合わせて楽しむ体験型の一膳。

anecdotes.text: 太平洋戦争が勃発し米の配給制度が敷かれた戦中戦後の食糧難の時代、2代目・三郎は「お客様にお腹いっぱい食べさせてあげたい」という思いから米の確保に奔走したと伝えられている。
初代・松五郎は浅草の米屋で育ち、のれん分けとして隅田川のほとりに「隅田屋商店」を開いたのが創業の始まり。
隅田屋最大の特長は商標登録された「古式精米製法」で、一般的な精米が一度で仕上げるのに対し、隅田屋では7〜10回に分け弱い圧力でじっくりと精米する。
2026年3月17日、創業120年の老舗米穀店「隅田屋」が浅草にアンテナショップをオープン。イートインスペースを備え、看板の「おむすび御膳」（2,000円）は客自身がオリジナルの型でご飯をおむすびに握り、7種の具を組み合わせる体験型メニュー。

cultural_comfort.note: The signature omusubi set is a simple, visual point-and-choose experience (pick from the filling options), so basic Japanese or gestures should be enough to order, though no English menu or English-speaking staff could be confirmed.

places.name: 光家 (Kushiage Mitsuya (光家))
places.gf_detail: Every skewer is coated in panko (wheat breadcrumbs) and deep-fried, and the shared dipping sauce is an Osaka-style Worcestershire-type sauce, which typically contains wheat. No gluten-free preparation, separate fryer, or wheat-free menu is mentioned anywhere. This is a high-risk venue for celiac disease or wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: The confirmed menu is centered on meat, fish, and cheese skewers (pork fillet katsu, ham katsu, cheese, quail egg, squid, shrimp, chikuwa fish cake, shiso pork belly). No vegetable-only or vegan skewers, and no vegan accommodation, were found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 平日16:00〜22:00、土日祝13:00〜20:00（水曜定休）／ Weekdays 16:00–22:00, weekends & holidays 13:00–20:00, closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: 花川戸 (Hanakawado), Asakusa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The proprietress, a former geisha born and raised in Asakusa, opened this standing kushiage counter in December 2001 a short walk from Asakusa Station. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: 光也 (Mitsuya) — known to regulars as "光家姐さん" (the Mitsuya-neesan)
chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (born and raised locally)
chef_bio.background: The proprietress, a former geisha born and raised in Asakusa, opened this standing kushiage counter in December 2001 a short walk from Asakusa Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: She set out to build a casual counter where a woman could feel comfortable walking in and drinking alone — and kept the same simple panko and oil for years so the light, crisp coating never changes.
chef_bio.specialty: Panko-breaded, deep-fried kushiage skewers (pork fillet katsu, ham katsu, cheese, quail egg, squid, shrimp, chikuwa fish cake) served Osaka-style with a shared Worcestershire-type dipping sauce and free cabbage.

anecdotes.text: Regulars are said to range in age from 4 to 94, with an unusually high share of women dining solo.
Like most kushiage counters, the house rule is no double-dipping in the communal sauce; a blogger who visited for skewers and lemon sours affectionately calls her "光家姐さん."

gf_cross_contamination.text: Skewers are coated in panko (wheat breadcrumbs) before deep-frying.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dipping is done in a shared Osaka-style Worcestershire-type sauce, with a house 'no double-dipping' rule since the sauce pot is communal.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The confirmed menu is fish-, meat-, and dairy-based (pork fillet katsu, ham katsu, cheese, quail egg, squid, shrimp, chikuwa fish cake, shiso-wrapped pork belly); no vegetable-only or plant-based skewers were found in any source reviewed.

cultural_comfort.note: A classic, no-frills standing kushiage counter with local etiquette (no double-dipping the communal sauce); no English menu was found, so some Japanese phrases or pointing at the counter items will help.

places.name: ビストロ福昇亭 (Bistro Fukushotei)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free options, or celiac-safety information was found in any source. Signature dishes are breaded, deep-fried cutlets (tonkatsu, chicken katsu) and wheat-noodle ramen, both inherently wheat-based; demi-glace sauce and soy sauce used in yoshoku cooking typically contain wheat as well. No mention of a dedicated fryer or gluten-free accommodation was found. Not recommended for strict celiac diners without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based options, or dashi/bonito-avoidance information was found. The menu centers on animal-based yoshoku dishes (pork cutlet, chicken cutlet, hamburger steak) and ramen, which typically uses meat- or fish-based broth and hidden animal-derived seasonings (dashi, katsuobushi). No evidence this restaurant caters to vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 火〜土：ランチ 11:30-14:30／ディナー 18:00-23:00、日・祝：11:30-21:00、月曜定休 (Tue-Sat: lunch 11:30-14:30, dinner 18:00-23:00; Sun/holidays: 11:30-21:00; closed Mondays)
places.neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] According to Asakusa-area food/tourism write-ups, the business behind Senso-ji has passed through three generations and three identities: the first generation ran a Chinese restaurant called Fukushotei (福昇亭), the second generation turned the space into a restaurant called Skyline · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.specialty: Pork cutlet (tonkatsu) with demi-glace sauce

anecdotes.text: Although the shop now operates as a yoshoku bistro, a Rocket News 24 feature reports that longtime regulars are known to skip the Western menu and order the restaurant's under-the-radar ramen instead.

The current dining room has held three different restaurant identities across three generations of the same family -- a Chinese restaurant, then 'Skyline', then today's Bistro Fukushotei -- reflecting decades of adaptation in the same spot tucked behind Senso-ji.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood family-run yoshoku diner popular with local regulars behind Senso-ji; no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found, so some Japanese may be needed to order comfortably.

places.name: スケマサ コーヒー (SUKEMASA COFFEE) (Sukemasa Coffee)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free bread or menu alternative was found in any source. The signature fruit sandwiches are made with regular wheat shokupan (soft milk bread), so they are not celiac-safe. The espresso and coffee drinks themselves are naturally gluten-free, but no information on shared prep surfaces, toasters, or cross-contact precautions was found, so even coffee-only visits carry some cross-contact uncertainty in a bakery-adjacent kitchen. Not recommended for strict celiac diners without calling ahead.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu items were identified. The seasonal fruit sandwiches contain fresh dairy cream and are not vegan. Straight black coffee/espresso would be vegan by nature, but no dairy-free milk option for lattes was found in any source, so vegan visitors are effectively limited to black coffee only.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-18:00, closed Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa), Taito-ku — next to Hanayashiki, ~4-5 min walk
places.cuisine: Specialty Coffee / Seasonal Fruit Sandwich C
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner originally trained as a pastry chef, working her way through a confectionery course at cooking school and then joining a well-regarded local Western-style cake shop. The physically demanding conditions of pastry work — long hours and constant work in cold-temperature ki · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: The owner originally trained as a pastry chef, working her way through a confectionery course at cooking school and then joining a well-regarded local Western-style cake shop. The physically demanding conditions of pastry work — long hours and constant work in cold-temperature kitchens/refrigerated rooms — took a toll on her health, and she eventually had to leave the pastry industry.
chef_bio.philosophy: After the 2011 Great East Japan Earthquake, while recovering from the health problems that ended her pastry career, she said she realized life can end at any moment, and decided she wanted to make a living doing what she loved — coffee and kimono — and live joyfully. That realization led her to open a coffee shop built around her two passions.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal fruit sandwiches (using fruit such as strawberries, shine muscat, Nagano purple grapes, white peach and mango depending on the season) paired with espresso and lattes; strawberry versions are paired with red azuki bean paste (ogura-an) and mikan versions with white bean paste (shiro-an) alongside fresh cream.

anecdotes.text: 木村さんは小さい頃からケーキ屋さんになると決めていて、実際にパティシエとなり働いていたが、室温の低い職場環境に身体を壊してしまいやむを得ず退職しました。開店のきっかけは東日本大震災で、『人生っていつ終わってしまうかわからないなと気づいて、それならば好きなことで生計を立てて、楽しく生きていきたいと思って自分の好きなコーヒーと着物のお店を開くのが目標になりました』と述べています。
着物姿のバリスタがラテアートを作るのが浅草らしい下町ならではの特徴で、2018年8月24日のオープン以来、常に大盛況の人気カフェとなっている。2022年には2号店『ペチカ スケマサコーヒー』も浅草にオープンした。

vegan_cross_contact.text: The fruit sandwiches are built on fresh dairy whipped cream together with azuki (ogura-an) or white bean (shiro-an) paste, so the standard menu is not vegan; no dairy-free cream substitute was mentioned in any source.

cultural_comfort.note: A very Japanese-aesthetic neighborhood cafe (kimono-clad barista, wagashi-inspired fillings) in the heart of tourist Asakusa; no English menu or English-speaking-staff information was found, so first-time visitors should be ready to point at the display case or use translation apps.

places.name: 房寿 (Boju)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, statement, or accommodation was found in any source. As a traditional Edomae sushi restaurant, soy sauce (shoyu) and any eel/nitsume-style sauces used for nigiri are very likely wheat-based, and no dedicated gluten-free soy sauce or fryer was mentioned. Celiac diners should call ahead and ask directly rather than assume any accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or accommodation was found. This is a traditional fish-centric Edomae sushi restaurant; miso soup and other non-nigiri items very likely use katsuo/bonito-based dashi, and there is no indication of a plant-based tasting option. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners without prior arrangement, and even then success is unconfirmed.
places.hours_raw: Sources conflict. One Tabelog-derived summary states daily 11:00-15:00 (lunch) and 17:00-24:00 (dinner, L.O. midnight) with no closed day. A separate Ekiten/Hotpepper-derived summary states weekday 11:30-15:00 / 17:00-24:00, weekends and holidays 11:30 straight through to close, with Monday as the regular closed day, and yet another snippet gives 12:00-15:00 / 18:00-24:00. Could not verify against the live page due to a session-wide fetch outage.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The head chef trained at the former Hotel New Otani Akasaka before opening this Edomae sushi counter on Asakusa's "Shokutsu-gai" (food-lovers' street), one block north of Kaminarimon-dori. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: The restaurant's name blends 房 (a character honoring a mentor the chef trained under and remains grateful to) with 寿 (an auspicious character associated with sushi/celebration) — chosen so that debt of gratitude would never be forgotten.
chef_bio.background: The head chef trained at the former Hotel New Otani Akasaka before opening this Edomae sushi counter on Asakusa's "Shokutsu-gai" (food-lovers' street), one block north of Kaminarimon-dori.
chef_bio.philosophy: Ingredients are sourced fresh daily (historically from Tsukiji), timed to the season, with fish finished to the second for optimal texture — uni from Hokkaido, kohada from Kyushu, eel prepared in Edomae style.
chef_bio.specialty: Kohada (gizzard shad) prepared with meticulous seasonal timing; hamaguri (clam) nigiri finished with a house sauce made from clam broth instead of the usual eel-based nitsume; and uni nigiri served deliberately without a nori wrap to let the sea urchin's flavor stand on its own.

anecdotes.text: The shop name combines a character from a mentor the chef trained under with 寿, intended as a lasting expression of gratitude.
The counter is a single board set at the angle that shows the sushi at its most beautiful, and the room mixes a 9-seat counter with table and sunken-kotatsu seating for families and older guests.
Lunch offers a very affordable "nami-chirashi" for around 500 yen alongside a premium 3000-yen chirashi, while dinner runs later as a full Edomae sushi counter experience.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional backstreet Edomae sushi counter with no English website found; expect a Japanese-language menu and counter experience where pointing at case items or simple Japanese will serve better than English.

places.name: とんかつ ゆたか (Tonkatsu Yutaka)
places.gf_detail: The signature cutlets are coated in house-made panko baked from domestic wheat bread — the breading itself contains gluten. No gluten-free menu items, substitutions, or dedicated frying process are mentioned anywhere in official or third-party sources, so this is not a safe option for celiac or gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian dishes were found on the menu. The restaurant is built entirely around pork cutlets and other meat dishes, with sides like miso soup that likely use fish-based dashi.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:00 (L.O.), 17:00-20:00 (L.O.); closed every Wednesday and Thursday
places.neighborhood: 浅草（雷門周辺）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A family-run tonkatsu restaurant tucked into a quiet backstreet between Kaminarimon-dori and Tanuki-dori in Asakusa. Founded in 1945 in the postwar years, it is now run by the third generation of the same family and has kept the same style of cooking for more than 80 years. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (founded 1945)
chef_bio.background: A family-run tonkatsu restaurant tucked into a quiet backstreet between Kaminarimon-dori and Tanuki-dori in Asakusa. Founded in 1945 in the postwar years, it is now run by the third generation of the same family and has kept the same style of cooking for more than 80 years.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated aim is a cutlet that feels crisp and satisfying without sitting heavy afterward: loin from Gunma-raised Yamato pork, panko that the shop bakes and grinds in-house from domestic-wheat bread for a sweeter, crunchier crust, and frying in cottonseed oil rather than lard so the dish is lighter on the stomach than a typical tonkatsu.
chef_bio.specialty: Rosu-katsu (pork loin cutlet) set meal — a cutlet prized for its thin, crackling crust and pink, juicy meat that diners describe as unusually light and easy on the stomach for fried pork.

anecdotes.text: The shop bakes its own bread and grinds it into panko using domestic wheat, giving the coating a sweeter flavor and crisper texture than store-bought breadcrumbs.

The restaurant fries in cottonseed oil instead of lard, aiming for a lighter, less greasy result that doesn't cause heartburn or a heavy stomach; the oil is kept so clear it looks almost like water, and breading crumbs are carefully skimmed out as the cutlets fry.

The interior retains structural elements from the old shophouse — a large central pillar and a woven-bamboo ceiling — giving the dining room the settled, dignified feel of a long-established restaurant.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All cutlets are coated in house-made panko baked from domestic wheat bread, so the breading itself contains gluten; no gluten-free preparation is described by the restaurant or reviewers.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers entirely on pork cutlets and other meat/seafood dishes; no vegan or vegetarian dishes appear on the published menu.

cultural_comfort.note: The shop posts English signage and appears on Tabelog's English site, but day-to-day service is in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help.

places.name: 鳥こじ (Tori Koji)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information was found for this restaurant. As a traditional yakitori-ya, the tare (glaze) is almost certainly a soy-sauce-based sauce containing wheat, brushed onto skewers on a shared grill; shio (salt-seasoned) skewers would avoid the tare itself but cross-contact on the same grill and shared brushes/tongs is likely. No dedicated fryer, wheat-free soy sauce, or allergy protocol was mentioned in any source found. Anyone with celiac disease should call ahead (in Japanese) and confirm directly rather than relying on this listing.
places.vegan_detail: This is a chicken-focused charcoal yakitori-ya with no indication of a vegan menu. A shiitake mushroom skewer is mentioned by reviewers and could plausibly be vegetarian/vegan if grilled shio-style without a wheat-soy tare, but this is not confirmed, and dashi/bonito are typically used in the salad dressing and rice-bowl broth at places like this. Vegans should ask specifically about the shiitake skewer's seasoning and whether any dashi or animal stock touches the vegetable dishes.
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Asakusa (near Asakusa View Hotel)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small husband-and-wife-run yakitori counter that opened in March 2021 in Nishi-Asakusa, a short walk from the Asakusa View Hotel. The shop occupies a renovated space that was formerly a tendon (rice-bowl) restaurant; the owners kept a sense of the building's history while givin · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A small husband-and-wife-run yakitori counter that opened in March 2021 in Nishi-Asakusa, a short walk from the Asakusa View Hotel. The shop occupies a renovated space that was formerly a tendon (rice-bowl) restaurant; the owners kept a sense of the building's history while giving it a clean, updated feel. It is run by just two people, the owner and a woman working the counter alongside him.
chef_bio.specialty: Skewers grilled over charcoal, including a raw-wasabi semi-seared chicken breast (namawasabi no hannama sasami) and skewers such as harami (skirt) and shiitake mushroom that reviewers single out; a set course (salad, five yakitori skewers, rice bowl, and dessert) is offered for about 3,000 yen.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant opened in March 2021 in a renovated building that used to house a tendon shop, with the owners keeping traces of the older building's character while making it feel fresh.
Diners highlight the harami and shiitake skewers as standouts, and the 3,000-yen course (salad, five grilled skewers, rice bowl, dessert) as good value.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny local counter (five seats plus one table and a small tatami room) with no official website or English presence found anywhere — expect a Japanese-only menu and no English-speaking accommodation, so a translation app or guide is recommended.

places.name: 中国手打拉麺 馬賊 浅草店（浅草本店） (Bazoku Asakusa (Chugoku Teuchi Ramen Bazoku )
places.gf_detail: The restaurant's signature product is wheat-flour hand-pulled noodles (手打ち麺), made fresh in-house and visible from the street; no gluten-free noodle or dish substitution is mentioned in any source found. The soy-sauce-based broth/tare also typically contains wheat. No dedicated gluten-free options, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodations were found in any reviewed source — this is not a safe venue for celiac or gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian option was found in any source. The broth is described as pork-bone-and-chicken based (豚骨鶏ガラ), with a soy-based tare and a signature sesame/hot-oil finish; the noodles, gyoza, and toppings (chashu pork, etc.) are all meat-inclusive in the dishes described by reviewers. There is no mention of a vegetable-broth or plant-based menu item.

It later added a second location in Nippori, but the Asakusa shop remains the original. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: noodle-pulling craftspeople (職人) who train for at least two years to master the technique
chef_bio.origin: Shandong-style Chinese hand-pulled noodle (拉麺) tradition, brought to Asakusa in 1977
chef_bio.background: Opened in Asakusa in 1977 (Showa 52), a short walk from Sensoji's Kaminarimon gate, as a Chinese-style hand-pulled noodle specialist. It later added a second location in Nippori, but the Asakusa shop remains the original.
chef_bio.philosophy: Noodles are pulled to order only after a customer orders, so the kitchen never holds pre-made noodles; the shop treats the hand-pulling itself as a craft, reportedly taking a noodle-puller at least two years to master.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-pulled noodles (手打ち麺) — stretched and slapped by hand in the shop's street-facing window and used across the menu: shoyu ramen, tantanmen, tsukemen, and hand-made gyoza

anecdotes.text: The percussive slap of the noodle-maker's hands hitting the dough echoes out through a window facing the street, so passersby on the way to Sensoji can watch the noodles being hand-pulled before they're even cooked.

One diner wrote about having craved the shop's hand-pulled noodles for 15 years before finally trying the tsukemen, topped with scallions, bean sprouts, chashu, nori and menma, describing a distinctive chewy, almost 'sliding' texture unlike ordinary ramen noodles.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, Japanese-language neighborhood noodle shop with no confirmed English menu; ordering will likely require pointing, a translation app, or basic Japanese, though its fame with tourists visiting nearby Sensoji means staff have some experience with non-Japanese-speaking customers.

places.name: ちゃんこ部屋 浅草店 (Chanko Beya Asakusa)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol is advertised on official or listing pages. The soy-sauce (shoyu) chanko broth almost certainly relies on standard Japanese soy sauce, which typically contains wheat, and the kitchen also serves charcoal-grilled and presumably fried items from shared equipment. Celiac diners should call ahead (050-5448-6268) to ask about ingredients before dining; nothing found confirms safe handling.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional sumo-style chanko nabe restaurant built around chicken, pork, seafood, and fish-based hot pot broths (dashi in Japanese hot pot typically includes bonito/katsuo), plus a sashimi and charcoal-grilled-meat/fish menu. No vegan menu, plant-based broth, or vegetarian option is advertised on official or listing pages.

cultural_comfort.note: Sits in touristy Asakusa near Sensō-ji, but no English menu or English-speaking staff could be confirmed from available sources, so expect a Japanese-language ordering experience.

places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based rolls, or vegan accommodation were mentioned in any source. chef_bio.chef_name: 板長マウンさん (Maung) and 板前ニイさん (Ni)
chef_bio.origin: Myanmar
chef_bio.background: Head chef Maung came to Japan in 1996, during a period when Myanmar's military government was cracking down on the democracy movement and the economy was struggling, and spent 24 years training as a sushi craftsman in Japan. Fellow chef Ni has close to 20 years of sushi experience in Japan. In June 2019 (the first year of the Reiwa era), a group of Myanmar-born residents living in Japan pooled their own money to set up a company together and open this sushi restaurant in Asakusa, naming it after the new era.
chef_bio.philosophy: To show that decades of hard-won craft — not nationality — is what makes a sushi chef, after facing skepticism from some early customers about non-Japanese chefs behind the counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Traditional Edomae-style sushi alongside original creations, including a spicy roll and the restaurant's signature 'Asakusa roll' (浅草巻き).

anecdotes.text: A magazine feature's headline recounts a customer's blunt first reaction on seeing the chefs — 'Why is a foreigner making this?!' — framed as the starting point of the chefs' effort to win diners over through skill.

The restaurant opened in June 2019 after Myanmar-born residents in Japan pooled their own funds to establish a company together, and the shop later ran a crowdfunding campaign on READYFOR to support its operations.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-speaking service was confirmed in any source; this is a small Japanese-run sushi counter, so most communication should be expected in Japanese, though the chefs' own immigrant backgrounds may make them personally sympathetic to non-Japanese diners.

places.name: 三角 (Sankaku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is mentioned anywhere in available sources. The menu is fugu-centric and includes karaage (fried fugu), which uses a wheat-flour batter/coating, and dishes are typically served with ponzu or soy-based dipping sauces that almost certainly contain wheat. There is no indication of a dedicated fryer, wheat-free soy sauce, or any celiac-safe protocol. Diners with celiac disease or serious gluten intolerance should not consider this safe without direct, detailed confirmation from the restaurant in Japanese.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around fugu (pufferfish): sashimi, hot pot, fried fugu, fin sake, and milt. There are no plant-based or vegetarian dishes evident on the menu from any source, and as a single-species fish specialty restaurant it is not set up to accommodate vegan diners.

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Founded in Meiji 35 (1902) as a Japanese restaurant specializing in fugu (pufferfish) cuisine in Asakusa. The restaurant has operated continuously for over 120 years, weathering the 1923 Great Kanto Earthquake and the aftermath of WWII in 1945. It is now run by a third-generation owner who was born and raised in Asakusa. The building was renovated in September 2016, with horigotatsu (sunken-kotatsu) seating added to both the first and second floors.
chef_bio.specialty: Fugu kaiseki course centered on tessa (fugu sashimi), techiri (fugu hot pot), nikogori (jellied fugu broth), karaage (fried fugu), hire-zake (fin sake), and shirako (fugu milt) — the grilled shirako, in season from around November, is especially prized by regulars.

anecdotes.text: Founded in Meiji 35 (1902), the restaurant has continued as a Japanese restaurant ever since, passing through three generations of ownership and surviving the 1923 Great Kanto Earthquake and the end of WWII in 1945.
The restaurant sits about a 1-minute walk from Kaminarimon Gate and roughly 3 minutes from Asakusa Station, tucked in the backstreets near Sensoji.
It was renovated in September 2016 (Heisei 28), with seating on both the first and second floors converted to horigotatsu sunken-kotatsu style.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional fugu preparations (ponzu-dipped tessa/sashimi, techiri hot pot dipping sauce) rely on standard soy-based sauces that typically contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce option is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A century-old, locally run fugu specialty restaurant with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff; phone reservation in Japanese is the norm, though its location one minute from Kaminarimon Gate means it does see some tourist foot traffic.

places.name: 鮨 奥 (Sushi Oku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or celiac-specific accommodation is advertised anywhere found. Traditional Edomae sushi relies on wheat-containing soy sauce (nikiri) brushed onto nigiri and may include tempura or other wheat-based elements within the omakase course. Celiac diners should call ahead (in Japanese, or via a Japanese-speaking concierge) to ask whether the chef can substitute tamari or omit sauced/fried items — no such accommodation could be confirmed from available sources.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional Edomae sushi omakase built around raw and cooked seafood; no vegetarian or vegan tasting menu is offered or advertised, and the fixed omakase course is not structured to be adapted to a plant-based diet.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri: dinner seating from 18:30. Wed, Sat: lunch seating from 12:00 and dinner seating from 18:30. Closed Sunday. Exact closing times not published (single-seating omakase course); one source separately listed evening hours as "from 17:00," so evening start time may vary by day or have changed — confirm when booking.

chef_bio.background: Chef Oku opened this nine-seat counter in Asakusa in April 2021. He trained previously at the respected Edomae sushi restaurant Sushi Hashiguchi (鮨 橋口), and the shop is often described by reviewers as carrying on the lineage/style of the well-regarded 'Takase' (高勢) school of Edomae sushi.
chef_bio.philosophy: Adherence to classic Edomae sushi fundamentals, with the chef's individual sensibility expressed through small technical details rather than novelty.
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri sushi omakase, with particular attention to knife work, cutting thickness, and rice/vinegar balance

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe a calm, personable nine-seat counter where the chef looks after regulars closely, giving the room a warm, low-key atmosphere rather than a formal one.
A sushi-focused blog profiled Oku as a lesser-known but highly skilled craftsman ('知る人ぞ知る腕利き職人') carrying on the Takase lineage in Asakusa.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, intimate Japanese sushi-ya with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking service; reservations are typically made by phone or Japanese reservation platforms, so first-time foreign visitors may want to book through a concierge or hotel.

places.name: 自家製麺NO11 ASAKUSA (Jikaseimen NO11 Asakusa)

No gluten-free noodle substitute, wheat-free soy sauce, or any celiac-safe alternative is mentioned anywhere in the shop's own posts or in any review found. Not a safe option for anyone avoiding gluten.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is centered on pork-bone soup and pork toppings (chāshū pork, back fat), and no plant-based broth, noodle, or topping option was found in any review, blog coverage, or the shop's own social posts. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Multiple sources (the shop's own X/Twitter posts, ramen blogs) report Sun–Thu hours of 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30), though one listing gives the evening opening as 17:30 rather than 17:00. Friday/Saturday hours were not confirmed in any source found; the same window is assumed here but is unverified.

In April 2025 he opened this second location on Asakusa's Hisago-dori shopping street, taking o · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Grew up in the ramen scene around Fujimaru's Nishiarai Daishi branch, a shop in the Ramen Jiro lineage
chef_bio.background: Owner Kimura trained at Ramen Fujimaru's Nishiarai Daishi location, a shop descended from the Ramen Jiro style, before opening the original 自家製麺NO11 in Itabashi (Ooyama/Shimoitabashi). In April 2025 he opened this second location on Asakusa's Hisago-dori shopping street, taking over a space that had previously housed 麺屋まるいち, a shop No11 had also produced.
chef_bio.specialty: Jiro-style pork ramen built on housemade extra-thick noodles and an emulsified pork-bone shoyu broth, topped with tender braised pork, soft-boiled 'kuta' vegetables, garlic and back fat.

anecdotes.text: Food blogger Miku Okazaki covered the Asakusa opening and interviewed owner Kimura about his approach to the noodles and broth for the shop's 'makanai interview' feature, noting a congratulatory flower display in the shop window marking the opening.
Tokyo ramen blog Ramental described the shop as drawing a constant queue and praised the sharp, clean cut of the emulsified pork-bone shoyu broth.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a shoyu (soy sauce) based, emulsified pork-bone soup; no wheat-free soy sauce, tamari, or gluten-free broth alternative is described in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broth is pork-bone based and every bowl comes with pork toppings by default; no vegetarian or vegan broth or topping substitution appears in the menu as described by reviewers (Ramen: ¥1,200, Pork Ramen: ¥1,600).

cultural_comfort.note: Orders go through a cash-only ticket vending machine, and Jiro-style etiquette means calling out topping preferences (vegetables, garlic, extra fat) when your bowl is called — a little Japanese vocabulary makes the ritual far less intimidating for first-time visitors.

places.name: うなぎの蒲の穂焼 牛タン 焼鳥 馬刺し いづも 浅草横町 (Izumo (Asakusa Yokocho))

Treat as unsafe for celiac diners absent direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No plant-based or vegan menu items were found. The menu centers on eel, beef tongue, chicken yakitori, and horse sashimi, and dashi/tare bases are unlikely to be free of fish/bonito. This does not appear to be a viable option for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Sources disagree: Asakusa Yokocho's own shop page lists 11:00–22:00 daily; one aggregator lists weekdays 12:00–22:00 / weekends & holidays 12:00–23:00; another lists 12:00–23:00 daily, matching the overall Asakusa Yokocho facility hours (12:00–23:00, last order 22:00).

roles: Eel (unagi) specialty izakaya, one of several branches of the いづも eel-skewer concept
chef_bio.origin: Osaka (Fukushima)
chef_bio.background: いづも is a small unagi (eel) specialty izakaya concept that began at a honten in Osaka's Fukushima district before expanding to a branch inside Osaka Station's LUCUA shopping complex and to this Asakusa Yokocho branch in Tokyo, a festival-themed food hall on the 4th floor of the Tokyo Rakutenchi Asakusa Building.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chain's stated aim is to make traditionally pricey, formal unagi more affordable and approachable by serving it izakaya-style in a lively, casual setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Unagi tamadon and unagi gyudon (eel rice bowls with egg or beef), unagi kamahoyaki (semi-grilled eel fillet), and eel kushiyaki skewers, served alongside grilled beef tongue, yakitori, and horse sashimi.

anecdotes.text: The Osaka honten relocated and relaunched with new menu items in 2024, described as an effort to carry Japanese eel food culture forward while keeping it reasonably priced ('大阪一リーズナブルな鰻屋').
The Asakusa branch sits inside 'Asakusa Yokocho,' a food-and-festival themed dining hall built to let visitors experience Japanese festival culture alongside food from multiple genres.

cultural_comfort.note: Sits inside a tourist-oriented "food and festival" dining hall a minute from Asakusa Station with an English Tabelog listing, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found.

places.name: つくし (Tsukushi)
places.gf_detail: Monjayaki and okonomiyaki batter here is wheat-flour based by nature of the dish, and the dashi-based batter plus any tare/Worcestershire-style sauces used in Tokyo-style monjayaki typically contain wheat as well. No gluten-free batter substitute, dedicated fryer, or celiac-specific protocol is advertised anywhere for this shop. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat and seafood toppings (beef tendon, pork, squid, shrimp) and egg-topped monja/okonomiyaki, and traditional Tokyo monjayaki batter is typically made with bonito (katsuobushi)-based dashi. No vegan or vegetarian-specific menu items are advertised, so vegan diners should expect very limited to no safe options without extensive kitchen negotiation.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 11:00–23:00; Sat/Sun & holidays 10:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:00); closed Tuesdays, though the closed day is not always fixed each week — call ahead.

It is roughly 55 years old and is often described as the second-oldest monjayaki shop in the neighborhood, ha · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Tsukushi is a long-running monjayaki (and okonomiyaki) restaurant on Asakusa's Hoppy-dōri (Hoppy Street), tucked behind the Denbō-in temple grounds just west of Sensō-ji. It is roughly 55 years old and is often described as the second-oldest monjayaki shop in the neighborhood, having kept its core recipe essentially unchanged for more than 40 years. It draws a mix of shitamachi (old-downtown) regulars, weekend queues, and a following of visiting celebrities.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop emphasizes continuity: staff describe preserving the same taste and recipes they've served for over 40 years rather than chasing novelty, treating monjayaki as a genuine old-Tokyo comfort food.
chef_bio.specialty: Monjayaki and okonomiyaki — notably the loaded 'Deluxe' monja topped with pork, beef, squid, shrimp, and a raw egg — alongside 40-plus menu items that include simmered beef tendon (gyū-suji nikomi) and various butter-grilled dishes.

anecdotes.text: The menu reportedly includes items created with input from celebrity regulars, including dishes nicknamed 'Gackt' and 'Chō-tokkyū (Super Express) monja.'

The restaurant sits on Hoppy-dōri, Asakusa's nostalgic strip of open-air izakaya stalls just behind Sensō-ji's Denbō-in temple grounds, an area long associated with Asakusa's downtown drinking-and-eating culture.

Monjayaki itself began as a favorite childhood snack in downtown Tokyo, especially around Asakusa, where children cooked it on small iron plates at candy stores in the 1950s–60s — the tradition Tsukushi continues today.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is reportedly available and staff will help first-timers cook monja tableside, but this is a lived-in shitamachi joint mostly full of Japanese regulars rather than a tourist-oriented setup.

places.name: もんじゃ 浅吉 (Monja Asakichi)
places.gf_detail: Monjayaki batter is inherently wheat-flour-based, and the menu is built around traditional flour-and-dashi cooking on a shared teppan griddle. No gluten-free batter substitute, dedicated cooking surface, or wheat-free soy/Worcestershire sauce was found advertised by the restaurant or any listing. Cross-contact with wheat on the shared teppan is essentially unavoidable — not recommended for celiac or severe gluten-sensitive diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on pork, squid, other seafood, cheese, and mentaiko (cod roe) toppings, and traditional monjayaki/okonomiyaki dashi is typically bonito (katsuo)-based. No vegan-specific items, dashi substitution, or vegan menu were found advertised by the restaurant or any listing — not a safe default for vegan diners without direct inquiry.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Hisago-dori shopping street)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop is run by a proprietress surnamed Tanabe who grew up helping out at monjayaki shops in Tokyo's shitamachi districts. She spent 16 years working in izakaya before running a monjayaki stall inside the Hanayashiki amusement park for nine years, building roughly 30 years of  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Proprietress (okami) — recipe development and hands-on monjayaki preparation
chef_bio.origin: Traditional Tokyo shitamachi (old-downtown) monjayaki, learned hands-on rather than through formal training.
chef_bio.background: The shop is run by a proprietress surnamed Tanabe who grew up helping out at monjayaki shops in Tokyo's shitamachi districts. She spent 16 years working in izakaya before running a monjayaki stall inside the Hanayashiki amusement park for nine years, building roughly 30 years of monjayaki experience before opening this Hisago-dori shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keeps the traditional shitamachi method of making monjayaki while adapting recipes to guests' requests, serving it in a style suited to today's tastes.
chef_bio.specialty: Monjayaki made to the proprietress's own recipe with a wide range of toppings — from the signature seafood-and-pork 'Asakichi-ten' to tomato-cheese and mentaiko-mochi-cheese varieties — alongside okonomiyaki and teppanyaki dishes; staff will cook it for you or let you grill it yourself.

anecdotes.text: The okami's monjayaki background spans about 30 years: 16 years working in izakaya, followed by 9 years running a monjayaki stall inside the Hanayashiki amusement park, before she opened this shop on Hisago-dori.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional shitamachi monjayaki shop with no confirmed English menu, though it sits on Hisago-dori, a tourist-frequented shopping street near Sensoji, so simple ordering with pointing/photos is manageable even without Japanese.

places.name: 田中そば店 浅草店 (Tanaka Sobaten Asakusa)
places.gf_detail: This is a standard chūka-soba/ramen shop: the noodles are ordinary wheat-based chūka-men, not gluten-free, and the broth is niboshi (dried sardine) and shoyu based — soy sauce in Japan typically contains wheat. No source mentions a gluten-free noodle substitution, a dedicated gluten-free prep area, or any allergen accommodation. Celiac and gluten-avoidant diners should treat this shop as unsuitable.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is built on niboshi (dried sardine) dashi with shoyu, and pork chāshū is a core topping across the menu. No source mentions any vegetarian or vegan broth/noodle option — fish- and meat-derived stock is central to essentially every dish, so vegan and vegetarian diners should treat this shop as unsuitable.
places.hours_raw: Most sources (Navitime, Yahoo Map, Gnavi-derived listings) state 11:00-21:00 every day, no closed days. One aggregator reported a different, possibly older schedule: weekdays 11:00-15:45 and 17:00-21:00 (split), weekends/holidays 11:00-21:00 straight through. Confirm current hours before visiting.
